0: A life-cycle assessment of greenhouse gas emissions associated with on-farm biogas production by Lia de Sousa Maldaner
A Thesis presented to The University of Guelph
In partial fulfilment of requirements for the degree of Doctor of Philosophy in Land Resource Science
ABSTRACT
A LIFE-CYCLE ASSESSMENT OF GREENHOUSE GAS EMISSIONS ASSOCIATED
<title>WITH ON-FARM BIOGAS PRODUCTION </title>
Lia de Sousa Maldaner University of Guelph, 2017
Advisor: Claudia Wagner-Riddle

Anaerobic digestion (AD) of liquid dairy manure can generate renewable energy and mit- igate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. A life cycle assessment (LCA) approach needs to be used to fully assess the AD benefits. However, most LCAs conducted to date have used data from pilot studies. Here, an LCA was conducted using year-round on-farm measure- ments of 1) emissions from untreated manure, 2) AD first year, and 3) AD fully operational. The data were collected at the same on-farm biogas facility throughout 5 years, starting be- fore the AD became fully operational with addition of industrial food waste (IFW). Plus, soil emissions after land application of untreated manure and digestate were included. The total annual methane emissions from the digestate storage were 1.0 kg CH4 m-3 y-1, in the first year, and 5.6 kg m-3 y-1 in the operational year, while untreated manure emitted 6.6 kgm-3-superscript(1). The main factor driving digestate CH4 emissions was temperature at 2 m depth 0.98, p 0.001). Volatile solids (VS) concentration in storage was similar in the first and operational years, but the emissions per VS were much higher in the latter, sug- gesting that VS is not a good predictor for emissions. In fact, digestate emissions were not correlated to VS concentration (r = 0.37; p = 0.29). The LCA assessed the potential GHG

environmental impact of the AD system as a substitute for conventional practices using the robust dataset derived from long-term field studies in Canada. The on-site emissions were higher (> 2x) for AD, but consideration of the avoided off-site emissions resulted in overall GHG emissions ~20% lower than the conventional manure treatment. The main emission sources were: storage, fugitive emissions from the anaerobic digesters and the co-generator unit. The environmental benefit came from the avoidance of IFW disposal, grid electricity provision, solid manure stockpiling and land application. The AD could potentially reduce 2.0 t CO2eq cow- y-1 from the impact of the conventional manure management practice (9.1 t CO2eq cow-1 y-1). Therefore, the co-digestion of manure and IFW should be encour- aged and financially supported by the governmental agencies to mitigate GHG emissions and climate change effect.

0: <title>ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS </title>

First of all, I would like to thank my supervisor Dr. Claudia Wagner-Riddle for all the guidance, friendship and support I received since I arrived in Guelph. I would also like to thank my committee: Dr. Andrew VanderZaag, Dr. Robert Gordon, Dr. Christopher Duke and Dr. Bill Van Heyst. If my work was not better or not done sooner is solely due to my own limitations. Special thanks to Chris Duke for taking me in a trip to visit the biogas farms in Ontario. Thank you, Chris, for all the interesting conversations about work and life. Thanks to Korb Whale and his family Kelly, Rya and Jack for allowing this research on their farm and for always being SO nice to me.

I would like to thank the current and past members of the Agmet research group, espe- cially who helped me to get parts of this work done: Adriana Furon, Dean G Louttit, Gud- mundur Johannesson, Martin Ngwabie, Jordan Forsyth, Darcy Snyder, Jen Smith, Sean Jordan, Susantha Jayasundara, Kumudinie Kariyapperuma, Zach DeBruyn, Jon Warland and Peter Von Bertoldi. A big "Thank you" to my friends Elke Eichelmann, Jeff Wood, Shannon Brown, Rezvan Taki, Therese Festin, Nicola Day, Yuki Audette and to the "fam- ily room Alex 111": Robyn Coleman, Amanda Malenica, Alassane Sissoko and Barbara Junqueira. Thanks to the SES administration staff, especially Linda Bissell, Jo Scarrow and Marie Vickery.

Finally, I would like to thank my family in Brazil for the unwavering support and love. Thanks to my husband Carlos and my two kids: Davi and Bento, who taught me that life is much more than I could ever imagined. Thanks to Victoria Ponce, for the loving care with my boys, SO I could focus on getting this work done. Also, thanks to all my friends who do not give up on our friendship even with my long silences.

Funding for this research was provided by Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada, the Dairy Farmers of Canada and OMAFRA. Support was also provided by the Science Without Borders program funded by CNPq - Ministry for Science and Tehcnology of Brazil in the form of a full doctorate scholarship.

0: <title>Chapter 1 </title>
1: <header>Introduction </header>
2: <header>1.1 Problem statement </header>

In cold climates, liquid manure is usually stored in open tanks for most of the year until acceptable weather conditions permit its land application as fertilizer. The minimum acceptable manure storage capacity varies depending on the prevailing climatic conditions of a region as well as legislative requirements: in the USA, it varies from 45 d (South) to 135 d (Pacific), 180 d (Central) and 225 d (Mid-Atlantic and Mid-west) (USEPA, 2001). In Canada, Ontario requires 240 d of minimum storage capacity (OMAFRA, 2010). The stored manure is rich in organic matter (OM), which is decomposed by microorganisms in anaerobic conditions generating biogas, a mixture of mainly methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2), both greenhouse gases (GHG). The CO2 released from manure decompo- sition is considered a reposition of CO2 from the atmosphere by animal feed production.
Atmospheric CH4 release, however, is an environmental concern due to its high global warming potential (GWP), 28 times higher than CO2 (Myhre et al., 2013).

Among the alternatives to reduce methane emissions from conventional manure man- agement practice, biodigestion (or anaerobic digestion) of manure is a very promising one. In the biodigestion treatment, manure is input and held in a closed reactor for a period of time (hydraulic retention time, HRT), where it is usually heated and continuously mixed to enhance biogas production. The biogas is collected and used as a fuel to generate energy (electrical, heat, mechanical), replacing fossil fuel use. The treated manure, called diges- tate, is then stored in a tank until it can be land applied, a similar management used for untreated manure under conventional practices.

It is expected that the CH4 emissions from the digestate storage would be lower than the untreated manure storage, since much of the OM decomposition would have occurred within the digester and CH4 captured to generate renewable energy. However, long term large-scale experiments quantifying emissions from storage of digestate are limited (Baldé et al., 2016b). Also, there are no large-scale field studies quantifying the environmental benefit before and after biodigestion treatment.

Biodigester systems require a considerable initial financial investment as well as con- siderable on-going oversight/management and thus need to be profitable to appeal to farm- ers. Manure alone does not produce sufficient gas to make the systems profitable SO co- substrates are typically added to enhance organic loading rate and, consequently, biogas and energy production (Pehme and Veromann, 2015). By adding industrial food wastes

(IFW) as co-substrates, farmers can also access "tipping" fees. This addition, however, adds complexity to the system and may result in increased CH4 emissions from the diges- tate storage, potentially offsetting the environmental benefit of the biodigestion technology. Long-term CH4 emissions from digestate at an on-farm facility that receives off-farm ma- terials have not been evaluated.

Environmental impact analysis is complex and, to account for possible trade-offs of environmental benefits, it is important to look at the broadest scenario, including all en- vironmental interaction within the system's life cycle. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a standardized methodology that addresses all the potential environmental impacts of a sys- tem/product throughout its life cycle, considering all the inputs (environmental resources) and outputs (environmental releases and consequences) of all stages of a system/product, from its generation to its final disposal. As a complex system, the co-digestion of untreated manure along with off-farm materials requires a broad analysis of the potential environ- mental impacts. The most suitable approach involves using a LCA to compare the potential environmental impacts of the biodigester systems to the conventional manure management systems.

The LCA of a system strongly depends on regional characteristics (climate, local man- agement practices, potential systems replaced, etc), because these factors affect the gaseous emissions in the form of CH4 and N2O (greenhouse gases) and NH3, a gas causing toxicity to sensitive ecosystems, eutrophication in surface waters, environmental acidification, for- mation of fine particulate matters in the atmosphere (Neerackal et al., 2015) and indirect

N2O emissions (Rodhe et al., 2015). A LCA quantifying the potential environmental im- pact of a biodigester system comparing to the conventional manure management practice has not been conducted in Canada using long-term field scale emission data. Due to the importance of CH4, N2O and NH3 emissions from digestate in determining the environ- mental impact, ideally the analysis should rely on data from field measurements. There is definitely a need for LCA that is driven by data at a real operation rather than based only on databases or emission estimates.

In this study, a micrometeorological mass balance method (Wagner-Riddle et al., 2006) was used to measure CH4 emissions from a circular storage tank on a dairy farm with a biogas system in southern Ontario. The measurements took place from 2013 through 2015, capturing the initial start-up of the operational phase of the biogas system (installed in 2012). The AD recipe varied from a conservative one (mainly untreated manure) to a more profitable one (with IFW co-substrates). Environmental (air and digestate temperature) and chemico-physical (composition) data were also collected during the study period to deter- mine the relationship between emission and possible emission drivers. A similar untreated manure dataset from a complementary project at the same farm in 2010 and 2011, before the biodigester system become operational, was used for comparison. In addition, data from experiments measuring gaseous emissions after applying manure and digestate from the same farm in a separate study were used in the LCA (Cambareri et al., 2017; Schwager et al., 2016).
The uniqueness of the analysis presented here is the reliance on field measured data

over multiple years conducted on a commercial dairy farm before and after installation of a biodigester system. This novelty allowed that important field variables related to manure characteristics (animal breed, animal feed) and CH4 emissions (climate) remained simi- lar for both scenarios. The novelty of this study consists in presenting a LCA based on extensive long-term field data, which has not been conducted for liquid dairy manure AD.

In this study, a micrometeorological mass balance method (Wagner-Riddle et al., 2006) was used to measure CH4 emissions from a circular storage tank on a dairy farm with a biogas system in southern Ontario. The measurements took place from 2013 through 2015, capturing the initial start-up of the operational phase of the biogas system (installed in 2012). The AD recipe varied from a conservative one (mainly untreated manure) to a more profitable one (with IFW co-substrates). Environmental (air and digestate temperature) and chemico-physical (composition) data were also collected during the study period to deter- mine the relationship between emission and possible emission drivers. A similar untreated manure dataset from a complementary project at the same farm in 2010 and 2011, before the biodigester system become operational, was used for comparison. In addition, data from experiments measuring gaseous emissions after applying manure and digestate from the same farm in a separate study were used in the LCA (Cambareri et al., 2017; Schwager et al., 2016).
The uniqueness of the analysis presented here is the reliance on field measured data

over multiple years conducted on a commercial dairy farm before and after installation of a biodigester system. This novelty allowed that important field variables related to manure characteristics (animal breed, animal feed) and CH4 emissions (climate) remained simi- lar for both scenarios. The novelty of this study consists in presenting a LCA based on extensive long-term field data, which has not been conducted for liquid dairy manure AD.
5: <header>1.1 Problem statement </header>

In cold climates, liquid manure is usually stored in open tanks for most of the year until acceptable weather conditions permit its land application as fertilizer. The minimum acceptable manure storage capacity varies depending on the prevailing climatic conditions of a region as well as legislative requirements: in the USA, it varies from 45 d (South) to 135 d (Pacific), 180 d (Central) and 225 d (Mid-Atlantic and Mid-west) (USEPA, 2001). The stored manure is rich in organic matter (OM), which is decomposed by microorganisms in anaerobic conditions generating biogas, a mixture of mainly methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2), both greenhouse gases (GHG). The CO2 released from manure decompo- sition is considered a reposition of CO2 from the atmosphere by animal feed production.
Atmospheric CH4 release, however, is an environmental concern due to its high global warming potential (GWP), 28 times higher than CO2 (Myhre et al., 2013).

Among the alternatives to reduce methane emissions from conventional manure man- agement practice, biodigestion (or anaerobic digestion) of manure is a very promising one. In the biodigestion treatment, manure is input and held in a closed reactor for a period of time (hydraulic retention time, HRT), where it is usually heated and continuously mixed to enhance biogas production. The biogas is collected and used as a fuel to generate energy (electrical, heat, mechanical), replacing fossil fuel use. It is expected that the CH4 emissions from the digestate storage would be lower than the untreated manure storage, since much of the OM decomposition would have occurred within the digester and CH4 captured to generate renewable energy. However, long term large-scale experiments quantifying emissions from storage of digestate are limited (Baldé et al., 2016b). Also, there are no large-scale field studies quantifying the environmental benefit before and after biodigestion treatment.

Biodigester systems require a considerable initial financial investment as well as con- siderable on-going oversight/management and thus need to be profitable to appeal to farm- ers. Manure alone does not produce sufficient gas to make the systems profitable SO co- substrates are typically added to enhance organic loading rate and, consequently, biogas and energy production (Pehme and Veromann, 2015). By adding industrial food wastes

(IFW) as co-substrates, farmers can also access "tipping" fees. This addition, however, adds complexity to the system and may result in increased CH4 emissions from the diges- tate storage, potentially offsetting the environmental benefit of the biodigestion technology. Long-term CH4 emissions from digestate at an on-farm facility that receives off-farm ma- terials have not been evaluated.

Environmental impact analysis is complex and, to account for possible trade-offs of environmental benefits, it is important to look at the broadest scenario, including all en- vironmental interaction within the system's life cycle. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a standardized methodology that addresses all the potential environmental impacts of a sys- tem/product throughout its life cycle, considering all the inputs (environmental resources) and outputs (environmental releases and consequences) of all stages of a system/product, from its generation to its final disposal. As a complex system, the co-digestion of untreated manure along with off-farm materials requires a broad analysis of the potential environ- mental impacts. The most suitable approach involves using a LCA to compare the potential environmental impacts of the biodigester systems to the conventional manure management systems.

N2O emissions (Rodhe et al., 2015). Due to the importance of CH4, N2O and NH3 emissions from digestate in determining the environ- mental impact, ideally the analysis should rely on data from field measurements. There is definitely a need for LCA that is driven by data at a real operation rather than based only on databases or emission estimates.

The novelty of this study consists in presenting a LCA based on extensive long-term field data, which has not been conducted for liquid dairy manure AD.

9: <header>1.2 Objectives </header>
The objectives of this research were to:

<list>1. Evaluate year-long CH4 emissions from digestate storage at an on-farm biogas facil- ity and establish possible relationships between digestate CH4 emissions and emis- sion drivers (temperature and volatile solids); 
2. Compare storage emissions from digestate to year-long CH4 emissions from un- treated dairy manure storage at the same site before a biogas system became op- erational (Chapter 2); 
3. Evaluate long-term field CH4 emissions from digestate throughout a biodigester sys- tem operational year, accounting for AD recipe (input) adjustments from the start-up phase, and determining the impact of the addition of off-farm materials (Chapter 3); 
4. Compare the potential environmental impact of an operational biodigestion system and the conventional manure management practice using a LCA approach based on field gaseous emission data from Ontario, Canada (Chapter 4).</list>
10: <header>1.3 Thesis organization </header>

This thesis is organized in five chapters, starting with the Introduction which gives the context of the research and the gaps it intends to fill, the study objectives, and a guide for the thesis. In Chapter 2, a comparison is presented of CH4 emissions during the first year of operation at a commercial biogas farm with storage emissions of untreated manure at the same farm before the biodigester system became operational addressing objectives 1 and 2. Chapter 3 evaluates the impact of off-farm material additions to increase energy production from dairy manure on CH4 emissions from the digestate storage based on three years of on-farm measurements addressing objective 3. Chapter 4 is considered the core of this thesis, as it integrates field measurements in a GHG life cycle assessment of the two manure management options: conventional manure storage versus biodigestion of manure addressing objective 4. While Chapters 2 and 3 present the environmental impact of the liquid storage only, Chapter 4 accounts for any eventual environmental impact trade-offs in the whole and complex chain of manure treatment. Chapters 2, 3 and 4 are written in manuscript format for submission to refereed journals. Finally, Chapter 5 summarizes the main findings and contributions of this research and presents some recommendations for future research.

0: <title>Methane emissions from storage of digestate at a dairy manure biogas facility in cold climates </title>

This Chapter was submitted to the Journal Agricultural and Forest Meteorology for the Special Issue: Greenhouse gas and ammonia emissions from livestock production systems on June 24, 2017. The submission is entitled:
"Methane emissions from storage of digestate at a dairy manure biogas facility" by Lia Maldaner; Claudia Wagner-Riddle; Andrew C. VanderZaag; Robert Gordon; Christopher Duke (AGRFORMET-D-17-00542).
1: <header>2.1 Abstract </header>

Conventional manure storages are an important source of methane (CH4), a potent greenhouse gas. Anaerobic digestion is an alternative manure management practice po- tentially able to provide environmental benefits, including the reduction of CH4 emissions from manure storage. This study was conducted at a commercial farm in Ontario where a mesophilic biodigester system fed with dairy manure and 35% of off-farm materials be- came operational in May 2012. The purpose was to quantify year-round CH4 emissions from a digestate storage tank, examine the relationship between emissions and its driv- ing factors, and compare these results to a similar emissions dataset from untreated manure measured one year before the biodigester became operational. A micrometeorological mass balance approach was used to measure CH4 fluxes. Total annual CH4 emissions from di- gestate storage were 1.0 kg CH4 m-3y-1, which was 85% lower compared to untreated manure. Monthly average volatile solids (VS) mass in the storage tank was 73 +24 Mg for digestate and 107 +30 Mg for manure, representing a 32% VS reduction in the tank, sug- gesting that lower emissions were not due to VS mass reduction after biodigestion alone. The annual CH4 emissions scaled by VS were 26 g CH4 kg-1 VS y-1 for digestate and 76 g CH4 kg-1 VS y-1 for manure, and the lower scaled emissions for digestate indicated that its VS likely was less suitable for methane production (less digestible). This was also verified when investigating the relationship between fluxes and its driving factors: VS con- centration was not significantly correlated to digestate CH4 emissions per volume (r = 0.37; p = 0.29), but it was significantly correlated to untreated manure emissions (r = 0.95; p =

0.002). On the other hand, the correlation of temperature with emission was stronger for digestate than manure. Temperature at all depths were significantly correlated to CH4 emis- sions from digestate and presented no lag, especially at 2 m depth (r = 0.98, p < 0.001). At the same air temperature, digestate was warmer than manure, owing to the digestate leaving the digester at 38 °C. This study showed that a 60-day hydraulic retention time biodigester system fed with dairy manure and 35% (vol) of off-farm materials was able to significantly reduce facility-scale CH4 emissions from the liquid storage tank.
Keywords: integrated horizontal flux, anaerobic digestion, emission drivers, manure man- agement, emission mitigation strategy.

0.002). On the other hand, the correlation of temperature with emission was stronger for digestate than manure. Temperature at all depths were significantly correlated to CH4 emis- sions from digestate and presented no lag, especially at 2 m depth (r = 0.98, p < 0.001). At the same air temperature, digestate was warmer than manure, owing to the digestate leaving the digester at 38 °C. This study showed that a 60-day hydraulic retention time biodigester system fed with dairy manure and 35% (vol) of off-farm materials was able to significantly reduce facility-scale CH4 emissions from the liquid storage tank.
Keywords: integrated horizontal flux, anaerobic digestion, emission drivers, manure man- agement, emission mitigation strategy.
3: <header>2.2 Introduction </header>

A large portion of dairy facilities use liquid manure systems (Jayasundara et al., 2016). Manure management in cold climates require storage capacity for at least 6 months in the United States (USEPA, 2001), and at least 240 d is required in Ontario (OMAFRA, 2010). In storage, anaerobic digestion (AD) of degradable organic matter in the liquid manure occurs naturally, generating biogas, a mixture of methane (50-75% vol), carbon dioxide (25-50%) and trace amounts of other gases (Gomez, 2013). Methane (CH4) when released to the atmosphere, however, is a serious environmental problem due to its global warming potential which is 28 times greater than carbon dioxide (CO2) (Myhre et al., 2013). CH4

emissions typically comprise > 95% of greenhouse gases (GHG) emissions from liquid ma- nure storages (Amon et al., 2006). In Canada, manure management accounted for 14% of agriculture's GHG emissions in 2014 (Environment and Climate Change Canada, 2016). A comprehensive review of field-based studies measuring GHG emissions from dairy manure (Owen and Silver, 2015) highlighted the great opportunity for GHG emission reduction from conventional liquid manure systems, while stressing the need for both field scale and long-term studies to provide more accurate emission reduction opportunities.

Use of liquid manure to generate biogas has long been suggested as a potential GHG mitigation practice (Amon et al., 2006; Wang, 2014). In a farm biodigester system, ma- nure is placed in a covered tank (anaerobic digester, also called a biodigester) to stabilize and optimize biogas production. The efficiency of biogas production depends on the ab- sence of oxygen (anaerobic conditions), uniform temperatures, optimum nutrient supply and pH (Gomez, 2013). Farm AD systems regularly adopt a single-step process in the mesophilic temperature range (32-42 °C) with wet fermentation (dry matter < 20%) and quasi-continuous feeding (Gomez, 2013). The degradable organic matter content is often increased with the addition of co-substrates such as industrial food waste (IFW) along with manure. Combined heat and power systems use the biogas to generate electricity and heat, potentially replacing energy from fossil fuels. The treated effluent (digestate) is pumped (frequently after solid/liquid separation) to an open outdoor storage tank, similar to un- treated manure storage in conventional practice, to be used later as an organic fertilizer. This system is an alternative option to manure management that can provide multiple envi-
ronmental benefits (Artrip et al., 2013).

It has been suggested that CH4 emissions from digestate storage are substantially lower compared to untreated manure, because part of the degradable matter (volatile solids) is consumed inside the biodigester, followed by a further decrease after solid separation (Amon et al., 2006). However, there are other factors, that might stimulate enhanced CH4 production after digestion. Substrate temperature plays a major role in controlling CH4 emissions in manure storages (Clemens et al., 2006; Sommer et al., 2007; Daniel-Gromke et al., 2015) and the digestate temperature can be substantially higher than the air temper- ature (Liebetrau et al., 2013). Liebetrau et al. (2013) determined the digestate storage tank was the major potential source of CH4 emissions, followed by CH4 slippage from gas uti- lization systems in 10 biogas plants in Germany. Daniel-Gromke et al. (2015) found that the residual gas potential of digestate was > 10% in 10 of 12 biogas plants investigated (range of 4 to 23%). Baldé et al. (2016b) found significant CH4 emissions from a biogas plant where annual cumulative emissions from digestate represented 12% of the CH4 produced within the digester. Quantifying emissions from digestate in storage is also important to determine opportunities to increase energy production and biogas plant efficiency by, for example, recovering CH4 from the digestate.

Few studies have quantified the avoidance of CH4 emissions associated with the storage of digestate compared to untreated manure (Amon et al., 2006; Clemens et al., 2006; Rodhe et al., 2015). Most studies have shown that AD has the potential to reduce GHG emissions (Amon et al., 2006; Owen and Silver, 2015). However, pilot-scale studies have shown

contradictory results. Amon et al. (2006) concluded that AD was effective in reducing CH4 emissions from the storage phase, as digestate had lower emissions than untreated stored manure over 80 d. In contrast, Rodhe et al. (2015) found that daily mean CH4 emissions over 3 summer months were significantly higher for digestate (~ 3 times higher on a per unit volume basis) than untreated manure. These studies highlight the need for further investigation to determine the CH4 emission reduction over a range of conditions.

Hrad et al. (2015) emphasized the importance of long-term field studies to capture me- teorological changes as well as operational activities in biogas plants. Liebetrau et al. (2013) found that CH4 losses from digestate were ~ 50% higher in the summer than win- ter for biodigesters fed with a mixture of manure and crops for two 1-week measurement periods on each of 10 plants using a floating chamber. Rodhe et al. (2015) found negli- gible emissions from digestate in the winter. These observations highlight the need for year-round measurements to determine the annual CH4 emissions of digestate. In the only year-round study at a commercial farm biodigester, Baldé et al. (2016b) used open-path sensors and a backward Lagrangian Stochastic (bLS) model to estimate CH4 emissions from digestate. Micrometeorological methods, such as bLS and micrometeorological mass balance (MMB), are well-suited for year-round monitoring (Harper et al., 2011). The MMB method is particularly adapted for year-round flux measurements of point sources such as manure/digestate tanks, since it is suitable for a broad wind direction range and there are no assumptions regarding the nature of turbulent flow or Monin-Obhukov similarity (Wagner- Riddle et al., 2006; VanderZaag et al., 2011). There is a need for long-term facility-scale
studies comparing the storage of untreated manure versus digestate to better understand the driving forces and quantify the real-world emission reduction potential of AD.

In this study, CH4 emissions from a digestate open storage tank on a commercial dairy farm were measured using the MMB method for one year and then compared to one year of manure emission data obtained using a similar experimental set up at the same site before the biodigester became operational (Kariyapperuma et al., 2017). Environmental conditions and digestate characteristics were also determined to allow the establishment of emission drivers and their relationships with emissions. The uniqueness of this study con- sisted in comparing digestate CH4 emissions with those from untreated manure at the same farm before the biodigester became operational, SO that important field variables related to manure production (animal breed, animal feed) remained similar for both scenarios.

The objectives of this study were to (i) quantify year-round field CH4 emissions from the storage of AD digestate; (ii) examine the relationship between emissions and emissions drivers (volatile solids and temperature); and (iii) compare CH4 emissions between the storage of untreated manure and digestate at the same farm before and after the biodigester installation.

Few studies have quantified the avoidance of CH4 emissions associated with the storage of digestate compared to untreated manure (Amon et al., 2006; Clemens et al., 2006; Rodhe et al., 2015). Most studies have shown that AD has the potential to reduce GHG emissions (Amon et al., 2006; Owen and Silver, 2015). However, pilot-scale studies have shown

contradictory results. Amon et al. (2006) concluded that AD was effective in reducing CH4 emissions from the storage phase, as digestate had lower emissions than untreated stored manure over 80 d. In contrast, Rodhe et al. (2015) found that daily mean CH4 emissions over 3 summer months were significantly higher for digestate (~ 3 times higher on a per unit volume basis) than untreated manure. These studies highlight the need for further investigation to determine the CH4 emission reduction over a range of conditions.

Hrad et al. (2015) emphasized the importance of long-term field studies to capture me- teorological changes as well as operational activities in biogas plants. Liebetrau et al. (2013) found that CH4 losses from digestate were ~ 50% higher in the summer than win- ter for biodigesters fed with a mixture of manure and crops for two 1-week measurement periods on each of 10 plants using a floating chamber. Rodhe et al. (2015) found negli- gible emissions from digestate in the winter. These observations highlight the need for year-round measurements to determine the annual CH4 emissions of digestate. In the only year-round study at a commercial farm biodigester, Baldé et al. (2016b) used open-path sensors and a backward Lagrangian Stochastic (bLS) model to estimate CH4 emissions from digestate. Micrometeorological methods, such as bLS and micrometeorological mass balance (MMB), are well-suited for year-round monitoring (Harper et al., 2011). The MMB method is particularly adapted for year-round flux measurements of point sources such as manure/digestate tanks, since it is suitable for a broad wind direction range and there are no assumptions regarding the nature of turbulent flow or Monin-Obhukov similarity (Wagner- Riddle et al., 2006; VanderZaag et al., 2011). There is a need for long-term facility-scale
studies comparing the storage of untreated manure versus digestate to better understand the driving forces and quantify the real-world emission reduction potential of AD.

In this study, CH4 emissions from a digestate open storage tank on a commercial dairy farm were measured using the MMB method for one year and then compared to one year of manure emission data obtained using a similar experimental set up at the same site before the biodigester became operational (Kariyapperuma et al., 2017). Environmental conditions and digestate characteristics were also determined to allow the establishment of emission drivers and their relationships with emissions. The uniqueness of this study con- sisted in comparing digestate CH4 emissions with those from untreated manure at the same farm before the biodigester became operational, SO that important field variables related to manure production (animal breed, animal feed) remained similar for both scenarios.

The objectives of this study were to (i) quantify year-round field CH4 emissions from the storage of AD digestate; (ii) examine the relationship between emissions and emissions drivers (volatile solids and temperature); and (iii) compare CH4 emissions between the storage of untreated manure and digestate at the same farm before and after the biodigester installation.
8: <header>2.2 Introduction </header>

A large portion of dairy facilities use liquid manure systems (Jayasundara et al., 2016). Manure management in cold climates require storage capacity for at least 6 months in the United States (USEPA, 2001), and at least 240 d is required in Ontario (OMAFRA, 2010). In storage, anaerobic digestion (AD) of degradable organic matter in the liquid manure occurs naturally, generating biogas, a mixture of methane (50-75% vol), carbon dioxide (25-50%) and trace amounts of other gases (Gomez, 2013). Methane (CH4) when released to the atmosphere, however, is a serious environmental problem due to its global warming potential which is 28 times greater than carbon dioxide (CO2) (Myhre et al., 2013). CH4

emissions typically comprise > 95% of greenhouse gases (GHG) emissions from liquid ma- nure storages (Amon et al., 2006). In Canada, manure management accounted for 14% of agriculture's GHG emissions in 2014 (Environment and Climate Change Canada, 2016). A comprehensive review of field-based studies measuring GHG emissions from dairy manure (Owen and Silver, 2015) highlighted the great opportunity for GHG emission reduction from conventional liquid manure systems, while stressing the need for both field scale and long-term studies to provide more accurate emission reduction opportunities.

Use of liquid manure to generate biogas has long been suggested as a potential GHG mitigation practice (Amon et al., 2006; Wang, 2014). In a farm biodigester system, ma- nure is placed in a covered tank (anaerobic digester, also called a biodigester) to stabilize and optimize biogas production. The efficiency of biogas production depends on the ab- sence of oxygen (anaerobic conditions), uniform temperatures, optimum nutrient supply and pH (Gomez, 2013). Farm AD systems regularly adopt a single-step process in the mesophilic temperature range (32-42 °C) with wet fermentation (dry matter < 20%) and quasi-continuous feeding (Gomez, 2013). The degradable organic matter content is often increased with the addition of co-substrates such as industrial food waste (IFW) along with manure. Combined heat and power systems use the biogas to generate electricity and heat, potentially replacing energy from fossil fuels. The treated effluent (digestate) is pumped (frequently after solid/liquid separation) to an open outdoor storage tank, similar to un- treated manure storage in conventional practice, to be used later as an organic fertilizer. This system is an alternative option to manure management that can provide multiple envi-
ronmental benefits (Artrip et al., 2013).

It has been suggested that CH4 emissions from digestate storage are substantially lower compared to untreated manure, because part of the degradable matter (volatile solids) is consumed inside the biodigester, followed by a further decrease after solid separation (Amon et al., 2006). However, there are other factors, that might stimulate enhanced CH4 production after digestion. Substrate temperature plays a major role in controlling CH4 emissions in manure storages (Clemens et al., 2006; Sommer et al., 2007; Daniel-Gromke et al., 2015) and the digestate temperature can be substantially higher than the air temper- ature (Liebetrau et al., 2013). Liebetrau et al. (2013) determined the digestate storage tank was the major potential source of CH4 emissions, followed by CH4 slippage from gas uti- lization systems in 10 biogas plants in Germany. Daniel-Gromke et al. (2015) found that the residual gas potential of digestate was > 10% in 10 of 12 biogas plants investigated (range of 4 to 23%). Baldé et al. (2016b) found significant CH4 emissions from a biogas plant where annual cumulative emissions from digestate represented 12% of the CH4 produced within the digester. Quantifying emissions from digestate in storage is also important to determine opportunities to increase energy production and biogas plant efficiency by, for example, recovering CH4 from the digestate.

Few studies have quantified the avoidance of CH4 emissions associated with the storage of digestate compared to untreated manure (Amon et al., 2006; Clemens et al., 2006; Rodhe et al., 2015). Most studies have shown that AD has the potential to reduce GHG emissions (Amon et al., 2006; Owen and Silver, 2015). However, pilot-scale studies have shown

contradictory results. Amon et al. (2006) concluded that AD was effective in reducing CH4 emissions from the storage phase, as digestate had lower emissions than untreated stored manure over 80 d. In contrast, Rodhe et al. (2015) found that daily mean CH4 emissions over 3 summer months were significantly higher for digestate (~ 3 times higher on a per unit volume basis) than untreated manure. These studies highlight the need for further investigation to determine the CH4 emission reduction over a range of conditions.

Hrad et al. (2015) emphasized the importance of long-term field studies to capture me- teorological changes as well as operational activities in biogas plants. Liebetrau et al. (2013) found that CH4 losses from digestate were ~ 50% higher in the summer than win- ter for biodigesters fed with a mixture of manure and crops for two 1-week measurement periods on each of 10 plants using a floating chamber. Rodhe et al. (2015) found negli- gible emissions from digestate in the winter. These observations highlight the need for year-round measurements to determine the annual CH4 emissions of digestate. In the only year-round study at a commercial farm biodigester, Baldé et al. (2016b) used open-path sensors and a backward Lagrangian Stochastic (bLS) model to estimate CH4 emissions from digestate. Micrometeorological methods, such as bLS and micrometeorological mass balance (MMB), are well-suited for year-round monitoring (Harper et al., 2011). The MMB method is particularly adapted for year-round flux measurements of point sources such as manure/digestate tanks, since it is suitable for a broad wind direction range and there are no assumptions regarding the nature of turbulent flow or Monin-Obhukov similarity (Wagner- Riddle et al., 2006; VanderZaag et al., 2011). There is a need for long-term facility-scale
studies comparing the storage of untreated manure versus digestate to better understand the driving forces and quantify the real-world emission reduction potential of AD.

In this study, CH4 emissions from a digestate open storage tank on a commercial dairy farm were measured using the MMB method for one year and then compared to one year of manure emission data obtained using a similar experimental set up at the same site before the biodigester became operational (Kariyapperuma et al., 2017). Environmental conditions and digestate characteristics were also determined to allow the establishment of emission drivers and their relationships with emissions. The uniqueness of this study con- sisted in comparing digestate CH4 emissions with those from untreated manure at the same farm before the biodigester became operational, SO that important field variables related to manure production (animal breed, animal feed) remained similar for both scenarios.

The objectives of this study were to (i) quantify year-round field CH4 emissions from the storage of AD digestate; (ii) examine the relationship between emissions and emissions drivers (volatile solids and temperature); and (iii) compare CH4 emissions between the storage of untreated manure and digestate at the same farm before and after the biodigester installation.

0: <title>2.3 Materials and methods </title>
1: <header>2.3.1 Site description </header>

The study was conducted at a commercial dairy farm near Alma, Ontario, Canada, (43°45'N 80°40'W, elevation 400 m) from Jan 1 to Dec 31, 2013. This area of Ontario has relatively high elevation and is relatively cool compared to other parts of Southern Ontario. The farm had Holstein cows divided into four main barns according to animal category (Figure 2.1). There were about 140 lactating cows, 25 transition and dry cows, 65 heifers in a side bank barn and 15 calves in a small calf nursery located at the farm entrance. Solids separated from the digestate were used as bedding material. Cow feeding consisted of a mixture of corn silage, alfalfa-grass silage, grass hay, straw, high moisture corn, and custom supplement (Ngwabie et al., 2014). Wash-water from milking robots was stored separately from manure and did not pass through the digester.

tank. Figure Methane sampling towers around tank are indicated with * Diagram not to 2.1: Site layout showing position of farm buildings and digestate/manure storage scale. CHP = combined heat and power unit.

Liquid manure collected from the two main barns (with lactating and dry cows) was temporarily stored in covered tanks under the barns with the capacity of about 3000 m³ to hold manure prior to digestion: four rectangular tanks under the dry cow barn (two 13 8 m, with 2.4 m depth; two 13 12 m, with 2.4 m depth), one tank (27 8 m, 3 m

depth) under the transition cow barn, one tank (42 X 8 m, 3 m depth) under the milking cow barn and a manure runoff tank (6.3 X 6.3 m, 2.6 m depth). From the under-barn storages, manure was pumped directly into the biodigester or into a mixing tank where it was combined with co-substrates before being pumped into the biodigester. There was an off-farm material storage tank (100 m³) to receive co-substrates to be used as feed for the biodigesters and a small tank (0.5 m³ working volume) where the biodigester input mixture was pasteurized (Figure 2.1). Manure from the calf nursery and heifer bank barn was not added to the anaerobic digesters or digestate storage.

The biodigester system was composed of continuous stirred-tank reactors that included two identical circular tanks (14.63 m diameter; 7.3 m depth, working volume 1102 m³) connected in series. The total hydraulic retention time (HRT) was 60 d and the operational temperature was kept around 38 °C (mesophilic digestion). The biodigesters had a rubber roof with a pressure control valve to safely retain the biogas produced. A pipeline directed the biogas to a 250 kW electrical cogeneration unit and a flare, used in case of excess biogas flow. There was a liquid/solid separator building where digestate leaving from the second biodigester passed through a screw-press and the separated solids were stored in a pile (the majority, but not all digestate passed through this screw-press because the unit was periodically bypassed). The liquid digestate was collected into a tank below the separator building and pumped into an open circular storage tank (30.5 m diameter; 3.65 m depth; 2665 m³) (Figure 2.1). Additional 7000 m³ capacity for digestate storage was supplied by an earthen storage (77 X 26 m, 3.65 m depth) at the back of an adjacent field where

manure or digestate was periodically transferred by pipeline as required (unsuitable for measurement due to surrounding trees and lack of electricity). This study focused on the open circular storage tank which provided an excellent platform for micrometeorological measurements of manure and digestate, and enabled emissions to be studied on per unit volume and per unit of volatile solids. Due to the complexity of the manure management system, however, care should be taken when extrapolating to whole-farm or whole-facility emissions.

Prior to this study, a complementary project at the same farm measured CH4 emissions from the untreated manure stored in the same tank as the current digestate before the biodi- gester system became operational, between Jul 2010 and Nov 2011, using the same setup (Kariyapperuma et al., 2017). To compare emissions from storage and physico-chemical properties from untreated manure and digestate on an annual basis, available data measured from Jul 15, 2010 to Nov 7, 2011 were combined to represent a typical year of emissions on a commercial farm. Data for day of year (DOY) 1 to 200 (Jan 1 to Jul 19) in 2011, and for DOY 201 to 365 (Jul 20 to Dec 31) in 2010 were assembled and referred as "manure year".

Cow feeding consisted of a mixture of corn silage, alfalfa-grass silage, grass hay, straw, high moisture corn, and custom supplement (Ngwabie et al., 2014). Wash-water from milking robots was stored separately from manure and did not pass through the digester.

Liquid manure collected from the two main barns (with lactating and dry cows) was temporarily stored in covered tanks under the barns with the capacity of about 3000 m³ to hold manure prior to digestion: four rectangular tanks under the dry cow barn (two 13 8 m, with 2.4 m depth; two 13 12 m, with 2.4 m depth), one tank (27 8 m, 3 m

depth) under the transition cow barn, one tank (42 X 8 m, 3 m depth) under the milking cow barn and a manure runoff tank (6.3 X 6.3 m, 2.6 m depth). From the under-barn storages, manure was pumped directly into the biodigester or into a mixing tank where it was combined with co-substrates before being pumped into the biodigester. There was an off-farm material storage tank (100 m³) to receive co-substrates to be used as feed for the biodigesters and a small tank (0.5 m³ working volume) where the biodigester input mixture was pasteurized (Figure 2.1). Manure from the calf nursery and heifer bank barn was not added to the anaerobic digesters or digestate storage.

The biodigester system was composed of continuous stirred-tank reactors that included two identical circular tanks (14.63 m diameter; 7.3 m depth, working volume 1102 m³) connected in series. The total hydraulic retention time (HRT) was 60 d and the operational temperature was kept around 38 °C (mesophilic digestion). The biodigesters had a rubber roof with a pressure control valve to safely retain the biogas produced. A pipeline directed the biogas to a 250 kW electrical cogeneration unit and a flare, used in case of excess biogas flow. There was a liquid/solid separator building where digestate leaving from the second biodigester passed through a screw-press and the separated solids were stored in a pile (the majority, but not all digestate passed through this screw-press because the unit was periodically bypassed). The liquid digestate was collected into a tank below the separator building and pumped into an open circular storage tank (30.5 m diameter; 3.65 m depth; 2665 m³) (Figure 2.1). Additional 7000 m³ capacity for digestate storage was supplied by an earthen storage (77 X 26 m, 3.65 m depth) at the back of an adjacent field where

manure or digestate was periodically transferred by pipeline as required (unsuitable for measurement due to surrounding trees and lack of electricity). This study focused on the open circular storage tank which provided an excellent platform for micrometeorological measurements of manure and digestate, and enabled emissions to be studied on per unit volume and per unit of volatile solids. Due to the complexity of the manure management system, however, care should be taken when extrapolating to whole-farm or whole-facility emissions.

Prior to this study, a complementary project at the same farm measured CH4 emissions from the untreated manure stored in the same tank as the current digestate before the biodi- gester system became operational, between Jul 2010 and Nov 2011, using the same setup (Kariyapperuma et al., 2017). To compare emissions from storage and physico-chemical properties from untreated manure and digestate on an annual basis, available data measured from Jul 15, 2010 to Nov 7, 2011 were combined to represent a typical year of emissions on a commercial farm. Data for day of year (DOY) 1 to 200 (Jan 1 to Jul 19) in 2011, and for DOY 201 to 365 (Jul 20 to Dec 31) in 2010 were assembled and referred as "manure year".

Cow feeding consisted of a mixture of corn silage, alfalfa-grass silage, grass hay, straw, high moisture corn, and custom supplement (Ngwabie et al., 2014). Wash-water from milking robots was stored separately from manure and did not pass through the digester.

7: <header>2.3.2 Biogas system operation </header>

The biogas system became operational in May 2012 with the addition of untreated ma- nure only into the first biodigester. The initial temperature was 15 °C and the tank was heated using a diesel boiler until the target temperature of 38 °C was reached at the end of

May. The biodigester total feeding rate of 34.5 m³d-¹ (12,600 m³ y-1) changed in compo- sition throughout 2013 (Table 2.1). Routine loadings of onions and bananas (26% of input by volume) started in Jan 2013, with the addition of cookie production sugar water (32% of input by volume) from Apr 2013 onwards, and food processing chicken by-products from Aug 2013 onwards (35% of input by volume). The off-farm materials, except onions and bananas, went through a continuous hydrolysis pre-treatment in the off-farm materials tank at 50 °C, mixing for 2.5 d on average, and pasteurization in a small tank where they were kept for 1 h at 70 °C before input into the first biodigester.
Table 2.1: The biogidester feeding recipe throughout 2013. Date indicates when the recipe was changed.

Date|Loading rate (m ³ d-¹|Loading rate (m ³ d-¹|Loading rate (m ³ d-¹|% of off-farm|Off-farm materials Date|Total|Manure|Off-farm materials|materials|composition Jan-2013|34.5|25.5|9|26|onions and bananas (100%) Apr-2013|34.5|23.5|11|32|onions and bananas (82%) sugar water (18%) Aug-2013|34.5|22.5|12|35|onions and bananas (13%) sugar water (16%) chicken meat waste (71%)

The 250 kW capacity cogeneration unit started to generate energy in Oct 2012 produc- ing an average of 1700 kW h d-¹ (~ 30 % of the generator capacity) in 2013. The typical biogas production rate in 2013 was 1,300 m³d-¹, which was only about one third of the target gas production rate due to the challenges associated with the start-up electrical grid connection and determining the optimal feeding recipes with the co-feedstocks available at
the time. Heat from the cogeneration unit was used primarily to heat the biodigesters and farm buildings.

Prior to the transition from manure to digestate, untreated manure was almost com- pletely removed (95%) from the storage tank. This resulted in 20 cm depth of untreated manure (145 m³) leftover in the 2,665 m³ storage tank on Aug 24, 2012. The first addi- tion of digestate into the storage tank occurred on Aug 29, 2012. The digestate pumping into the tank was done through a surface loading pipe frequently, but at irregular intervals, throughout the year.

10: <header>2.3.3 Flux measurements </header>

CH4 fluxes from the digestate storage tank were measured using the MMB method (Wagner-Riddle et al., 2006). Three air sampling towers and one wind tower were in- stalled around the circular storage tank (Figure 2.2). CH4 concentrations and windspeed were measured at four heights: 0.25, 1.00, 2.00, and 4.50 m above the tank wall (Figure 2.2). Windspeed was measured using four cup-anemometers (F460, Climatronics Corp., Bohemia, NY) connected to a 21X datalogger (Campbell Scientific Inc., Logan, UT) along with a wind vane (R.M. Young, Model 05103) installed at the top of the wind tower for wind direction measurement. The datalogger recorded one-minute wind direction and half- hourly windspeed averages. CH4 concentrations were determined using a custom-made, 12 intake air sampling system (Campbell Scientific Inc., Logan, UT) with a multi-inlet man- ifold with integrated pressure control and flow measurement, a tunable diode laser Trace

Gas Analyzer (TGA100A, Campbell Scientific Inc., Logan, UT) and a datalogger CR3000 (Campbell Scientific Inc., Logan, UT), as described in Kariyapperuma et al. (2017). At each intake, ~ 650 ml min-1 (maximum of 1000 ml min-1 was drawn at all times through single tube sample dryers (PD1T-1.5, Campbell Scientific Inc., Logan, UT). Part of this flow was directed to the analyzer (450 ml min-1 for 15 S (one intake at a time). Mean- while, flow from the other intakes went to a by-pass along with the excess flow from the selected intake (200 ml min-1. The TGA100A measured CH4 concentrations at 10 Hz (150 data points total per intake per switching cycle) and the first 4.5 S (45 data points) after a switch were omitted to avoid mixing samples from two intakes. One cycle lasted 180 S (12 intakes 15 s), hence there were 10 cycles per 30-min period.

TGA indicates the placement of trailer with trace gas analyzer. Diagram not to scale.

CH4 flux densities were calculated using the Integrated Horizontal Flux (IHF) method, ideal for sources like a manure/digestate storage tank. The method assumes that the flux emitted from a horizontal surface is carried by the mean horizontal wind and spread ver- tically downwind of the source. From the conservation of mass, knowing the downwind and the background (upwind) CH4 concentration (Cd and Cu, respectively), the horizontal windspeed Uz and the horizontal distance that the wind travels above the source (L), the
Time averaging the instantaneous flux densities and writing the wind and concentrations as a sum of their mean (bar) and fluctuation about the mean (prime), Eq. 2.1 becomes:

with two components: a mean flux (due to horizontal convection) and the turbulent flux (due to horizontal diffusion). No horizontal gradient in the upwind concentration (cu= = 0) is assumed (Denmead, 2012). In practical applications of the MMB method, the turbulent component is ignored and only the mean flux is measured (Park et al., 2010). Integration of Eq. 2.2, and assuming the turbulent component is negligible, results in half-hourly CH4 fluxes calculated from Park et al. (2006) and Wagner-Riddle et al. (2006):

where L is the fetch (m), Uz is the windspeed per height (m s-1), Acz is the concentration difference between upwind and downwind towers for each height (mg m-3 and Ah is the thickness of each layer above the source (0.5, 1.0, 1.0, and 2.0 m for Z from 1 to 4 at heights 0.25, 1.00, 2.00, 4.50 m, respectively). Each of the three towers was classified as upwind or downwind depending on wind direction. A tower was considered upwind for a

half-hour period when 1-min wind direction stayed within 180° from the tower location (Figure 2.2) during the half-hour period (i.e. 306° to 106° for the tower at 26° from North, 1° to 161° for the tower at 81° from North, and 130° to 290° for the tower at 210° from North). A tower was classified as downwind when 1 min wind direction stayed within + 70° from the tower location plus 180° (i.e. 136° to 276° for tower at 26°, 191° to 331° for tower at 81°, and 320° to 100° for tower at 210°). Only 30-min records with at least 80% of the maximum of 1050 observations per intake were considered in calculations. Fetch (i.e. distance over the manure surface that the wind traveled before reaching the downwind tower) was calculated for every minute based on the 1-min wind direction and the circular tank's geometry, and then averaged for the 30-min period according to Wagner-Riddle et al. (2006). When there were two upwind towers, the upwind concentration for each height was the average of the two upwind towers. When there were two downwind towers, the flux was calculated for each one separately and then averaged to obtain the final flux for the 30-min period. Daily fluxes were calculated by averaging the available 30-min fluxes on each day. Monthly emission totals were calculated by first averaging the forty eight 30- min means for each month and then scaling these values to monthly totals to reduce biases due to more data points retained after filtering for day-time (as explained below). Annual emission totals were calculated by summing monthly emissions.

half-hour period when 1-min wind direction stayed within 180° from the tower location (Figure 2.2) during the half-hour period (i.e. 306° to 106° for the tower at 26° from North, 1° to 161° for the tower at 81° from North, and 130° to 290° for the tower at 210° from North). A tower was classified as downwind when 1 min wind direction stayed within + 70° from the tower location plus 180° (i.e. 136° to 276° for tower at 26°, 191° to 331° for tower at 81°, and 320° to 100° for tower at 210°). Only 30-min records with at least 80% of the maximum of 1050 observations per intake were considered in calculations. Fetch (i.e. distance over the manure surface that the wind traveled before reaching the downwind tower) was calculated for every minute based on the 1-min wind direction and the circular tank's geometry, and then averaged for the 30-min period according to Wagner-Riddle et al. (2006). When there were two upwind towers, the upwind concentration for each height was the average of the two upwind towers. When there were two downwind towers, the flux was calculated for each one separately and then averaged to obtain the final flux for the 30-min period. Daily fluxes were calculated by averaging the available 30-min fluxes on each day. Monthly emission totals were calculated by first averaging the forty eight 30- min means for each month and then scaling these values to monthly totals to reduce biases due to more data points retained after filtering for day-time (as explained below). Annual emission totals were calculated by summing monthly emissions.

16: Mean half-hourly upwind CH4 concentration profiles were plotted to investigate even- tual influences from external CH4 sources and turbulent back diffusion effect on the lower air intakes (Wagner-Riddle et al., 2006). The lowest sampling height (at 0.25 m above the tank wall) of upwind towers presented concentrations much higher than the expected background CH4 concentration. The magnitude of this upwind enrichment of CH4 concen- tration was higher when the tank depth was low, presumably due to enhanced turbulence from the tank wall increasing the back-contamination effect. Hence, it was assumed that the upwind concentration of the lowest intake was equal to the concentration of the intake immediate above (at 1 m from the tank wall) at all times.

Filters were applied to remove unreliable data. Flux estimated for the half hour was ignored if the TGA100A was not working properly for the period, or any of the intakes had sample flow rates out of the range from 400 to 1000 ml min or the by-pass pressure was above 500 mb (6.4% of cases). The 30-min flux data from mean wind direction below 25° or above 250° were also removed to filter out the wind range when the wind came from the farm structures surrounding the storage tank (43.2% of cases) (Figure 2.1). Data points were removed anytime the 30-min mean wind speed was lower than 1.2 m s-1 at any height or below 2 m S for the top cup anemometer at 4.5 m above the tank wall (22.9% of cases) and when there was any instrumentation malfunction (3.7% of cases).
Potential outliers were identified by performing a case-wise diagnostic (considering three standard deviation limit) in a linear regression analysis between daily CH4 flux per

volume (dependent variable) and digestate temperature at 1 m (independent variable). Fur- ther investigation of the potential outliers was done considering factors like the number of valid 30-min periods in the day, the time of the day, the downwind CH4 concentration profile and wind direction when the unusual flux occurred as well if there was any special activity occurring at that time as reported in the logbook. The expected concentration pro- file has its maximum value at the lowest intake, close to the source, and it decreases until the background CH4 concentration at the highest intake is reached. If the highest down- wind CH4 concentration did not occur at the lowest intake (at 0.25 m height), but above it (at 1 m or 2 m height) the data were removed. In addition, negative fluxes (< -5 ug m m-2 s-1), data sampled during human activity in the tank vicinity, data points where upwind CH4 concentration was > 3 ppmv at any sampling height or data from downwind CH4 con- centration > 3.5 ppmv at the fourth air sampling intake (at 4.5 m) were also removed (all manual filters accounted for 0.5% of cases).

The useful data, after all filtering criteria were applied, corresponded to 23% of the 17,520 30-min periods in the year, being 54% daytime fluxes (from 0800 to 2000 h) and 46% night-time (from 2000 to 0800 h). The seasonal distribution of the valid data was 26% in winter, 30% in spring, 24% in summer and 20% in fall.

Fur- ther investigation of the potential outliers was done considering factors like the number of valid 30-min periods in the day, the time of the day, the downwind CH4 concentration profile and wind direction when the unusual flux occurred as well if there was any special activity occurring at that time as reported in the logbook. The expected concentration pro- file has its maximum value at the lowest intake, close to the source, and it decreases until the background CH4 concentration at the highest intake is reached. If the highest down- wind CH4 concentration did not occur at the lowest intake (at 0.25 m height), but above it (at 1 m or 2 m height) the data were removed. In addition, negative fluxes (< -5 ug m m-2 s-1), data sampled during human activity in the tank vicinity, data points where upwind CH4 concentration was > 3 ppmv at any sampling height or data from downwind CH4 con- centration > 3.5 ppmv at the fourth air sampling intake (at 4.5 m) were also removed (all manual filters accounted for 0.5% of cases).

The useful data, after all filtering criteria were applied, corresponded to 23% of the 17,520 30-min periods in the year, being 54% daytime fluxes (from 0800 to 2000 h) and 46% night-time (from 2000 to 0800 h). The seasonal distribution of the valid data was 26% in winter, 30% in spring, 24% in summer and 20% in fall.
19: Mean half-hourly upwind CH4 concentration profiles were plotted to investigate even- tual influences from external CH4 sources and turbulent back diffusion effect on the lower air intakes (Wagner-Riddle et al., 2006). The lowest sampling height (at 0.25 m above the tank wall) of upwind towers presented concentrations much higher than the expected background CH4 concentration. The magnitude of this upwind enrichment of CH4 concen- tration was higher when the tank depth was low, presumably due to enhanced turbulence from the tank wall increasing the back-contamination effect. Hence, it was assumed that the upwind concentration of the lowest intake was equal to the concentration of the intake immediate above (at 1 m from the tank wall) at all times.

Filters were applied to remove unreliable data. Flux estimated for the half hour was ignored if the TGA100A was not working properly for the period, or any of the intakes had sample flow rates out of the range from 400 to 1000 ml min or the by-pass pressure was above 500 mb (6.4% of cases). The 30-min flux data from mean wind direction below 25° or above 250° were also removed to filter out the wind range when the wind came from the farm structures surrounding the storage tank (43.2% of cases) (Figure 2.1). Data points were removed anytime the 30-min mean wind speed was lower than 1.2 m s-1 at any height or below 2 m S for the top cup anemometer at 4.5 m above the tank wall (22.9% of cases) and when there was any instrumentation malfunction (3.7% of cases).

20: Digestate temperature per depth was measured using three sets of four thermocouples vertically attached to a string at different depths: 0.05, 1.00, and 2.00 m below a floating lid

at the surface. A fourth thermocouple was installed at 0.50 m above the tank bottom using a float bottle attached to a weight. One thermocouple set was installed at the centre of the circular tank and the other two were installed at the southwest and northeast edges, where there were also two infrared radiometers facing the digestate surface (SW and NE) (Figure 2.2). For data analysis, temperature at each depth was calculated by averaging the 3 sets (centre, SW and NE). The thermocouple sets remained in the tank for most of the study period, except from DOY 123 to 144 (May 3 to May 24, 2013), when they were removed to allow digestate mixing and pumping out of the tank. All 14 temperature sensors and a shielded thermocouple that measured air temperature were attached to an AM416 Relay Multiplexer connected to a 23X datalogger (Campbell Scientific Inc., Logan, UT). Half- hourly averages of air and digestate temperatures were recorded. Digestate surface depth from the upper edge of the concrete was measured manually approximately twice weekly with a measuring stick.

22: <header>2.3.6 Digestate and manure sampling and analysis </header>

In 2013, a digestate sample was collected every month, except for Mar and Dec, totaling 10 samples. The samples were collected from the 0-0.50 m surface layer. Each time, five samples ( 5 L each) were collected with a bucket attached to a long PVC pipe (3.7 m), around the tank edge and mixed to get one representative sample (0.5L) from the digestate. During winter time, when digestate surface was frozen, the samples were taken close to the loading pipe. Similarly, during the manure year, 8 manure samples comprising the total

depth of manure in the tank were collected at 0.50 m depth intervals in Jan, Mar, Apr, Sept, Oct and Dec. The manure sampling per depth was done by closing a custom-made sampler, sinking it into the sampling depth (middle of the 0.50 m interval), allowing the sample to flow in (0.5-1.0 L) closing it again and lifting up to the surface. Each manure sample per depth was a composite of 4-5 samples collected around the tank edge at the specific depth.

The samples were sent to the Agriculture and Food Laboratory Services (University of Guelph) for carbon analyses (organic carbon, inorganic carbon and total carbon) and to the Ontario Rural Wastewater Center (University of Guelph-Campus d'Alfred) to be analyzed for pH, total solids (TS), volatile solids (VS), total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN), ammonium (NH), nitrate (NO) and volatile fatty acids (VFAs, acetic, propionic and butyric).

The samples were sent to the Agriculture and Food Laboratory Services (University of Guelph) for carbon analyses (organic carbon, inorganic carbon and total carbon) and to the Ontario Rural Wastewater Center (University of Guelph-Campus d'Alfred) to be analyzed for pH, total solids (TS), volatile solids (VS), total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN), ammonium (NH), nitrate (NO) and volatile fatty acids (VFAs, acetic, propionic and butyric).
24: Emissions were normalized by volume and VS by dividing the 30-min flux densities per linearly interpolated 30-min manure depth and total mass of VS, respectively (considering the tank surface area). To calculate the total VS mass, data from the manure year (sampling per depth) was used to identify a typical depth VS distribution in the storage tank and adjust the concentration obtained from the samples taken for the surface layer only. The correction factor was 0.35, on average, which indicates how much of the total VS calculated from the VS concentration at the surface (VS surface concentration times total volume) was representative of the total VS calculated using the VS concentration profile (the sum of VS concentration per depth times the volume per layer of 0.50 m). Hence, the tank

VS concentration was calculated by dividing the VS concentration at the surface by 0.35. Total VS mass in the tank was calculated by multiplying the tank VS concentration by the volume. Based on the laboratory results for VS and historical loading and emptying time of the tank, three VS concentrations were obtained for manure: 59 g L-1 from DOY 1 to 90 (Jan 1 to Mar 31), 39 g L-1 from DOY 91 to 212 (Apr 1 to Jul 31) and 89 g L-1 from DOY 213 to 365 (Aug 1 to Dec 31. For digestate, a daily VS concentration vector was created by linearly interpolating the discrete values on sampling dates. The VS total mass monthly mean was calculated from averaging daily VS total mass (linearly interpolating VS concentration linearly interpolated daily volume).

The mean monthly CH4 fluxes per volume (g m-3 d-¹ were multiplied by the num- ber of days per month and summed to obtain the annual CH4 emission per volume. The total monthly and annual CH4 emissions per VS were obtained by summing the linearly interpolated daily fluxes per VS throughout the month/year, respectively.

Shapiro-Wilk tests of normality and normal Q-Q plots were analysed for all parameters. Pairwise comparison two-tailed t-tests were used to compare the mean of all parameters between digestate and untreated manure for normal distributions. Wilcoxon signed rank test was used to compare means of parameters with non-normal distributions. Pearson's and cross correlation analyses were used to examine the relationships between daily and monthly CH4 fluxes and digestate parameters to identify emission drivers or inhibitors. Statistical analysis was done in SPSS.

0: <title>2.4 Results and discussion </title>
1: <header>2.4.1 Manure and digestate composition </header>

The digestate and manure mean composition were significantly different for total chem- ical oxygen demand, organic, inorganic and total carbon, ammonium, total VFAs, acetic acid and pH (Table 2.2). Total carbon content in digestate was slightly lower due to a 20% decrease in organic carbon content in manure, although digestate inorganic carbon increased in proportion to the total carbon and it was more than 3 times higher than the ma- nure inorganic carbon (15% in digestate and 4% in manure). The greatest difference was observed in the total VFA concentration, which was 80% lower in the digestate, mostly due to the significant decrease in acetic acid concentration (4 times lower). Digestate pH was alkaline (8.0+0.1) and higher than manure at a pH (7.3+0.2), which was close to neutral.

The digestate mean TS and VS concentrations in the storage tank were 71 and 43 g kg-1, , respectively. These were both within the range reported in other studies with diges- tate from cattle manure or mixtures of cattle manure and co-substrates (off-farm or energy crops). The range of data available being 33-70 g TS kg-1 and 27-86 g VS kg-1 (Sommer et al., 2007; Amon et al., 2006; Liebetrau et al., 2013; Rodhe et al., 2015; Baldé et al., 2016b). The relation between VS and TS in digestate was 0.61 g VS g-1 TS, on average. Total and ammoniacal-N mean concentrations were within the range observed in other studies 2.0-5.8 g NL and 1.1-3.3 g NH4 L-1 (Amon et al., 2006; Liebetrau et al., 2013; Rodhe et al., 2015), respectively.

Table 2.2: Manure and digestate composition based on samples taken from the surface layer during 2010/2011 and 2013. Note that a correction factor was applied to surface samples to obtain a tank-average by dividing by 0.35 for DM, TS and VS parameters.
*Means for the manure and digestate significantly different at the 0.05 level.

6: <header>2.4.2 Environmental factors </header>

In 2013, the total rainfall was higher than the 30-year average (1981-2010), while in the manure year it was lower than normal. Overall, 2013 was wet compared with the historical data and the manure year was dryer (Table 2.3). Daily air temperature at the research site varied from - 17.6 °C to 27.1 °C in 2013, with monthly averages compatible with the 30- year normal. Most months in the manure year and in 2013 had monthly mean temperatures within the 30-year normal range (mean + STD) with Apr and Nov slightly cooler 1°C) in 2013, and Jul and Aug slightly warmer (~ 1 °C) in the manure year. The summer was slightly 2 °C) cooler in 2013 as compared to the manure year. Overall, both years had similar and normal temperature conditions.

The monthly mean digestate temperatures, though, were warmer than manure monthly temperatures (Figure 2.3) for almost all months despite the differences in the average monthly tank depth (Table 2.3). In Dec, when the mean storage volume and air temper- ature were similar for both years, the temperature at 0.50 m above the bottom of the tank was 3 °C higher for digestate (2013) compared to manure (2010). In May, Jun and Jul, when average digestate depth was at least 0.5 m (375 m³) lower than manure depth, mean temperature of digestate was > 7 °C higher than manure, even though the air temperature was lower in July 2013 (~ 2°C). In Oct and Nov, digestate depth was at least 0.5 m higher and digestate temperatures were at least 5 °C higher compared to manure. Overall, for similar air temperature, digestate was warmer than manure and the correlation coefficient between air temperature and the temperature at 0.50 m above the bottom of the storage tank

Overall, for similar air temperature, digestate was warmer than manure and the correlation coefficient between air temperature and the temperature at 0.50 m above the bottom of the storage tank

9: <header>2.4.2 Environmental factors </header>

In 2013, the total rainfall was higher than the 30-year average (1981-2010), while in the manure year it was lower than normal. Overall, 2013 was wet compared with the historical data and the manure year was dryer (Table 2.3). Daily air temperature at the research site varied from - 17.6 °C to 27.1 °C in 2013, with monthly averages compatible with the 30- year normal. Most months in the manure year and in 2013 had monthly mean temperatures within the 30-year normal range (mean + STD) with Apr and Nov slightly cooler 1°C) in 2013, and Jul and Aug slightly warmer (~ 1 °C) in the manure year. The summer was slightly 2 °C) cooler in 2013 as compared to the manure year. Overall, both years had similar and normal temperature conditions.

The monthly mean digestate temperatures, though, were warmer than manure monthly temperatures (Figure 2.3) for almost all months despite the differences in the average monthly tank depth (Table 2.3). In Dec, when the mean storage volume and air temper- ature were similar for both years, the temperature at 0.50 m above the bottom of the tank was 3 °C higher for digestate (2013) compared to manure (2010). In May, Jun and Jul, when average digestate depth was at least 0.5 m (375 m³) lower than manure depth, mean temperature of digestate was > 7 °C higher than manure, even though the air temperature was lower in July 2013 (~ 2°C). In Oct and Nov, digestate depth was at least 0.5 m higher and digestate temperatures were at least 5 °C higher compared to manure. Overall, for similar air temperature, digestate was warmer than manure and the correlation coefficient between air temperature and the temperature at 0.50 m above the bottom of the storage tank
