12: <header>2.4.3 Methane fluxes from digestate and driving factors </header>

Several loading and emptying cycles occurred over 2013 (Figure 2.4a and b). From the beginning of the year until the end of May 30 (DOY 150) the temperature was low (< 10 °C) in the whole bulk of digestate (Figure 2.4a) and the fluxes were low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1 (Figure 2.4b). Through the winter until mid-Mar, flux peaks were noticed when the air temperature increased to positive values (e.g. DOY 10 (Jan 10), air temperature 0.5 °C; DOY 30 (Jan 30), 7.6 °C; DOY 69 (Mar 10), 4.7 °C; DOY 70 (Mar 11), 4.3 °C). At

that time, the surface was fully covered with ice and the increased air temperature caused thaw, likely releasing CH4 trapped below the ice cover, which is consistent with previous observations (VanderZaag et al., 2011). After the air temperature became positive in Apr, ice cover was only partial and these events did not occur. In May, the tank was partially emptied (DOY 129; May 9) and the air temperature started to increase, but the average CH4 flux was still low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1. In Jun, the tank was loaded again, but this time, due to higher air temperatures since May, the bulk of digestate was warmer (from a monthly average of 5.9 °C in Apr to 12.8 °C in May for the bottom of the tank). Throughout Jun the daily average CH4 fluxes increased to > 200 ug m-2 s-¹. From Jul to Oct the average monthly CH4 flux from digestate was 178 ug m-2 s-1 about 8 times higher than in May (monthly average 21 ug It is important to note that summer digestate tempera- tures were lower at this site (maximum monthly average at 50 cm above bottom of 19 °C; Table 2.3, Figure 2.5) in comparison to other studies on treated manure in Ontario. (Baldé et al., 2016a) reported temperatures above 19 °C from Jul to Sept with a peak monthly av- erage of 22 °C at a concrete tank with separated liquid dairy manure. Baldé et al. (2016b) reported peak temperature of 21 °C at an earthen storage of unseparated digestate with a thick surface crust. In our study, there was a thin crust throughout summer only and some bubbles on the digestate surface in summer and fall.

Digestate temperature started decreasing in Nov (from the monthly average of 16.8 °C in Oct to 11.8 °C in Nov at the bottom of the tank) when the tank was being emptied and air temperature was also decreasing (Figure 2.4a). The daily CH4 fluxes decreased to < 50

ug in Nov (Figure 2.4b). The tank was loaded again on Dec 3 (DOY 337), but the bulk of digestate remained < 10 °C and the few data points of flux measured this month were similar to the beginning of the year.

There was a significant (p < 0.05) positive correlation between CH4 fluxes and all tem- peratures within the digestate and air temperature (Table 2.4), with the strongest correlation found for the digestate temperature measured at 2 m depth (r = 0.98) with no evident lag (Figure 2.5). There was not a significant correlation at 0.05 level between VS concentration and CH4 fluxes from digestate (r = 0.37, Table 2.4).

Bars at the top show colour scale air temperature in 2013 and the average digestate temperature across all depths. (b) Daily methane (CH4) fluxes (ug CH4 m-2 -superscript(1)) pened when thermocouple sets were removed from the tank for agitation and emptying of and digestate volume (m³ in the storage tank in 2013. Jan 10) or outliers excluding the peaks related to specific events (e.g. frozen surface pattern Average season, emissions per 30-min were plotted to determine the diurnal emission tern (Figure that occurred only once during the period and affected the thawing in 2 times higher 2.6). A diurnal pattern was observed in summer and fall, with seasonal pat- per than nighttime fluxes occurring from 0700 to 1000 h in summer daytime and fluxes from

1100 to 1600 h in fall. VanderZaag et al. (2014), measuring whole farm emissions before and after emptying the manure storage tank, also showed a clear diurnal pattern with above average emissions between 0900 to 1700 h. In our study, peak fluxes occurred early morn- ing in summer (0700 to 1000 h) and late morning (1100 h) in fall, which corresponded to sunrise and solar heating of the digestate surface causing bubble bursting and CH4 release. Diurnal cycles and peak related to sunrise in summer and fall have been reported in other studies on manure (Baldé et al., 2016b; VanderZaag et al., 2014). Baldé et al. (2016b) reported a diurnal peak after sunrise (0800 to 1000 h) in summer (Jun to Aug) and late morning (1000 h) in fall (Sep to Nov). In spring, fluxes consistently below the season aver- age occurred from 0000 to 0800 h and for the rest of the day no noticeable peak occurred. Winter fluxes were close to the average at all times of the day. The diurnal trend in summer showed emissions close to the mean value early in the morning (from 0400 to 0630 h), similar to the findings of Wood et al. (2013), for dairy manure, and in the afternoon (1430 to 1700h), suggesting that these would be the appropriate times for manual measurements of the fluxes, if necessary.

Figure 2.6: Diurnal methane (CH4) emissions (g CH4 m-3 d-1) per season from digestate storage: winter (Jan 1 to Mar 31), spring (Apr 1 to Jun 30), summer (Jul 1 to Sep 30) and Fall (Oct 1 to Dec 31) in 2013. Vertical bars show the standard error of the mean. Numbers on the right show the season average flux and season average digestate temperature at the bottom of the tank in brackets.

The digestate added to the storage tank was much warmer (temperature in the biodi- gesters was 38 °C) than the untreated manure, and digestate storage temperature varied throughout the year playing a major role in driving CH4 emissions. Although average di- gestate temperatures at the bottom of the tank were similar in spring and fall (11.6 and 12.8° °C, respectively), the fall average flux was almost two times higher than the spring av- erage (3.15 0.83 g in fall and 1.73 0.53 g m-superscript(3) in spring), probably due to microbial communities increasing during summer and remaining active in fall (Figure 2.6). This observation is consistent with previous studies showing that longer retention times

at moderately warm temperatures lead to exponentially increasing CH4 emission rates (Le Riche et al., 2016). Similar to untreated manure storage, there was a hysteresis effect on the CH4 flux response to temperature, where the fluxes in fall (cooling) were much higher than in spring (warming), despite similar temperatures Kariyapperuma et al. (2017). When spring starts, the digestate volume is cool due to the cold winter months and the CH4 pro- duction activity is very low. In contrast, fall is preceded by a very warm season when the digestate volume is already warm and the CH4 production is active. The time for warming up or cooling down the bulk of digestate depends on the storage volume and conditions, SO that digestate temperature close to the bottom of the tank is a better predictor than air temperature. In fact, seasonal average air temperatures were much higher in spring than fall (11.6 and 1.4 C, respectively) contrasting the results for manure temperature. These results indicate that air temperature is not a good predictor of fluxes and that there is a strong influence of the precedent conditions.

Average daily emissions in summer were 5.8+1.0 g m-3 d-¹, slightly lower than but the same order of magnitude reported in Rodhe et al. (2015) and Clemens et al. (2006), 7.8 and 8.2 g m-3 d-¹ respectively, from pilot-scale storages with no solid-separation. The annual average CH4 emission from digestate of 2.7 0.5 g was 7 times lower than the 19 g m-3 d-¹ found in the year-long field study in a single digester biogas plant near Ottawa (Baldé et al., 2016b), likely due to the longer HRT and the second biodigester operating in series in our study site. Likewise, when scaled by VS, the annual average emission was 0.07 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹, four times lower than the 0.27 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹ in Baldé et al.

(2016b). Determining the annual total digestate emissions for the entire biogas plant requires some additional calculations and assumptions due to the multiple storages at the facility because emissions were measured only from the concrete tank (2,665 m³) Considering the annual biodigester loading rate of 12,600 m³ we can make the assumption that the annual total volume of digestate produced was approximately 11,000 m³ (after digestion and solid separation). Using the measured annual average digestate emission rate per volume of 2.7 g CH4 m³d-¹ we calculate an annual total digestate emission of 10.8 Mg y-1. By compar- ison, Baldé et al. (2016b) reported annual digestate emissions of 65 Mg y-1 at a biogas plant with a 1000 m³ reactor and 370 kW generator where manure was digested along with 48% off-farm materials with a 27-day hydraulic retention time and stored without solid- liquid separation in a large earthen storage (peak capacity 10,000 m³) with high quantities of residual sludge at the bottom and summer temperature > 20 °C for 3 months. In contrast, the biodigester in our study had less off-farm material added, the digester had a two-stage design with two reactors in series giving a 60-day overall retention time, solid-liquid sepa- ration after digestion, and the concrete digestate storage tank was purposefully emptied of as much sludge and residual organic material as possible prior to the study, thereby min- imizing methanogenic inoculum and reducing residual organic substrates in the storage. These findings from two biogas plants in Ontario, Canada show that biogas plant recipe (type and percentage of off-farm materials), biogas plant operation (hydraulic retention time and solid/liquid-separation), and digestate storage conditions (residual sludge, tem-
perature, and retention time) have a major impact on the CH4 emissions from the digestate storage.

At

that time, the surface was fully covered with ice and the increased air temperature caused thaw, likely releasing CH4 trapped below the ice cover, which is consistent with previous observations (VanderZaag et al., 2011). After the air temperature became positive in Apr, ice cover was only partial and these events did not occur. In May, the tank was partially emptied (DOY 129; May 9) and the air temperature started to increase, but the average CH4 flux was still low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1. In Jun, the tank was loaded again, but this time, due to higher air temperatures since May, the bulk of digestate was warmer (from a monthly average of 5.9 °C in Apr to 12.8 °C in May for the bottom of the tank). Throughout Jun the daily average CH4 fluxes increased to > 200 ug m-2 s-¹. From Jul to Oct the average monthly CH4 flux from digestate was 178 ug m-2 s-1 about 8 times higher than in May (monthly average 21 ug It is important to note that summer digestate tempera- tures were lower at this site (maximum monthly average at 50 cm above bottom of 19 °C; Table 2.3, Figure 2.5) in comparison to other studies on treated manure in Ontario. (Baldé et al., 2016a) reported temperatures above 19 °C from Jul to Sept with a peak monthly av- erage of 22 °C at a concrete tank with separated liquid dairy manure. Baldé et al. (2016b) reported peak temperature of 21 °C at an earthen storage of unseparated digestate with a thick surface crust. In our study, there was a thin crust throughout summer only and some bubbles on the digestate surface in summer and fall.

Digestate temperature started decreasing in Nov (from the monthly average of 16.8 °C in Oct to 11.8 °C in Nov at the bottom of the tank) when the tank was being emptied and air temperature was also decreasing (Figure 2.4a). The daily CH4 fluxes decreased to < 50

ug in Nov (Figure 2.4b). The tank was loaded again on Dec 3 (DOY 337), but the bulk of digestate remained < 10 °C and the few data points of flux measured this month were similar to the beginning of the year.

There was a significant (p < 0.05) positive correlation between CH4 fluxes and all tem- peratures within the digestate and air temperature (Table 2.4), with the strongest correlation found for the digestate temperature measured at 2 m depth (r = 0.98) with no evident lag (Figure 2.5). There was not a significant correlation at 0.05 level between VS concentration and CH4 fluxes from digestate (r = 0.37, Table 2.4).

Bars at the top show colour scale air temperature in 2013 and the average digestate temperature across all depths. (b) Daily methane (CH4) fluxes (ug CH4 m-2 -superscript(1)) pened when thermocouple sets were removed from the tank for agitation and emptying of and digestate volume (m³ in the storage tank in 2013. Jan 10) or outliers excluding the peaks related to specific events (e.g. frozen surface pattern Average season, emissions per 30-min were plotted to determine the diurnal emission tern (Figure that occurred only once during the period and affected the thawing in 2 times higher 2.6). A diurnal pattern was observed in summer and fall, with seasonal pat- per than nighttime fluxes occurring from 0700 to 1000 h in summer daytime and fluxes from

1100 to 1600 h in fall. VanderZaag et al. (2014), measuring whole farm emissions before and after emptying the manure storage tank, also showed a clear diurnal pattern with above average emissions between 0900 to 1700 h. In our study, peak fluxes occurred early morn- ing in summer (0700 to 1000 h) and late morning (1100 h) in fall, which corresponded to sunrise and solar heating of the digestate surface causing bubble bursting and CH4 release. Diurnal cycles and peak related to sunrise in summer and fall have been reported in other studies on manure (Baldé et al., 2016b; VanderZaag et al., 2014). Baldé et al. (2016b) reported a diurnal peak after sunrise (0800 to 1000 h) in summer (Jun to Aug) and late morning (1000 h) in fall (Sep to Nov). In spring, fluxes consistently below the season aver- age occurred from 0000 to 0800 h and for the rest of the day no noticeable peak occurred. Winter fluxes were close to the average at all times of the day. The diurnal trend in summer showed emissions close to the mean value early in the morning (from 0400 to 0630 h), similar to the findings of Wood et al. (2013), for dairy manure, and in the afternoon (1430 to 1700h), suggesting that these would be the appropriate times for manual measurements of the fluxes, if necessary.

Figure 2.6: Diurnal methane (CH4) emissions (g CH4 m-3 d-1) per season from digestate storage: winter (Jan 1 to Mar 31), spring (Apr 1 to Jun 30), summer (Jul 1 to Sep 30) and Fall (Oct 1 to Dec 31) in 2013. Vertical bars show the standard error of the mean. Numbers on the right show the season average flux and season average digestate temperature at the bottom of the tank in brackets.

The digestate added to the storage tank was much warmer (temperature in the biodi- gesters was 38 °C) than the untreated manure, and digestate storage temperature varied throughout the year playing a major role in driving CH4 emissions. Although average di- gestate temperatures at the bottom of the tank were similar in spring and fall (11.6 and 12.8° °C, respectively), the fall average flux was almost two times higher than the spring av- erage (3.15 0.83 g in fall and 1.73 0.53 g m-superscript(3) in spring), probably due to microbial communities increasing during summer and remaining active in fall (Figure 2.6). This observation is consistent with previous studies showing that longer retention times

at moderately warm temperatures lead to exponentially increasing CH4 emission rates (Le Riche et al., 2016). Similar to untreated manure storage, there was a hysteresis effect on the CH4 flux response to temperature, where the fluxes in fall (cooling) were much higher than in spring (warming), despite similar temperatures Kariyapperuma et al. (2017). When spring starts, the digestate volume is cool due to the cold winter months and the CH4 pro- duction activity is very low. In contrast, fall is preceded by a very warm season when the digestate volume is already warm and the CH4 production is active. The time for warming up or cooling down the bulk of digestate depends on the storage volume and conditions, SO that digestate temperature close to the bottom of the tank is a better predictor than air temperature. In fact, seasonal average air temperatures were much higher in spring than fall (11.6 and 1.4 C, respectively) contrasting the results for manure temperature. These results indicate that air temperature is not a good predictor of fluxes and that there is a strong influence of the precedent conditions.

Average daily emissions in summer were 5.8+1.0 g m-3 d-¹, slightly lower than but the same order of magnitude reported in Rodhe et al. (2015) and Clemens et al. (2006), 7.8 and 8.2 g m-3 d-¹ respectively, from pilot-scale storages with no solid-separation. The annual average CH4 emission from digestate of 2.7 0.5 g was 7 times lower than the 19 g m-3 d-¹ found in the year-long field study in a single digester biogas plant near Ottawa (Baldé et al., 2016b), likely due to the longer HRT and the second biodigester operating in series in our study site. Likewise, when scaled by VS, the annual average emission was 0.07 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹, four times lower than the 0.27 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹ in Baldé et al.

(2016b). Determining the annual total digestate emissions for the entire biogas plant requires some additional calculations and assumptions due to the multiple storages at the facility because emissions were measured only from the concrete tank (2,665 m³) Considering the annual biodigester loading rate of 12,600 m³ we can make the assumption that the annual total volume of digestate produced was approximately 11,000 m³ (after digestion and solid separation). Using the measured annual average digestate emission rate per volume of 2.7 g CH4 m³d-¹ we calculate an annual total digestate emission of 10.8 Mg y-1. By compar- ison, Baldé et al. (2016b) reported annual digestate emissions of 65 Mg y-1 at a biogas plant with a 1000 m³ reactor and 370 kW generator where manure was digested along with 48% off-farm materials with a 27-day hydraulic retention time and stored without solid- liquid separation in a large earthen storage (peak capacity 10,000 m³) with high quantities of residual sludge at the bottom and summer temperature > 20 °C for 3 months. In contrast, the biodigester in our study had less off-farm material added, the digester had a two-stage design with two reactors in series giving a 60-day overall retention time, solid-liquid sepa- ration after digestion, and the concrete digestate storage tank was purposefully emptied of as much sludge and residual organic material as possible prior to the study, thereby min- imizing methanogenic inoculum and reducing residual organic substrates in the storage. These findings from two biogas plants in Ontario, Canada show that biogas plant recipe (type and percentage of off-farm materials), biogas plant operation (hydraulic retention time and solid/liquid-separation), and digestate storage conditions (residual sludge, tem-
perature, and retention time) have a major impact on the CH4 emissions from the digestate storage.

Although average di- gestate temperatures at the bottom of the tank were similar in spring and fall (11.6 and 12.8° °C, respectively), the fall average flux was almost two times higher than the spring av- erage (3.15 0.83 g in fall and 1.73 0.53 g m-superscript(3) in spring), probably due to microbial communities increasing during summer and remaining active in fall (Figure 2.6). This observation is consistent with previous studies showing that longer retention times

at moderately warm temperatures lead to exponentially increasing CH4 emission rates (Le Riche et al., 2016). Similar to untreated manure storage, there was a hysteresis effect on the CH4 flux response to temperature, where the fluxes in fall (cooling) were much higher than in spring (warming), despite similar temperatures Kariyapperuma et al. (2017). When spring starts, the digestate volume is cool due to the cold winter months and the CH4 pro- duction activity is very low. In contrast, fall is preceded by a very warm season when the digestate volume is already warm and the CH4 production is active. The time for warming up or cooling down the bulk of digestate depends on the storage volume and conditions, SO that digestate temperature close to the bottom of the tank is a better predictor than air temperature. In fact, seasonal average air temperatures were much higher in spring than fall (11.6 and 1.4 C, respectively) contrasting the results for manure temperature. These results indicate that air temperature is not a good predictor of fluxes and that there is a strong influence of the precedent conditions.

Average daily emissions in summer were 5.8+1.0 g m-3 d-¹, slightly lower than but the same order of magnitude reported in Rodhe et al. (2015) and Clemens et al. (2006), 7.8 and 8.2 g m-3 d-¹ respectively, from pilot-scale storages with no solid-separation. The annual average CH4 emission from digestate of 2.7 0.5 g was 7 times lower than the 19 g m-3 d-¹ found in the year-long field study in a single digester biogas plant near Ottawa (Baldé et al., 2016b), likely due to the longer HRT and the second biodigester operating in series in our study site. Likewise, when scaled by VS, the annual average emission was 0.07 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹, four times lower than the 0.27 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹ in Baldé et al.

(2016b). Determining the annual total digestate emissions for the entire biogas plant requires some additional calculations and assumptions due to the multiple storages at the facility because emissions were measured only from the concrete tank (2,665 m³) Considering the annual biodigester loading rate of 12,600 m³ we can make the assumption that the annual total volume of digestate produced was approximately 11,000 m³ (after digestion and solid separation). Using the measured annual average digestate emission rate per volume of 2.7 g CH4 m³d-¹ we calculate an annual total digestate emission of 10.8 Mg y-1. By compar- ison, Baldé et al. (2016b) reported annual digestate emissions of 65 Mg y-1 at a biogas plant with a 1000 m³ reactor and 370 kW generator where manure was digested along with 48% off-farm materials with a 27-day hydraulic retention time and stored without solid- liquid separation in a large earthen storage (peak capacity 10,000 m³) with high quantities of residual sludge at the bottom and summer temperature > 20 °C for 3 months. In contrast, the biodigester in our study had less off-farm material added, the digester had a two-stage design with two reactors in series giving a 60-day overall retention time, solid-liquid sepa- ration after digestion, and the concrete digestate storage tank was purposefully emptied of as much sludge and residual organic material as possible prior to the study, thereby min- imizing methanogenic inoculum and reducing residual organic substrates in the storage. These findings from two biogas plants in Ontario, Canada show that biogas plant recipe (type and percentage of off-farm materials), biogas plant operation (hydraulic retention time and solid/liquid-separation), and digestate storage conditions (residual sludge, tem-
perature, and retention time) have a major impact on the CH4 emissions from the digestate storage.
17: <header>2.4.3 Methane fluxes from digestate and driving factors </header>

Several loading and emptying cycles occurred over 2013 (Figure 2.4a and b). From the beginning of the year until the end of May 30 (DOY 150) the temperature was low (< 10 °C) in the whole bulk of digestate (Figure 2.4a) and the fluxes were low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1 (Figure 2.4b). Through the winter until mid-Mar, flux peaks were noticed when the air temperature increased to positive values (e.g. DOY 10 (Jan 10), air temperature 0.5 °C; DOY 30 (Jan 30), 7.6 °C; DOY 69 (Mar 10), 4.7 °C; DOY 70 (Mar 11), 4.3 °C). At

that time, the surface was fully covered with ice and the increased air temperature caused thaw, likely releasing CH4 trapped below the ice cover, which is consistent with previous observations (VanderZaag et al., 2011). After the air temperature became positive in Apr, ice cover was only partial and these events did not occur. In May, the tank was partially emptied (DOY 129; May 9) and the air temperature started to increase, but the average CH4 flux was still low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1. In Jun, the tank was loaded again, but this time, due to higher air temperatures since May, the bulk of digestate was warmer (from a monthly average of 5.9 °C in Apr to 12.8 °C in May for the bottom of the tank). Throughout Jun the daily average CH4 fluxes increased to > 200 ug m-2 s-¹. From Jul to Oct the average monthly CH4 flux from digestate was 178 ug m-2 s-1 about 8 times higher than in May (monthly average 21 ug It is important to note that summer digestate tempera- tures were lower at this site (maximum monthly average at 50 cm above bottom of 19 °C; Table 2.3, Figure 2.5) in comparison to other studies on treated manure in Ontario. (Baldé et al., 2016a) reported temperatures above 19 °C from Jul to Sept with a peak monthly av- erage of 22 °C at a concrete tank with separated liquid dairy manure. Baldé et al. (2016b) reported peak temperature of 21 °C at an earthen storage of unseparated digestate with a thick surface crust. In our study, there was a thin crust throughout summer only and some bubbles on the digestate surface in summer and fall.

Digestate temperature started decreasing in Nov (from the monthly average of 16.8 °C in Oct to 11.8 °C in Nov at the bottom of the tank) when the tank was being emptied and air temperature was also decreasing (Figure 2.4a). The daily CH4 fluxes decreased to < 50

ug in Nov (Figure 2.4b). The tank was loaded again on Dec 3 (DOY 337), but the bulk of digestate remained < 10 °C and the few data points of flux measured this month were similar to the beginning of the year.

There was a significant (p < 0.05) positive correlation between CH4 fluxes and all tem- peratures within the digestate and air temperature (Table 2.4), with the strongest correlation found for the digestate temperature measured at 2 m depth (r = 0.98) with no evident lag (Figure 2.5). There was not a significant correlation at 0.05 level between VS concentration and CH4 fluxes from digestate (r = 0.37, Table 2.4).

Bars at the top show colour scale air temperature in 2013 and the average digestate temperature across all depths. (b) Daily methane (CH4) fluxes (ug CH4 m-2 -superscript(1)) pened when thermocouple sets were removed from the tank for agitation and emptying of and digestate volume (m³ in the storage tank in 2013. Vertical bars show the standard deviation for the daily mean.

Jan 10) or outliers excluding the peaks related to specific events (e.g. frozen surface pattern Average season, emissions per 30-min were plotted to determine the diurnal emission tern (Figure that occurred only once during the period and affected the thawing in 2 times higher 2.6). A diurnal pattern was observed in summer and fall, with seasonal pat- per than nighttime fluxes occurring from 0700 to 1000 h in summer daytime and fluxes from

1100 to 1600 h in fall. VanderZaag et al. (2014), measuring whole farm emissions before and after emptying the manure storage tank, also showed a clear diurnal pattern with above average emissions between 0900 to 1700 h. In our study, peak fluxes occurred early morn- ing in summer (0700 to 1000 h) and late morning (1100 h) in fall, which corresponded to sunrise and solar heating of the digestate surface causing bubble bursting and CH4 release. Diurnal cycles and peak related to sunrise in summer and fall have been reported in other studies on manure (Baldé et al., 2016b; VanderZaag et al., 2014). Baldé et al. (2016b) reported a diurnal peak after sunrise (0800 to 1000 h) in summer (Jun to Aug) and late morning (1000 h) in fall (Sep to Nov). In spring, fluxes consistently below the season aver- age occurred from 0000 to 0800 h and for the rest of the day no noticeable peak occurred. Winter fluxes were close to the average at all times of the day. The diurnal trend in summer showed emissions close to the mean value early in the morning (from 0400 to 0630 h), similar to the findings of Wood et al. (2013), for dairy manure, and in the afternoon (1430 to 1700h), suggesting that these would be the appropriate times for manual measurements of the fluxes, if necessary.

Jan 10) or outliers excluding the peaks related to specific events (e.g. frozen surface pattern Average season, emissions per 30-min were plotted to determine the diurnal emission tern (Figure that occurred only once during the period and affected the thawing in 2 times higher 2.6). A diurnal pattern was observed in summer and fall, with seasonal pat- per than nighttime fluxes occurring from 0700 to 1000 h in summer daytime and fluxes from

1100 to 1600 h in fall. VanderZaag et al. (2014), measuring whole farm emissions before and after emptying the manure storage tank, also showed a clear diurnal pattern with above average emissions between 0900 to 1700 h. In our study, peak fluxes occurred early morn- ing in summer (0700 to 1000 h) and late morning (1100 h) in fall, which corresponded to sunrise and solar heating of the digestate surface causing bubble bursting and CH4 release. Diurnal cycles and peak related to sunrise in summer and fall have been reported in other studies on manure (Baldé et al., 2016b; VanderZaag et al., 2014). Baldé et al. (2016b) reported a diurnal peak after sunrise (0800 to 1000 h) in summer (Jun to Aug) and late morning (1000 h) in fall (Sep to Nov). In spring, fluxes consistently below the season aver- age occurred from 0000 to 0800 h and for the rest of the day no noticeable peak occurred. Winter fluxes were close to the average at all times of the day. The diurnal trend in summer showed emissions close to the mean value early in the morning (from 0400 to 0630 h), similar to the findings of Wood et al. (2013), for dairy manure, and in the afternoon (1430 to 1700h), suggesting that these would be the appropriate times for manual measurements of the fluxes, if necessary.

Figure 2.6: Diurnal methane (CH4) emissions (g CH4 m-3 d-1) per season from digestate storage: winter (Jan 1 to Mar 31), spring (Apr 1 to Jun 30), summer (Jul 1 to Sep 30) and Fall (Oct 1 to Dec 31) in 2013. Vertical bars show the standard error of the mean. Numbers on the right show the season average flux and season average digestate temperature at the bottom of the tank in brackets.

The digestate added to the storage tank was much warmer (temperature in the biodi- gesters was 38 °C) than the untreated manure, and digestate storage temperature varied throughout the year playing a major role in driving CH4 emissions. Although average di- gestate temperatures at the bottom of the tank were similar in spring and fall (11.6 and 12.8° °C, respectively), the fall average flux was almost two times higher than the spring av- erage (3.15 0.83 g in fall and 1.73 0.53 g m-superscript(3) in spring), probably due to microbial communities increasing during summer and remaining active in fall (Figure 2.6). This observation is consistent with previous studies showing that longer retention times

at moderately warm temperatures lead to exponentially increasing CH4 emission rates (Le Riche et al., 2016). Similar to untreated manure storage, there was a hysteresis effect on the CH4 flux response to temperature, where the fluxes in fall (cooling) were much higher than in spring (warming), despite similar temperatures Kariyapperuma et al. (2017). When spring starts, the digestate volume is cool due to the cold winter months and the CH4 pro- duction activity is very low. In contrast, fall is preceded by a very warm season when the digestate volume is already warm and the CH4 production is active. The time for warming up or cooling down the bulk of digestate depends on the storage volume and conditions, SO that digestate temperature close to the bottom of the tank is a better predictor than air temperature. In fact, seasonal average air temperatures were much higher in spring than fall (11.6 and 1.4 C, respectively) contrasting the results for manure temperature. These results indicate that air temperature is not a good predictor of fluxes and that there is a strong influence of the precedent conditions.

Average daily emissions in summer were 5.8+1.0 g m-3 d-¹, slightly lower than but the same order of magnitude reported in Rodhe et al. (2015) and Clemens et al. (2006), 7.8 and 8.2 g m-3 d-¹ respectively, from pilot-scale storages with no solid-separation. The annual average CH4 emission from digestate of 2.7 0.5 g was 7 times lower than the 19 g m-3 d-¹ found in the year-long field study in a single digester biogas plant near Ottawa (Baldé et al., 2016b), likely due to the longer HRT and the second biodigester operating in series in our study site. Likewise, when scaled by VS, the annual average emission was 0.07 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹, four times lower than the 0.27 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹ in Baldé et al.

(2016b). Determining the annual total digestate emissions for the entire biogas plant requires some additional calculations and assumptions due to the multiple storages at the facility because emissions were measured only from the concrete tank (2,665 m³) Considering the annual biodigester loading rate of 12,600 m³ we can make the assumption that the annual total volume of digestate produced was approximately 11,000 m³ (after digestion and solid separation). Using the measured annual average digestate emission rate per volume of 2.7 g CH4 m³d-¹ we calculate an annual total digestate emission of 10.8 Mg y-1. By compar- ison, Baldé et al. (2016b) reported annual digestate emissions of 65 Mg y-1 at a biogas plant with a 1000 m³ reactor and 370 kW generator where manure was digested along with 48% off-farm materials with a 27-day hydraulic retention time and stored without solid- liquid separation in a large earthen storage (peak capacity 10,000 m³) with high quantities of residual sludge at the bottom and summer temperature > 20 °C for 3 months. In contrast, the biodigester in our study had less off-farm material added, the digester had a two-stage design with two reactors in series giving a 60-day overall retention time, solid-liquid sepa- ration after digestion, and the concrete digestate storage tank was purposefully emptied of as much sludge and residual organic material as possible prior to the study, thereby min- imizing methanogenic inoculum and reducing residual organic substrates in the storage. These findings from two biogas plants in Ontario, Canada show that biogas plant recipe (type and percentage of off-farm materials), biogas plant operation (hydraulic retention time and solid/liquid-separation), and digestate storage conditions (residual sludge, tem-
perature, and retention time) have a major impact on the CH4 emissions from the digestate storage.
20: <header>2.4.3 Methane fluxes from digestate and driving factors </header>

Several loading and emptying cycles occurred over 2013 (Figure 2.4a and b). From the beginning of the year until the end of May 30 (DOY 150) the temperature was low (< 10 °C) in the whole bulk of digestate (Figure 2.4a) and the fluxes were low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1 (Figure 2.4b). Through the winter until mid-Mar, flux peaks were noticed when the air temperature increased to positive values (e.g. DOY 10 (Jan 10), air temperature 0.5 °C; DOY 30 (Jan 30), 7.6 °C; DOY 69 (Mar 10), 4.7 °C; DOY 70 (Mar 11), 4.3 °C). At

that time, the surface was fully covered with ice and the increased air temperature caused thaw, likely releasing CH4 trapped below the ice cover, which is consistent with previous observations (VanderZaag et al., 2011). After the air temperature became positive in Apr, ice cover was only partial and these events did not occur. In May, the tank was partially emptied (DOY 129; May 9) and the air temperature started to increase, but the average CH4 flux was still low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1. In Jun, the tank was loaded again, but this time, due to higher air temperatures since May, the bulk of digestate was warmer (from a monthly average of 5.9 °C in Apr to 12.8 °C in May for the bottom of the tank). Throughout Jun the daily average CH4 fluxes increased to > 200 ug m-2 s-¹. From Jul to Oct the average monthly CH4 flux from digestate was 178 ug m-2 s-1 about 8 times higher than in May (monthly average 21 ug It is important to note that summer digestate tempera- tures were lower at this site (maximum monthly average at 50 cm above bottom of 19 °C; Table 2.3, Figure 2.5) in comparison to other studies on treated manure in Ontario. (Baldé et al., 2016a) reported temperatures above 19 °C from Jul to Sept with a peak monthly av- erage of 22 °C at a concrete tank with separated liquid dairy manure. Baldé et al. (2016b) reported peak temperature of 21 °C at an earthen storage of unseparated digestate with a thick surface crust. In our study, there was a thin crust throughout summer only and some bubbles on the digestate surface in summer and fall.

Digestate temperature started decreasing in Nov (from the monthly average of 16.8 °C in Oct to 11.8 °C in Nov at the bottom of the tank) when the tank was being emptied and air temperature was also decreasing (Figure 2.4a). The daily CH4 fluxes decreased to < 50

ug in Nov (Figure 2.4b). The tank was loaded again on Dec 3 (DOY 337), but the bulk of digestate remained < 10 °C and the few data points of flux measured this month were similar to the beginning of the year.

There was a significant (p < 0.05) positive correlation between CH4 fluxes and all tem- peratures within the digestate and air temperature (Table 2.4), with the strongest correlation found for the digestate temperature measured at 2 m depth (r = 0.98) with no evident lag (Figure 2.5). There was not a significant correlation at 0.05 level between VS concentration and CH4 fluxes from digestate (r = 0.37, Table 2.4).

Bars at the top show colour scale air temperature in 2013 and the average digestate temperature across all depths. (b) Daily methane (CH4) fluxes (ug CH4 m-2 -superscript(1)) pened when thermocouple sets were removed from the tank for agitation and emptying of and digestate volume (m³ in the storage tank in 2013. Vertical bars show the standard deviation for the daily mean.

(2016b) reported annual digestate emissions of 65 Mg y-1 at a biogas plant with a 1000 m³ reactor and 370 kW generator where manure was digested along with 48% off-farm materials with a 27-day hydraulic retention time and stored without solid- liquid separation in a large earthen storage (peak capacity 10,000 m³) with high quantities of residual sludge at the bottom and summer temperature > 20 °C for 3 months. In contrast, the biodigester in our study had less off-farm material added, the digester had a two-stage design with two reactors in series giving a 60-day overall retention time, solid-liquid sepa- ration after digestion, and the concrete digestate storage tank was purposefully emptied of as much sludge and residual organic material as possible prior to the study, thereby min- imizing methanogenic inoculum and reducing residual organic substrates in the storage. These findings from two biogas plants in Ontario, Canada show that biogas plant recipe (type and percentage of off-farm materials), biogas plant operation (hydraulic retention time and solid/liquid-separation), and digestate storage conditions (residual sludge, tem-
perature, and retention time) have a major impact on the CH4 emissions from the digestate storage.
22: <header>2.4.3 Methane fluxes from digestate and driving factors </header>

Several loading and emptying cycles occurred over 2013 (Figure 2.4a and b). From the beginning of the year until the end of May 30 (DOY 150) the temperature was low (< 10 °C) in the whole bulk of digestate (Figure 2.4a) and the fluxes were low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1 (Figure 2.4b). Through the winter until mid-Mar, flux peaks were noticed when the air temperature increased to positive values (e.g. DOY 10 (Jan 10), air temperature 0.5 °C; DOY 30 (Jan 30), 7.6 °C; DOY 69 (Mar 10), 4.7 °C; DOY 70 (Mar 11), 4.3 °C). At

that time, the surface was fully covered with ice and the increased air temperature caused thaw, likely releasing CH4 trapped below the ice cover, which is consistent with previous observations (VanderZaag et al., 2011). After the air temperature became positive in Apr, ice cover was only partial and these events did not occur. In May, the tank was partially emptied (DOY 129; May 9) and the air temperature started to increase, but the average CH4 flux was still low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1. In Jun, the tank was loaded again, but this time, due to higher air temperatures since May, the bulk of digestate was warmer (from a monthly average of 5.9 °C in Apr to 12.8 °C in May for the bottom of the tank). Throughout Jun the daily average CH4 fluxes increased to > 200 ug m-2 s-¹. From Jul to Oct the average monthly CH4 flux from digestate was 178 ug m-2 s-1 about 8 times higher than in May (monthly average 21 ug It is important to note that summer digestate tempera- tures were lower at this site (maximum monthly average at 50 cm above bottom of 19 °C; Table 2.3, Figure 2.5) in comparison to other studies on treated manure in Ontario. (Baldé et al., 2016a) reported temperatures above 19 °C from Jul to Sept with a peak monthly av- erage of 22 °C at a concrete tank with separated liquid dairy manure. Baldé et al. (2016b) reported peak temperature of 21 °C at an earthen storage of unseparated digestate with a thick surface crust. In our study, there was a thin crust throughout summer only and some bubbles on the digestate surface in summer and fall.

Digestate temperature started decreasing in Nov (from the monthly average of 16.8 °C in Oct to 11.8 °C in Nov at the bottom of the tank) when the tank was being emptied and air temperature was also decreasing (Figure 2.4a). The daily CH4 fluxes decreased to < 50

ug in Nov (Figure 2.4b). The tank was loaded again on Dec 3 (DOY 337), but the bulk of digestate remained < 10 °C and the few data points of flux measured this month were similar to the beginning of the year.

There was a significant (p < 0.05) positive correlation between CH4 fluxes and all tem- peratures within the digestate and air temperature (Table 2.4), with the strongest correlation found for the digestate temperature measured at 2 m depth (r = 0.98) with no evident lag (Figure 2.5). There was not a significant correlation at 0.05 level between VS concentration and CH4 fluxes from digestate (r = 0.37, Table 2.4).

Bars at the top show colour scale air temperature in 2013 and the average digestate temperature across all depths. (b) Daily methane (CH4) fluxes (ug CH4 m-2 -superscript(1)) pened when thermocouple sets were removed from the tank for agitation and emptying of and digestate volume (m³ in the storage tank in 2013. Jan 10) or outliers excluding the peaks related to specific events (e.g. frozen surface pattern Average season, emissions per 30-min were plotted to determine the diurnal emission tern (Figure that occurred only once during the period and affected the thawing in 2 times higher 2.6). A diurnal pattern was observed in summer and fall, with seasonal pat- per than nighttime fluxes occurring from 0700 to 1000 h in summer daytime and fluxes from

1100 to 1600 h in fall. VanderZaag et al. (2014), measuring whole farm emissions before and after emptying the manure storage tank, also showed a clear diurnal pattern with above average emissions between 0900 to 1700 h. In our study, peak fluxes occurred early morn- ing in summer (0700 to 1000 h) and late morning (1100 h) in fall, which corresponded to sunrise and solar heating of the digestate surface causing bubble bursting and CH4 release. Diurnal cycles and peak related to sunrise in summer and fall have been reported in other studies on manure (Baldé et al., 2016b; VanderZaag et al., 2014). Baldé et al. (2016b) reported a diurnal peak after sunrise (0800 to 1000 h) in summer (Jun to Aug) and late morning (1000 h) in fall (Sep to Nov). In spring, fluxes consistently below the season aver- age occurred from 0000 to 0800 h and for the rest of the day no noticeable peak occurred. Winter fluxes were close to the average at all times of the day. The diurnal trend in summer showed emissions close to the mean value early in the morning (from 0400 to 0630 h), similar to the findings of Wood et al. (2013), for dairy manure, and in the afternoon (1430 to 1700h), suggesting that these would be the appropriate times for manual measurements of the fluxes, if necessary.

Figure 2.6: Diurnal methane (CH4) emissions (g CH4 m-3 d-1) per season from digestate storage: winter (Jan 1 to Mar 31), spring (Apr 1 to Jun 30), summer (Jul 1 to Sep 30) and Fall (Oct 1 to Dec 31) in 2013. Vertical bars show the standard error of the mean. Numbers on the right show the season average flux and season average digestate temperature at the bottom of the tank in brackets.

The digestate added to the storage tank was much warmer (temperature in the biodi- gesters was 38 °C) than the untreated manure, and digestate storage temperature varied throughout the year playing a major role in driving CH4 emissions. Although average di- gestate temperatures at the bottom of the tank were similar in spring and fall (11.6 and 12.8° °C, respectively), the fall average flux was almost two times higher than the spring av- erage (3.15 0.83 g in fall and 1.73 0.53 g m-superscript(3) in spring), probably due to microbial communities increasing during summer and remaining active in fall (Figure 2.6). This observation is consistent with previous studies showing that longer retention times

at moderately warm temperatures lead to exponentially increasing CH4 emission rates (Le Riche et al., 2016). Similar to untreated manure storage, there was a hysteresis effect on the CH4 flux response to temperature, where the fluxes in fall (cooling) were much higher than in spring (warming), despite similar temperatures Kariyapperuma et al. (2017). When spring starts, the digestate volume is cool due to the cold winter months and the CH4 pro- duction activity is very low. In contrast, fall is preceded by a very warm season when the digestate volume is already warm and the CH4 production is active. The time for warming up or cooling down the bulk of digestate depends on the storage volume and conditions, SO that digestate temperature close to the bottom of the tank is a better predictor than air temperature. In fact, seasonal average air temperatures were much higher in spring than fall (11.6 and 1.4 C, respectively) contrasting the results for manure temperature. These results indicate that air temperature is not a good predictor of fluxes and that there is a strong influence of the precedent conditions.

Average daily emissions in summer were 5.8+1.0 g m-3 d-¹, slightly lower than but the same order of magnitude reported in Rodhe et al. (2015) and Clemens et al. (2006), 7.8 and 8.2 g m-3 d-¹ respectively, from pilot-scale storages with no solid-separation. The annual average CH4 emission from digestate of 2.7 0.5 g was 7 times lower than the 19 g m-3 d-¹ found in the year-long field study in a single digester biogas plant near Ottawa (Baldé et al., 2016b), likely due to the longer HRT and the second biodigester operating in series in our study site. Likewise, when scaled by VS, the annual average emission was 0.07 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹, four times lower than the 0.27 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹ in Baldé et al.

(2016b). Determining the annual total digestate emissions for the entire biogas plant requires some additional calculations and assumptions due to the multiple storages at the facility because emissions were measured only from the concrete tank (2,665 m³) Considering the annual biodigester loading rate of 12,600 m³ we can make the assumption that the annual total volume of digestate produced was approximately 11,000 m³ (after digestion and solid separation). Using the measured annual average digestate emission rate per volume of 2.7 g CH4 m³d-¹ we calculate an annual total digestate emission of 10.8 Mg y-1. By compar- ison, Baldé et al. (2016b) reported annual digestate emissions of 65 Mg y-1 at a biogas plant with a 1000 m³ reactor and 370 kW generator where manure was digested along with 48% off-farm materials with a 27-day hydraulic retention time and stored without solid- liquid separation in a large earthen storage (peak capacity 10,000 m³) with high quantities of residual sludge at the bottom and summer temperature > 20 °C for 3 months. In contrast, the biodigester in our study had less off-farm material added, the digester had a two-stage design with two reactors in series giving a 60-day overall retention time, solid-liquid sepa- ration after digestion, and the concrete digestate storage tank was purposefully emptied of as much sludge and residual organic material as possible prior to the study, thereby min- imizing methanogenic inoculum and reducing residual organic substrates in the storage. These findings from two biogas plants in Ontario, Canada show that biogas plant recipe (type and percentage of off-farm materials), biogas plant operation (hydraulic retention time and solid/liquid-separation), and digestate storage conditions (residual sludge, tem-
perature, and retention time) have a major impact on the CH4 emissions from the digestate storage.

A diurnal pattern was observed in summer and fall, with seasonal pat- per than nighttime fluxes occurring from 0700 to 1000 h in summer daytime and fluxes from

1100 to 1600 h in fall. VanderZaag et al. (2014), measuring whole farm emissions before and after emptying the manure storage tank, also showed a clear diurnal pattern with above average emissions between 0900 to 1700 h. In our study, peak fluxes occurred early morn- ing in summer (0700 to 1000 h) and late morning (1100 h) in fall, which corresponded to sunrise and solar heating of the digestate surface causing bubble bursting and CH4 release. Diurnal cycles and peak related to sunrise in summer and fall have been reported in other studies on manure (Baldé et al., 2016b; VanderZaag et al., 2014). Baldé et al. (2016b) reported a diurnal peak after sunrise (0800 to 1000 h) in summer (Jun to Aug) and late morning (1000 h) in fall (Sep to Nov). In spring, fluxes consistently below the season aver- age occurred from 0000 to 0800 h and for the rest of the day no noticeable peak occurred. Winter fluxes were close to the average at all times of the day. The diurnal trend in summer showed emissions close to the mean value early in the morning (from 0400 to 0630 h), similar to the findings of Wood et al. (2013), for dairy manure, and in the afternoon (1430 to 1700h), suggesting that these would be the appropriate times for manual measurements of the fluxes, if necessary.

Figure 2.6: Diurnal methane (CH4) emissions (g CH4 m-3 d-1) per season from digestate storage: winter (Jan 1 to Mar 31), spring (Apr 1 to Jun 30), summer (Jul 1 to Sep 30) and Fall (Oct 1 to Dec 31) in 2013. Vertical bars show the standard error of the mean. Numbers on the right show the season average flux and season average digestate temperature at the bottom of the tank in brackets.

The digestate added to the storage tank was much warmer (temperature in the biodi- gesters was 38 °C) than the untreated manure, and digestate storage temperature varied throughout the year playing a major role in driving CH4 emissions. Although average di- gestate temperatures at the bottom of the tank were similar in spring and fall (11.6 and 12.8° °C, respectively), the fall average flux was almost two times higher than the spring av- erage (3.15 0.83 g in fall and 1.73 0.53 g m-superscript(3) in spring), probably due to microbial communities increasing during summer and remaining active in fall (Figure 2.6). This observation is consistent with previous studies showing that longer retention times

at moderately warm temperatures lead to exponentially increasing CH4 emission rates (Le Riche et al., 2016). Similar to untreated manure storage, there was a hysteresis effect on the CH4 flux response to temperature, where the fluxes in fall (cooling) were much higher than in spring (warming), despite similar temperatures Kariyapperuma et al. (2017). When spring starts, the digestate volume is cool due to the cold winter months and the CH4 pro- duction activity is very low. In contrast, fall is preceded by a very warm season when the digestate volume is already warm and the CH4 production is active. The time for warming up or cooling down the bulk of digestate depends on the storage volume and conditions, SO that digestate temperature close to the bottom of the tank is a better predictor than air temperature. In fact, seasonal average air temperatures were much higher in spring than fall (11.6 and 1.4 C, respectively) contrasting the results for manure temperature. These results indicate that air temperature is not a good predictor of fluxes and that there is a strong influence of the precedent conditions.

Average daily emissions in summer were 5.8+1.0 g m-3 d-¹, slightly lower than but the same order of magnitude reported in Rodhe et al. (2015) and Clemens et al. (2006), 7.8 and 8.2 g m-3 d-¹ respectively, from pilot-scale storages with no solid-separation. The annual average CH4 emission from digestate of 2.7 0.5 g was 7 times lower than the 19 g m-3 d-¹ found in the year-long field study in a single digester biogas plant near Ottawa (Baldé et al., 2016b), likely due to the longer HRT and the second biodigester operating in series in our study site. Likewise, when scaled by VS, the annual average emission was 0.07 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹, four times lower than the 0.27 g CH4 kg-1 VS d-¹ in Baldé et al.

(2016b). Determining the annual total digestate emissions for the entire biogas plant requires some additional calculations and assumptions due to the multiple storages at the facility because emissions were measured only from the concrete tank (2,665 m³) Considering the annual biodigester loading rate of 12,600 m³ we can make the assumption that the annual total volume of digestate produced was approximately 11,000 m³ (after digestion and solid separation). Using the measured annual average digestate emission rate per volume of 2.7 g CH4 m³d-¹ we calculate an annual total digestate emission of 10.8 Mg y-1. By compar- ison, Baldé et al. (2016b) reported annual digestate emissions of 65 Mg y-1 at a biogas plant with a 1000 m³ reactor and 370 kW generator where manure was digested along with 48% off-farm materials with a 27-day hydraulic retention time and stored without solid- liquid separation in a large earthen storage (peak capacity 10,000 m³) with high quantities of residual sludge at the bottom and summer temperature > 20 °C for 3 months. In contrast, the biodigester in our study had less off-farm material added, the digester had a two-stage design with two reactors in series giving a 60-day overall retention time, solid-liquid sepa- ration after digestion, and the concrete digestate storage tank was purposefully emptied of as much sludge and residual organic material as possible prior to the study, thereby min- imizing methanogenic inoculum and reducing residual organic substrates in the storage. These findings from two biogas plants in Ontario, Canada show that biogas plant recipe (type and percentage of off-farm materials), biogas plant operation (hydraulic retention time and solid/liquid-separation), and digestate storage conditions (residual sludge, tem-
perature, and retention time) have a major impact on the CH4 emissions from the digestate storage.
25: <header>2.4.3 Methane fluxes from digestate and driving factors </header>

Several loading and emptying cycles occurred over 2013 (Figure 2.4a and b). From the beginning of the year until the end of May 30 (DOY 150) the temperature was low (< 10 °C) in the whole bulk of digestate (Figure 2.4a) and the fluxes were low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1 (Figure 2.4b). Through the winter until mid-Mar, flux peaks were noticed when the air temperature increased to positive values (e.g. DOY 10 (Jan 10), air temperature 0.5 °C; DOY 30 (Jan 30), 7.6 °C; DOY 69 (Mar 10), 4.7 °C; DOY 70 (Mar 11), 4.3 °C). At

that time, the surface was fully covered with ice and the increased air temperature caused thaw, likely releasing CH4 trapped below the ice cover, which is consistent with previous observations (VanderZaag et al., 2011). After the air temperature became positive in Apr, ice cover was only partial and these events did not occur. In May, the tank was partially emptied (DOY 129; May 9) and the air temperature started to increase, but the average CH4 flux was still low (< 50 ug m-2 s-1. In Jun, the tank was loaded again, but this time, due to higher air temperatures since May, the bulk of digestate was warmer (from a monthly average of 5.9 °C in Apr to 12.8 °C in May for the bottom of the tank). Throughout Jun the daily average CH4 fluxes increased to > 200 ug m-2 s-¹. From Jul to Oct the average monthly CH4 flux from digestate was 178 ug m-2 s-1 about 8 times higher than in May (monthly average 21 ug It is important to note that summer digestate tempera- tures were lower at this site (maximum monthly average at 50 cm above bottom of 19 °C; Table 2.3, Figure 2.5) in comparison to other studies on treated manure in Ontario. (Baldé et al., 2016a) reported temperatures above 19 °C from Jul to Sept with a peak monthly av- erage of 22 °C at a concrete tank with separated liquid dairy manure. Baldé et al. (2016b) reported peak temperature of 21 °C at an earthen storage of unseparated digestate with a thick surface crust. In our study, there was a thin crust throughout summer only and some bubbles on the digestate surface in summer and fall.

Digestate temperature started decreasing in Nov (from the monthly average of 16.8 °C in Oct to 11.8 °C in Nov at the bottom of the tank) when the tank was being emptied and air temperature was also decreasing (Figure 2.4a). The daily CH4 fluxes decreased to < 50

ug in Nov (Figure 2.4b). The tank was loaded again on Dec 3 (DOY 337), but the bulk of digestate remained < 10 °C and the few data points of flux measured this month were similar to the beginning of the year.

There was a significant (p < 0.05) positive correlation between CH4 fluxes and all tem- peratures within the digestate and air temperature (Table 2.4), with the strongest correlation found for the digestate temperature measured at 2 m depth (r = 0.98) with no evident lag (Figure 2.5). There was not a significant correlation at 0.05 level between VS concentration and CH4 fluxes from digestate (r = 0.37, Table 2.4).

26: <header>2.4.4 Digestate and untreated manure emissions comparison </header>

The monthly average VS mass in the tank was 73+24 Mg for digestate (2013 year) and 107+30 Mg for manure (2010-2011 year), representing a 32% VS reduction in the tank. The total annual CH4 emission per volume from digestate was 1.0 kg m-3-1, which represents an 85% emission reduction when compared with the total annual CH4 emis- sion per volume from the untreated manure at the same farm (6.6 kg m-3y-1). This result suggests that the reduced emission from the digestate tank is not only due to the VS re- moval, but also due to a less digestible form of VS in the digestate. This finding is con- sistent with VanderZaag et al. (2017) study to assess the CH4 emission potential reduction of two manure treatments. VanderZaag et al. (2017) reported 35% VS content reduction in digestate compared to the untreated manure, an additional 32% reduction in the CH4 emission potential per VS and a slower emission rate in digestate, indicating a less degrad- able form of VS. Other possible contributing factors include the fact that the tank was completely emptied immediately prior to the digestate emission measurements, thereby re- moving methanogenic inoculum and residual accumulated sludge; in comparison, this type of intentional removal was not conducted prior to the manure emission measurements in 2010-2011.
The total monthly CH4 emissions scaled by VS were higher for the untreated manure

in all months except for Jun, when the digestate emissions were slightly higher due to the 3 times higher temperature at the bottom of the digestate tank (Figure 2.7). Overall, the annual CH4 emissions per VS were almost 65% lower for the digestate compared to the untreated manure. When digestate and manure had similar temperatures (Jan-Mar, Aug and Sep), the VS mass in the manure tank was higher than in the digestate tank but scaling of fluxes per VS indicated higher emissions from manure. In April, when the VS mass in storage and temperature at the bottom of the tank were similar for digestate and manure, CH4 emission per VS was 2.3 times lower from the digestate. These observations suggest the VS form in the digestate is less capable of driving CH4 emissions, therefore, not a suitable CH4 flux predictor as it is for manure. The monthly average CH4 emission per VS varied less throughout the year for digestate (annual average of 2.1+1.6 g CH4 kg-1 VS mth-1 compared to manure (6.3+6.2 CH4 kg-1 VS mth-1), probably also due to a more stable form of VS in the digestate, less affected by seasonal temperature variation in the system (Figure 2.7).

Figure 2.7: Monthly average methane (CH4) fluxes (g CH4 kg-1 VS month-1 from diges- tate and manure, monthly average digestate/manure temperature (C) at 50 cm above the bottom of the tank and average volatile solids (VS) mass (kg) in the tank per month in 2013 and and in the manure year.

The total annual CH4 emissions scaled by VS were 26 g CH4 kg-1 VS y-1 for digestate and 76 g CH4 kg-1 VS y-1 for manure. This result indicates that the emission reduction in the digestate is not only due to the removal of VS in the digesters, but suggests that the form of VS in the digestate was not as "digestible" as the VS in manure. In addition, there are likely other factors related to biodigester and digestate storage management that inhibit or enhance CH4 production.
The relationship between CH4 emissions and the emission drivers (temperature and VS)

for digestate was different for manure (Table 2.4). The VS concentration, which did not show statistically significant relationship with digestate emission, was strongly correlated with emission from manure (r = 0.96, p <0.002). The temperature at all depths and air tem- perature showed, on the other hand, stronger relationships with emissions from digestate than from manure (Table 2.4).

These observations suggest the VS form in the digestate is less capable of driving CH4 emissions, therefore, not a suitable CH4 flux predictor as it is for manure. The monthly average CH4 emission per VS varied less throughout the year for digestate (annual average of 2.1+1.6 g CH4 kg-1 VS mth-1 compared to manure (6.3+6.2 CH4 kg-1 VS mth-1), probably also due to a more stable form of VS in the digestate, less affected by seasonal temperature variation in the system (Figure 2.7).

Figure 2.7: Monthly average methane (CH4) fluxes (g CH4 kg-1 VS month-1 from diges- tate and manure, monthly average digestate/manure temperature (C) at 50 cm above the bottom of the tank and average volatile solids (VS) mass (kg) in the tank per month in 2013 and and in the manure year.

The total annual CH4 emissions scaled by VS were 26 g CH4 kg-1 VS y-1 for digestate and 76 g CH4 kg-1 VS y-1 for manure. This result indicates that the emission reduction in the digestate is not only due to the removal of VS in the digesters, but suggests that the form of VS in the digestate was not as "digestible" as the VS in manure. In addition, there are likely other factors related to biodigester and digestate storage management that inhibit or enhance CH4 production.
The relationship between CH4 emissions and the emission drivers (temperature and VS)

for digestate was different for manure (Table 2.4). The VS concentration, which did not show statistically significant relationship with digestate emission, was strongly correlated with emission from manure (r = 0.96, p <0.002). The temperature at all depths and air tem- perature showed, on the other hand, stronger relationships with emissions from digestate than from manure (Table 2.4).
30: <header>2.4.4 Digestate and untreated manure emissions comparison </header>

The monthly average VS mass in the tank was 73+24 Mg for digestate (2013 year) and 107+30 Mg for manure (2010-2011 year), representing a 32% VS reduction in the tank. The total annual CH4 emission per volume from digestate was 1.0 kg m-3-1, which represents an 85% emission reduction when compared with the total annual CH4 emis- sion per volume from the untreated manure at the same farm (6.6 kg m-3y-1). This result suggests that the reduced emission from the digestate tank is not only due to the VS re- moval, but also due to a less digestible form of VS in the digestate. This finding is con- sistent with VanderZaag et al. (2017) study to assess the CH4 emission potential reduction of two manure treatments. VanderZaag et al. (2017) reported 35% VS content reduction in digestate compared to the untreated manure, an additional 32% reduction in the CH4 emission potential per VS and a slower emission rate in digestate, indicating a less degrad- able form of VS. Other possible contributing factors include the fact that the tank was completely emptied immediately prior to the digestate emission measurements, thereby re- moving methanogenic inoculum and residual accumulated sludge; in comparison, this type of intentional removal was not conducted prior to the manure emission measurements in 2010-2011.
The total monthly CH4 emissions scaled by VS were higher for the untreated manure

in all months except for Jun, when the digestate emissions were slightly higher due to the 3 times higher temperature at the bottom of the digestate tank (Figure 2.7). Overall, the annual CH4 emissions per VS were almost 65% lower for the digestate compared to the untreated manure. When digestate and manure had similar temperatures (Jan-Mar, Aug and Sep), the VS mass in the manure tank was higher than in the digestate tank but scaling of fluxes per VS indicated higher emissions from manure. In April, when the VS mass in storage and temperature at the bottom of the tank were similar for digestate and manure, CH4 emission per VS was 2.3 times lower from the digestate. These observations suggest the VS form in the digestate is less capable of driving CH4 emissions, therefore, not a suitable CH4 flux predictor as it is for manure. The monthly average CH4 emission per VS varied less throughout the year for digestate (annual average of 2.1+1.6 g CH4 kg-1 VS mth-1 compared to manure (6.3+6.2 CH4 kg-1 VS mth-1), probably also due to a more stable form of VS in the digestate, less affected by seasonal temperature variation in the system (Figure 2.7).

for digestate was different for manure (Table 2.4). 31: Monthly digestate temperature at 0.5 m from the bottom of the manure/digestate storage tank ranged from 5.6 to 19.0 °C and was significantly correlated to CH4 emissions with no lag. Despite the correlation, digestate monthly temperature alone was not a good predictor of the monthly CH4 emissions during the spring and fall. The precedent temperature (if the bulk of digestate was warming up or cooling down) had an effect on the CH4 emissions, be- ing higher in fall (after summer) than in spring (after winter) despite a similar temperature range (hysteresis).

There was not a significant relationship between CH4 emissions from digestate and the mass of VS in storage, whereas there was a significant relationship for manure. There was a 32% reduction of VS in the digestate, but a 65% reduction in CH4 emission per mass of VS compared to manure. The CH4 emission by volume was even lower, reducing emissions by 83% of the manure CH4 emissions. This indicates that there were other factors beside the VS removal that inhibited CH4 emissions in the digestate, likely the type of VS is less
degradable and care should be taken if using VS as an input (predictor) of CH4 emissions from storages in existing models.

Our study showed that a 60-day hydraulic retention time for AD of dairy manure with 35% off-farm materials significantly reduced CH4 emission. However, the AD recipe was mainly manure and simple carbohydrates (onions, bananas, sugar water) and easily di- gestible chicken meat wastes throughout most of the year. A change in the composition and/or loading rate of the off-farm materials might mean very different results. Further research with off-farm materials richer in fats and proteins must be conducted.
Addition of off-farm materials to manure increases methane emissions from digestate storage at a dairy biogas facility

33: Manure storage is an important agricultural greenhouse gas (GHG) emission source. This is primarily due to methane (CH4) production in the anaerobic conditions developed in storage tanks. CH4 is a potent GHG with global warming potential 28 times higher than carbon dioxide (Myhre et al., 2013). Anaerobic digestion (AD) of manure has the potential

to significantly reduce CH4 emissions from the slurry storage, reducing its environmental impact and contribution to global warming (Wang, 2014). However, there are a number of aspects that could affect and compromise the environmental benefits from on-farm AD systems.

On-farm AD systems often use off-farm materials as co-substrates to enhance biogas and energy production, making the system feasible and profitable. The addition of sub- stances that increase CH4 production inside the biodigesters could, depending on the sys- tem design and activities, also increase CH4 emissions. A pilot scale study in Sweden (Rodhe et al., 2015) comparing CH4 emissions from untreated manure and digestate found equivalent or even higher CH4 emissions per volume from the latter. Other studies have shown that addition of some off-farm material still results in lower CH4 emissions per vol- ume from storage when compared to untreated manure (Chapter 2; Amon et al. (2006); Clemens et al. (2006); Holly et al. (2017)). However, studies agree that, due to the com- plexity of the off-farm material alternatives, more research is needed.

There are limited emission studies from digestate storage at the field scale and most of the available data were measured for a short period of time (few days or months). To date, only two studies measured year-long CH4 emissions at the farm-scale (Chapter 2; Baldé et al. (2016b)) using micrometeorological methods. These micrometeorological methods are the most appropriate to capture on-farm emissions when the physical conditions of the research site are suitable for its use. The micrometeorological mass balance method (MMB), also known as integrated horizontal flux method, is the most suited to capture gas
emissions from point sources or small-scale area sources, like a digestate storage.

In a previous study (Chapter 2) CH4 emissions from a digestate storage were mea- sured during the first year of operation of a biogas facility and compared the results with untreated manure storage emissions from the same farm before the biodigester system be- came operational. The results showed that AD significantly reduced CH4 emissions with total annual storage emissions 85% lower than before the biodigestion treatment. In that first year, the biogas system did not produce enough biogas to run the electrical generator at full power and generate sufficient revenue to offset the system investment costs to build and operate the facility. To increase the facility's revenue, the electricity production needed to be increased and sold to the local utility provider. To achieve this goal, the AD recipe was adjusted by increasing the percentage and quality of the industrial food waste (IFW) used as co-substrates. By doing this, not only was the biogas production higher, but there was a monetary "tipping" fee added to the facility's revenue stream.

To characterize the impact of recipe change on residual emissions from the digestate storage, CH4 emissions were measured for two additional years (2014-2015). Samples from the system inputs and throughout the AD process were collected several times during the study period to establish the relationship between input/output compositions and emis- sions. The results were compared to the first year of AD system operation (2013) as well as to the untreated manure emissions at the same farm (2010-2011).
The objectives of this study were to:
1. quantify CH4 emissions from digestate derived from the anaerobic digestion of dairy
manure with ~40% in volume of off-farm materials (industrial food waste);

<list>2. relate the emissions to emissions drivers; 
3. compare the results with the first year of the system operation and determine the impact of the change in recipe; and 
4. compare the operational digestate storage impact to untreated manure storage at the same farm before the biogas system became operational.</list>

38: <header>3.2.1 Site description and biogas system operation </header>

Mesurements were conducted at the Clovermead Farm is located near Alma, Ontario, Canada, (43°45'N 80°40'W). In 2014-2015, the dairy herd was composed of 140 lactating cows, 15 dry cows, 100 heifers and 22 calves, in 4 different barns (Figure 2.1). Liquid and solid manure respectively from the lactating cows (main barn) and heifers (bank barn) were input into the biodigesters along with off-farm materials. Solid manure from dry cows and calves were composted off site. The average milk production was 4000 L d-¹ (28 L cow-1 d-¹), with milk fat and protein 3.83 and 3.35%, respectively.

There were manure storage tanks under the barn (~ 3,000 m³ capacity) (Chapter 2) and an outdoor storage area (~ 770 m³) at the back of the bank barn to store solid manure. The pre-treatment consisted of a 100 m³ capacity hydrolysis tank, continuously mixed and

heated at 50 °C where off-farm materials were held on average for 2.5 days before being mixed with manure in the mixer tank (20 m³) and a 0.5 m³ pasteurizer tank that kept the mixture at 70 °C for one hour before input into the primary biodigester. There were two identical biodigesters (1,102 m³ working volume each) working in series, with 30-day hydraulic retention time each. There was a solid-liquid separator building, with a screw- press to separate solids (used as barn bedding) and liquid digestate, pumped into an open storage tank (2,665 m³ capacity) (Figure 2.1).

The biogas was directed to a 250 kW co-generation unit producing heat, used to heat the system (all liquid input tanks and all farm buildings) and electricity, connected to the local grid (sold to the electricity provider). The CH4 content in the biogas produced was on average 57% and the reported electrical efficiency was 43%.

The biodigesters started being filled in May 2012 with manure only, but off-farm ma- terials started being incorporated into the system by the end of that year. Throughout the following years, the recipe changed in proportion and quality of off-farm materials (Table 3.1). As a desired result, biogas production increased from 50 to 80 m³ h in the first year, to 160 m³ h-superscript(1) in 2014-2015. The average energy production grew from 1600 kWh d-¹ in 2013 to 5220 kWh d-¹ in 2015.

In 2014-2015, the dairy herd was composed of 140 lactating cows, 15 dry cows, 100 heifers and 22 calves, in 4 different barns (Figure 2.1). Solid manure from dry cows and calves were composted off site. The average milk production was 4000 L d-¹ (28 L cow-1 d-¹), with milk fat and protein 3.83 and 3.35%, respectively.

There were manure storage tanks under the barn (~ 3,000 m³ capacity) (Chapter 2) and an outdoor storage area (~ 770 m³) at the back of the bank barn to store solid manure. The pre-treatment consisted of a 100 m³ capacity hydrolysis tank, continuously mixed and

heated at 50 °C where off-farm materials were held on average for 2.5 days before being mixed with manure in the mixer tank (20 m³) and a 0.5 m³ pasteurizer tank that kept the mixture at 70 °C for one hour before input into the primary biodigester. There were two identical biodigesters (1,102 m³ working volume each) working in series, with 30-day hydraulic retention time each. There was a solid-liquid separator building, with a screw- press to separate solids (used as barn bedding) and liquid digestate, pumped into an open storage tank (2,665 m³ capacity) (Figure 2.1).

The biogas was directed to a 250 kW co-generation unit producing heat, used to heat the system (all liquid input tanks and all farm buildings) and electricity, connected to the local grid (sold to the electricity provider). The CH4 content in the biogas produced was on average 57% and the reported electrical efficiency was 43%.

The biodigesters started being filled in May 2012 with manure only, but off-farm ma- terials started being incorporated into the system by the end of that year. Throughout the following years, the recipe changed in proportion and quality of off-farm materials (Table 3.1). As a desired result, biogas production increased from 50 to 80 m³ h in the first year, to 160 m³ h-superscript(1) in 2014-2015. The average energy production grew from 1600 kWh d-¹ in 2013 to 5220 kWh d-¹ in 2015.
