The seasonal emissions in our studies were at least 45% lower in all seasons, except in spring when the average CH4 emission (25 g m-3 d-¹ was 2x the spring average (13 g m-3 d-¹ reported in Baldé et al. (2016b). The monthly average digestate temperatures were similar in both sites, but while the volume of digestate stored was high throughout the spring at the Ottawa's biogas facility, there were peaks caused by tank agitation and emptying in our study site.

The total annual cumulative CH4 emissions were similar for digestate in the operational years and untreated manure, 8.9 and 9.6 Mg CH4 y-1, respectively, but the emissions OC- curred much earlier for digestate storage (Figure 3.4). By the middle of 2014-2015 (DOY
Figure 3.4: Cumulative methane (CH4) emission (Mg CH4) in the manure year (2010- 2011), 2013 (start up phase) and 2014-2015 (operational phase).
180), the cumulative CH4 emission had already reached the total annual in 2013 (1.8 Mg CH4) and increased more than double of this value in the second half of the year.

The average monthly CH4 emission scaled by the VS mass were 18.2+13.0 kg-1 VS month-1 in 2014-2015, much higher than from the digestate in 2013 (2.1+1.6 g kg-1 VS month-1 and even higher than the untreated manure in 2010-2011 (6.3+6.2 g kg-1 VS month-1. So, even though there was a significant VS reduction in the AD, the VS in the digestate from the operational years was more reactive in the storage tank, emitting more CH4 per mass unit. This observation is likely related to a more active microbial community in the operational digestate or to a presence of easily digestible VS. The increased digestate temperature also suggests that there might be some microbial differentiation in the opera-
tional year digestate. Analysis of the microbiota present in the digestate storage tank in the start up and operational phases would be desired to understand the observed phenomenon.

The CH4 emission scaled by the VS mass in the operational year (0.6 g kg-1 VS d-¹ was higher than the values found in previous studies (0.21 and 0.27 g kg-1 VS d-1, Rodhe et al. (2015) and Baldé et al. (2016b), respectively). Considering the poor correlation be- tween emissions from digestate and VS content, this parameter should not be used to pre- dict emissions from digestate storage, in contrast to emissions from manure. This finding is relevant, because in the absence of an emission model to predict CH4 emission specifically from digestate, emission models for manure based on VS mass have been used in LCA studies (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017; Ebner et al., 2015).

The storage tank emitted 2.87 and 4.98 g kWh-Superscript(1) in the start-up and operational years, respectively, which corresponded to 1 and 2% of the utilized CH4. These values were within the range, but close to its lower end, of residual emissions from the storage of digestate in 10 biogas plants: 0.22 to 11.2% Liebetrau et al. (2013).

The two AD tanks in sequence reduce the risk of incompletely digested material short circuiting the system and passing through in less than the 60 d HRT. The high CH4 emis- sion per VS might indicate that with the addition of IFW, a longer retention time is required to achieve the same digestion degree of VS observed in 2013. The increased CH4 emission potential in digestate compared to untreated manure suggests that IFW addition had a neg- ative effect in terms of reducing emissions from storage. However, to compare the overall impact of the AD, the alternative disposal of IFW (avoided emissions) has to be included
62: The depth of digestate (100% = 3.66 m) in the storage varied throughout 2014 from 17 to 99% of the total storage capacity and from 4 to 95% in 2015 (Figure 3.1c). In 2013, the digestate depth also varied throughout the year (from 27 to 96%). In the beginning of 2014, the tank was being loaded and the surface was almost fully ice covered, with a small opening close to the inlet loading pipe. An overall loading period with few emptying events happened until Apr 14 (Day of year, DOY 104), when the tank was almost completely full and all snow covering the surface was gone. During this period in 2014, the digestate

temperature at the bottom of the tank was above 8 °C, >4 °C higher than the same period in 2013 (Figure 3.1b), despite similar air temperatures (Figure 3.1a). The CH4 fluxes were also much higher than the same period in the previous year. There was a 20-day period until May 4, 2014 (DOY 124) when the tank volume stayed at its highest capacity, followed by a period of emptying until its lowest level was reached on Jun 3, 2014 (DOY 154). The remaining digestate in the tank was thick, a crust was formed at the surface and remained in the tank with some cracks and bubbling events observed until early Aug 8, 2014 (DOY 220). On Aug 1, 2014 (DOY 213) there was an unusual addition of beef processing sludge co-substrate that had an almost immediate impact on the biogas and energy production (Table 3.1) and, later, on the emissions from the digestate storage tank. The digestate released in mid-Sep (DOY 252) was foamy, probably due to the unusual large load of beef sludge in Aug, 2014. The beef sludge became part of the regular feed recipe along with a larger amount of chicken meat waste, keeping the high-energy production throughout the rest of the year and the next year, 2015. An extended black thick crust with foamy liquid spaced in the cracks was formed in Sep 23 (DOY 266) and present until Oct 4, 2014 (DOY 277). The thick crust dissipated with agitation, loading and emptying events and only some foam was present on the surface until the end of the year when the tank was emptied considerably.

In the beginning of 2015 the tank was also loaded, with one emptying event in mid-Feb (DOY 43) and another in mid-Mar (DOY 78). During the winter (Jan 1, DOY 1 to Mar 31, DOY 90), the digestate surface had continuous snow/ice cover. The CH4 flux measurements started in mid-Mar, 2015 (DOY 71), when the tank was full and being agitated before pumping. There was still some snow cover but most of the digestate was exposed with no crust at this time. Throughout the emptying period the digestate was constantly being agitated and some high fluxes for this period (Mar to May) were measured. During this period, there was always some floating material over the digestate surface with a foamy, viscous crust along the outer perimeter of the tank until a thick hard crust over the entire surface formed by the end of the period, when the digestate level was low. By Jun 1, 2015 (DOY152) the tank reached its lowest depth, with only 0.16 m (4% full, 119 m³) of digestate remaining, followed by a long period of loading an average rate of 25 m³ d-¹ until Sep. During the loading period, a soft crust started to form over most part of the surface with some bubbling events during the daytime. By Jul 27, 2015 (DOY 208), a black solid crust was covering the whole surface. The crust dissipated in mid-Aug (DOY 226), but by Aug 28 (DOY 240) a 100% black crust cover had formed again. After the agitation and pumping events, on Sep 9 (DOY 252) and Sep 25 (DOY 268) the crust had dissipated completely. After that, bubbling events and some crust formation were observed. From mid-Oct (DOY 289) to mid-Nov (DOY 319), there was another period of regular loading with crust formation followed by a significant emptying event.

The digestate temperature at the bottom of the tank was always much higher (average of 2 °C higher) in 2014 and 2015 as compared to 2013, except for Nov 26 (DOY 330), when temperature was lower in 2014 (Table 3.2). Despite similar monthly average air tem- peratures for all three years, the digestate temperature at 2 m depth and at 0.50 m from the bottom were significantly (p < 0.05) higher for 2014-2015 when compared to 2013 (Table 3.2). An increased storage temperature with AD was also found by Hansen et al. (2006). There was not enough information regarding to changes in the microbiota, digestate char- acteristics or system operation, though, to explain why this temperature increase happened. It may be related to microbiota activity in the tank or some insulation effect caused by the crust cover present most of 2014-2015 and not as frequent in 2013, since the AD and air temperatures were similar for all years. The temperature in the digesters was kept at 38 °C since the first year, but a change in the way or speed the digestate was transferred from digester to storage might have contributed to the increased storage temperature in the oper- ational years (e.g. skipping the solid-separation more often). Unfortunately, there were no reliable records to confirm this hypothesis.

Figure 3.1: (a) Air temperature (C), (b) digestate temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank, (c) daily methane (CH4) fluxes (ug CH4 m 2 s-1 and digestate depth (cm) in the storage tank in 2013, 2014 and 2015. Vertical bars show the standard deviation for the daily mean CH4 flux.
Table 3.2: Monthly average air temperature (C) and digestate temperature (C) in 2013 and 2014-2015 for various depths.

There was not a significant correlation between CH4 fluxes per volume and VS concen- trations in the digestate tank, as also observed in 2013 (Chapter 2). The cross-correlation coefficients (r) between monthly average CH4 fluxes per volume and the measured tem- peratures for 2014 and 2015 were significant for air temperature (r = 0.91) and digestate temperatures at 0.05, 1, and 2 m depths and at 0.50 m above the bottom (r = 0.95) with no lag observed in all cases. The flux response increase to a higher digestate temperature was more prominent in the operational years than in 2013, but still lower than of the untreated manure (Figure 3.2).

Figure 3.2: Monthly averages of methane (CH4) fluxes scaled by volume (g CH4 m -3 d-¹ versus digestate temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank for manure (2010- 2011), digestate in 2013, 2014, 2015 and the combination of 2014/15 data, with respective linear fit.

The monthly VS concentrations in the digestate tank in 2014-2015 were similar to val- ues throughout 2013 (Figure 3.3), with the monthly averages not significantly different (Table 3.3). On the other hand, the CH4 fluxes were higher in 2014-2015 likely due to the much higher digestate temperature. However, the increased CH4 emissions in 2014-2015 were lower than emissions from the manure (2010-2011) in winter, likely due to its higher VS concentration (Figure 3.3). The spring 2014-2015 CH4 emissions were significantly higher than previously measured for that period at this farm for manure (2010-2011) or

digestate (2013). The rapid increase in CH4 emissions in May was due to a combination of frequent tank agitation and pumping from late April and the fast increase in the digestate temperature during the long tank loading period after almost complete emptying. Although the VS concentrations in the tank did not vary much during this period in 2014-2015, diges- tate temperature was higher than in spring 2010-2011 (manure year, Figure 3.3). From Aug on, manure in storage during 2010-2011 warmed up, even though it never reached temper- atures as high as in 2014-2015. Because of the much higher VS concentration, CH4 fluxes from manure were much higher (more than double) than from digestate in 2014-2015 until the rest of the year, with the highest monthly peak emission occurring in Sep 2010-2011 from manure.

The high CH4 emis- sion per VS might indicate that with the addition of IFW, a longer retention time is required to achieve the same digestion degree of VS observed in 2013. The increased CH4 emission potential in digestate compared to untreated manure suggests that IFW addition had a neg- ative effect in terms of reducing emissions from storage. However, to compare the overall impact of the AD, the alternative disposal of IFW (avoided emissions) has to be included
64: The depth of digestate (100% = 3.66 m) in the storage varied throughout 2014 from 17 to 99% of the total storage capacity and from 4 to 95% in 2015 (Figure 3.1c). In 2013, the digestate depth also varied throughout the year (from 27 to 96%). In the beginning of 2014, the tank was being loaded and the surface was almost fully ice covered, with a small opening close to the inlet loading pipe. An overall loading period with few emptying events happened until Apr 14 (Day of year, DOY 104), when the tank was almost completely full and all snow covering the surface was gone. During this period in 2014, the digestate

temperature at the bottom of the tank was above 8 °C, >4 °C higher than the same period in 2013 (Figure 3.1b), despite similar air temperatures (Figure 3.1a). The CH4 fluxes were also much higher than the same period in the previous year. There was a 20-day period until May 4, 2014 (DOY 124) when the tank volume stayed at its highest capacity, followed by a period of emptying until its lowest level was reached on Jun 3, 2014 (DOY 154). The remaining digestate in the tank was thick, a crust was formed at the surface and remained in the tank with some cracks and bubbling events observed until early Aug 8, 2014 (DOY 220). On Aug 1, 2014 (DOY 213) there was an unusual addition of beef processing sludge co-substrate that had an almost immediate impact on the biogas and energy production (Table 3.1) and, later, on the emissions from the digestate storage tank. The digestate released in mid-Sep (DOY 252) was foamy, probably due to the unusual large load of beef sludge in Aug, 2014. The beef sludge became part of the regular feed recipe along with a larger amount of chicken meat waste, keeping the high-energy production throughout the rest of the year and the next year, 2015. An extended black thick crust with foamy liquid spaced in the cracks was formed in Sep 23 (DOY 266) and present until Oct 4, 2014 (DOY 277). The thick crust dissipated with agitation, loading and emptying events and only some foam was present on the surface until the end of the year when the tank was emptied considerably.

In the beginning of 2015 the tank was also loaded, with one emptying event in mid-Feb (DOY 43) and another in mid-Mar (DOY 78). During the winter (Jan 1, DOY 1 to Mar 31, DOY 90), the digestate surface had continuous snow/ice cover. The CH4 flux measurements started in mid-Mar, 2015 (DOY 71), when the tank was full and being agitated before pumping. There was still some snow cover but most of the digestate was exposed with no crust at this time. Throughout the emptying period the digestate was constantly being agitated and some high fluxes for this period (Mar to May) were measured. During this period, there was always some floating material over the digestate surface with a foamy, viscous crust along the outer perimeter of the tank until a thick hard crust over the entire surface formed by the end of the period, when the digestate level was low. By Jun 1, 2015 (DOY152) the tank reached its lowest depth, with only 0.16 m (4% full, 119 m³) of digestate remaining, followed by a long period of loading an average rate of 25 m³ d-¹ until Sep. During the loading period, a soft crust started to form over most part of the surface with some bubbling events during the daytime. By Jul 27, 2015 (DOY 208), a black solid crust was covering the whole surface. The crust dissipated in mid-Aug (DOY 226), but by Aug 28 (DOY 240) a 100% black crust cover had formed again. After the agitation and pumping events, on Sep 9 (DOY 252) and Sep 25 (DOY 268) the crust had dissipated completely. After that, bubbling events and some crust formation were observed. From mid-Oct (DOY 289) to mid-Nov (DOY 319), there was another period of regular loading with crust formation followed by a significant emptying event.

The digestate temperature at the bottom of the tank was always much higher (average of 2 °C higher) in 2014 and 2015 as compared to 2013, except for Nov 26 (DOY 330), when temperature was lower in 2014 (Table 3.2). Despite similar monthly average air tem- peratures for all three years, the digestate temperature at 2 m depth and at 0.50 m from the bottom were significantly (p < 0.05) higher for 2014-2015 when compared to 2013 (Table 3.2). An increased storage temperature with AD was also found by Hansen et al. (2006). There was not enough information regarding to changes in the microbiota, digestate char- acteristics or system operation, though, to explain why this temperature increase happened. It may be related to microbiota activity in the tank or some insulation effect caused by the crust cover present most of 2014-2015 and not as frequent in 2013, since the AD and air temperatures were similar for all years. The temperature in the digesters was kept at 38 °C since the first year, but a change in the way or speed the digestate was transferred from digester to storage might have contributed to the increased storage temperature in the oper- ational years (e.g. skipping the solid-separation more often). Unfortunately, there were no reliable records to confirm this hypothesis.

Figure 3.1: (a) Air temperature (C), (b) digestate temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank, (c) daily methane (CH4) fluxes (ug CH4 m 2 s-1 and digestate depth (cm) in the storage tank in 2013, 2014 and 2015. Vertical bars show the standard deviation for the daily mean CH4 flux.
Table 3.2: Monthly average air temperature (C) and digestate temperature (C) in 2013 and 2014-2015 for various depths.

There was not a significant correlation between CH4 fluxes per volume and VS concen- trations in the digestate tank, as also observed in 2013 (Chapter 2). The cross-correlation coefficients (r) between monthly average CH4 fluxes per volume and the measured tem- peratures for 2014 and 2015 were significant for air temperature (r = 0.91) and digestate temperatures at 0.05, 1, and 2 m depths and at 0.50 m above the bottom (r = 0.95) with no lag observed in all cases. The flux response increase to a higher digestate temperature was more prominent in the operational years than in 2013, but still lower than of the untreated manure (Figure 3.2).

Figure 3.2: Monthly averages of methane (CH4) fluxes scaled by volume (g CH4 m -3 d-¹ versus digestate temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank for manure (2010- 2011), digestate in 2013, 2014, 2015 and the combination of 2014/15 data, with respective linear fit.

The monthly VS concentrations in the digestate tank in 2014-2015 were similar to val- ues throughout 2013 (Figure 3.3), with the monthly averages not significantly different (Table 3.3). On the other hand, the CH4 fluxes were higher in 2014-2015 likely due to the much higher digestate temperature. However, the increased CH4 emissions in 2014-2015 were lower than emissions from the manure (2010-2011) in winter, likely due to its higher VS concentration (Figure 3.3). The spring 2014-2015 CH4 emissions were significantly higher than previously measured for that period at this farm for manure (2010-2011) or

digestate (2013). The rapid increase in CH4 emissions in May was due to a combination of frequent tank agitation and pumping from late April and the fast increase in the digestate temperature during the long tank loading period after almost complete emptying. Although the VS concentrations in the tank did not vary much during this period in 2014-2015, diges- tate temperature was higher than in spring 2010-2011 (manure year, Figure 3.3). From Aug on, manure in storage during 2010-2011 warmed up, even though it never reached temper- atures as high as in 2014-2015. Because of the much higher VS concentration, CH4 fluxes from manure were much higher (more than double) than from digestate in 2014-2015 until the rest of the year, with the highest monthly peak emission occurring in Sep 2010-2011 from manure.

|VS concentration (g L-1)|VS concentration (g L-1)|VS concentration (g L-1)|VS concentration (g L-1)|VS concentration (g L-1)|VS concentration (g L-1)|pH|pH|pH|pH|pH|pH |2013|2013|2013|2014-2015|2014-2015|2014-2015|2013|2013|2013|2014-2015|2014-2015|2014-2015 |avg|std|n|avg|std|n|avg|std|n|avg|std|n Manure|90*|13|4|33*|20|8|5.8|1.2|3|7.2|0.4|9 Off-farm|279*|32|9|226*|81|18|4.3*|0.3|7|5.2*|0.7|14 material Digestate|25|3|17|28|15|18|7.9|0.2|17|8|0.2|17
Temperature was the main emission driver for digestate, but even with higher storage temperatures, digestate still emitted less CH4 than untreated manure in Summer and Fall.

Figure 3.3: Monthly total methane (CH4) emission per volume (gCH4 m-3 and monthly average volatile solids (VS) concentrations (kg m-3 and temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank in the manure year (2010-2011), 2013 (start up phase) and 2014- 2015(operational phase).

This environmental benefit, though, was offset by the much larger emissions from digestate in the Spring, as untreated manure took longer to warm up. Note that volume of manure or digestate in the tank was not a factor in this difference as the emissions were scaled by volume (Figure 3.3). Overall, the total annual CH4 emissions from digestate in 2014-2015, 5.6 kg , was significantly higher than in 2013 (1.0kgm-3y-1)and not significantly different than the annual total from manure (2010-2011), 6.6 kg m-3 y-1. . The conflicting results depending on season (spring VS. summer and fall) highlight the importance of year-
long measurements to evaluate the net effect of digestate storage.

The annual average CH4 emission per volume in the operational year (15.6 m-3 d-¹ was twice as much the summer average emission per volume found in pilot-scale studies (7.8 and 8.2 g m-3 d-1, in Rodhe et al. (2015) and Clemens et al. (2006), respectively), but similar to the another long-term study at a commercial biogas facility in Ottawa, ON (19 in Baldé et al. (2016b)). This finding stresses the importance of large-scale studies to proper quantify the emissions considering real-world situations, since agitation and emptying of the tank can increase CH4 emissions substantially.

In agreement with Baldé et al. (2016b), the lowest monthly average emission was 1 g m-3 d-¹, in Jan. The peak monthly average emission, though, was lower and occurred earlier in our study (35 g m d-¹, in Jun) when compared to the biogas facility in Ottawa (52 g m-3 d-1, in Jul), which had a 27-d HRT with 48% (vol) of off-farm materials. The seasonal emissions in our studies were at least 45% lower in all seasons, except in spring when the average CH4 emission (25 g m-3 d-¹ was 2x the spring average (13 g m-3 d-¹ reported in Baldé et al. (2016b). The monthly average digestate temperatures were similar in both sites, but while the volume of digestate stored was high throughout the spring at the Ottawa's biogas facility, there were peaks caused by tank agitation and emptying in our study site.

The total annual cumulative CH4 emissions were similar for digestate in the operational years and untreated manure, 8.9 and 9.6 Mg CH4 y-1, respectively, but the emissions OC- curred much earlier for digestate storage (Figure 3.4). By the middle of 2014-2015 (DOY
Figure 3.4: Cumulative methane (CH4) emission (Mg CH4) in the manure year (2010- 2011), 2013 (start up phase) and 2014-2015 (operational phase).
180), the cumulative CH4 emission had already reached the total annual in 2013 (1.8 Mg CH4) and increased more than double of this value in the second half of the year.

The average monthly CH4 emission scaled by the VS mass were 18.2+13.0 kg-1 VS month-1 in 2014-2015, much higher than from the digestate in 2013 (2.1+1.6 g kg-1 VS month-1 and even higher than the untreated manure in 2010-2011 (6.3+6.2 g kg-1 VS month-1. So, even though there was a significant VS reduction in the AD, the VS in the digestate from the operational years was more reactive in the storage tank, emitting more CH4 per mass unit. This observation is likely related to a more active microbial community in the operational digestate or to a presence of easily digestible VS. The increased digestate temperature also suggests that there might be some microbial differentiation in the opera-
tional year digestate. Analysis of the microbiota present in the digestate storage tank in the start up and operational phases would be desired to understand the observed phenomenon.

The CH4 emission scaled by the VS mass in the operational year (0.6 g kg-1 VS d-¹ was higher than the values found in previous studies (0.21 and 0.27 g kg-1 VS d-1, Rodhe et al. (2015) and Baldé et al. (2016b), respectively). Considering the poor correlation be- tween emissions from digestate and VS content, this parameter should not be used to pre- dict emissions from digestate storage, in contrast to emissions from manure. This finding is relevant, because in the absence of an emission model to predict CH4 emission specifically from digestate, emission models for manure based on VS mass have been used in LCA studies (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017; Ebner et al., 2015).

The storage tank emitted 2.87 and 4.98 g kWh-Superscript(1) in the start-up and operational years, respectively, which corresponded to 1 and 2% of the utilized CH4. These values were within the range, but close to its lower end, of residual emissions from the storage of digestate in 10 biogas plants: 0.22 to 11.2% Liebetrau et al. (2013).

The two AD tanks in sequence reduce the risk of incompletely digested material short circuiting the system and passing through in less than the 60 d HRT. The high CH4 emis- sion per VS might indicate that with the addition of IFW, a longer retention time is required to achieve the same digestion degree of VS observed in 2013. The increased CH4 emission potential in digestate compared to untreated manure suggests that IFW addition had a neg- ative effect in terms of reducing emissions from storage. However, to compare the overall impact of the AD, the alternative disposal of IFW (avoided emissions) has to be included

During this period, there was always some floating material over the digestate surface with a foamy, viscous crust along the outer perimeter of the tank until a thick hard crust over the entire surface formed by the end of the period, when the digestate level was low. By Jun 1, 2015 (DOY152) the tank reached its lowest depth, with only 0.16 m (4% full, 119 m³) of digestate remaining, followed by a long period of loading an average rate of 25 m³ d-¹ until Sep. During the loading period, a soft crust started to form over most part of the surface with some bubbling events during the daytime. By Jul 27, 2015 (DOY 208), a black solid crust was covering the whole surface. The crust dissipated in mid-Aug (DOY 226), but by Aug 28 (DOY 240) a 100% black crust cover had formed again. After the agitation and pumping events, on Sep 9 (DOY 252) and Sep 25 (DOY 268) the crust had dissipated completely. After that, bubbling events and some crust formation were observed. From mid-Oct (DOY 289) to mid-Nov (DOY 319), there was another period of regular loading with crust formation followed by a significant emptying event.

The digestate temperature at the bottom of the tank was always much higher (average of 2 °C higher) in 2014 and 2015 as compared to 2013, except for Nov 26 (DOY 330), when temperature was lower in 2014 (Table 3.2). Despite similar monthly average air tem- peratures for all three years, the digestate temperature at 2 m depth and at 0.50 m from the bottom were significantly (p < 0.05) higher for 2014-2015 when compared to 2013 (Table 3.2). An increased storage temperature with AD was also found by Hansen et al. (2006). There was not enough information regarding to changes in the microbiota, digestate char- acteristics or system operation, though, to explain why this temperature increase happened. It may be related to microbiota activity in the tank or some insulation effect caused by the crust cover present most of 2014-2015 and not as frequent in 2013, since the AD and air temperatures were similar for all years. The temperature in the digesters was kept at 38 °C since the first year, but a change in the way or speed the digestate was transferred from digester to storage might have contributed to the increased storage temperature in the oper- ational years (e.g. skipping the solid-separation more often). Unfortunately, there were no reliable records to confirm this hypothesis.

Figure 3.1: (a) Air temperature (C), (b) digestate temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank, (c) daily methane (CH4) fluxes (ug CH4 m 2 s-1 and digestate depth (cm) in the storage tank in 2013, 2014 and 2015. Vertical bars show the standard deviation for the daily mean CH4 flux.
Table 3.2: Monthly average air temperature (C) and digestate temperature (C) in 2013 and 2014-2015 for various depths.

There was not a significant correlation between CH4 fluxes per volume and VS concen- trations in the digestate tank, as also observed in 2013 (Chapter 2). The cross-correlation coefficients (r) between monthly average CH4 fluxes per volume and the measured tem- peratures for 2014 and 2015 were significant for air temperature (r = 0.91) and digestate temperatures at 0.05, 1, and 2 m depths and at 0.50 m above the bottom (r = 0.95) with no lag observed in all cases. The flux response increase to a higher digestate temperature was more prominent in the operational years than in 2013, but still lower than of the untreated manure (Figure 3.2).

Figure 3.2: Monthly averages of methane (CH4) fluxes scaled by volume (g CH4 m -3 d-¹ versus digestate temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank for manure (2010- 2011), digestate in 2013, 2014, 2015 and the combination of 2014/15 data, with respective linear fit.

The monthly VS concentrations in the digestate tank in 2014-2015 were similar to val- ues throughout 2013 (Figure 3.3), with the monthly averages not significantly different (Table 3.3). On the other hand, the CH4 fluxes were higher in 2014-2015 likely due to the much higher digestate temperature. However, the increased CH4 emissions in 2014-2015 were lower than emissions from the manure (2010-2011) in winter, likely due to its higher VS concentration (Figure 3.3). The spring 2014-2015 CH4 emissions were significantly higher than previously measured for that period at this farm for manure (2010-2011) or

digestate (2013). The rapid increase in CH4 emissions in May was due to a combination of frequent tank agitation and pumping from late April and the fast increase in the digestate temperature during the long tank loading period after almost complete emptying. Although the VS concentrations in the tank did not vary much during this period in 2014-2015, diges- tate temperature was higher than in spring 2010-2011 (manure year, Figure 3.3). From Aug on, manure in storage during 2010-2011 warmed up, even though it never reached temper- atures as high as in 2014-2015. Because of the much higher VS concentration, CH4 fluxes from manure were much higher (more than double) than from digestate in 2014-2015 until the rest of the year, with the highest monthly peak emission occurring in Sep 2010-2011 from manure.

Temperature was the main emission driver for digestate, but even with higher storage temperatures, digestate still emitted less CH4 than untreated manure in Summer and Fall.

Figure 3.3: Monthly total methane (CH4) emission per volume (gCH4 m-3 and monthly average volatile solids (VS) concentrations (kg m-3 and temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank in the manure year (2010-2011), 2013 (start up phase) and 2014- 2015(operational phase).

This environmental benefit, though, was offset by the much larger emissions from digestate in the Spring, as untreated manure took longer to warm up. Note that volume of manure or digestate in the tank was not a factor in this difference as the emissions were scaled by volume (Figure 3.3). Overall, the total annual CH4 emissions from digestate in 2014-2015, 5.6 kg , was significantly higher than in 2013 (1.0kgm-3y-1)and not significantly different than the annual total from manure (2010-2011), 6.6 kg m-3 y-1. . The conflicting results depending on season (spring VS. summer and fall) highlight the importance of year-
long measurements to evaluate the net effect of digestate storage.

The annual average CH4 emission per volume in the operational year (15.6 m-3 d-¹ was twice as much the summer average emission per volume found in pilot-scale studies (7.8 and 8.2 g m-3 d-1, in Rodhe et al. (2015) and Clemens et al. (2006), respectively), but similar to the another long-term study at a commercial biogas facility in Ottawa, ON (19 in Baldé et al. (2016b)). This finding stresses the importance of large-scale studies to proper quantify the emissions considering real-world situations, since agitation and emptying of the tank can increase CH4 emissions substantially.

In agreement with Baldé et al. (2016b), the lowest monthly average emission was 1 g m-3 d-¹, in Jan. The peak monthly average emission, though, was lower and occurred earlier in our study (35 g m d-¹, in Jun) when compared to the biogas facility in Ottawa (52 g m-3 d-1, in Jul), which had a 27-d HRT with 48% (vol) of off-farm materials. The seasonal emissions in our studies were at least 45% lower in all seasons, except in spring when the average CH4 emission (25 g m-3 d-¹ was 2x the spring average (13 g m-3 d-¹ reported in Baldé et al. (2016b). The monthly average digestate temperatures were similar in both sites, but while the volume of digestate stored was high throughout the spring at the Ottawa's biogas facility, there were peaks caused by tank agitation and emptying in our study site.

The total annual cumulative CH4 emissions were similar for digestate in the operational years and untreated manure, 8.9 and 9.6 Mg CH4 y-1, respectively, but the emissions OC- curred much earlier for digestate storage (Figure 3.4). By the middle of 2014-2015 (DOY
Figure 3.4: Cumulative methane (CH4) emission (Mg CH4) in the manure year (2010- 2011), 2013 (start up phase) and 2014-2015 (operational phase).
180), the cumulative CH4 emission had already reached the total annual in 2013 (1.8 Mg CH4) and increased more than double of this value in the second half of the year.

The average monthly CH4 emission scaled by the VS mass were 18.2+13.0 kg-1 VS month-1 in 2014-2015, much higher than from the digestate in 2013 (2.1+1.6 g kg-1 VS month-1 and even higher than the untreated manure in 2010-2011 (6.3+6.2 g kg-1 VS month-1. So, even though there was a significant VS reduction in the AD, the VS in the digestate from the operational years was more reactive in the storage tank, emitting more CH4 per mass unit. This observation is likely related to a more active microbial community in the operational digestate or to a presence of easily digestible VS. The increased digestate temperature also suggests that there might be some microbial differentiation in the opera-
tional year digestate. Analysis of the microbiota present in the digestate storage tank in the start up and operational phases would be desired to understand the observed phenomenon.

The CH4 emission scaled by the VS mass in the operational year (0.6 g kg-1 VS d-¹ was higher than the values found in previous studies (0.21 and 0.27 g kg-1 VS d-1, Rodhe et al. (2015) and Baldé et al. (2016b), respectively). Considering the poor correlation be- tween emissions from digestate and VS content, this parameter should not be used to pre- dict emissions from digestate storage, in contrast to emissions from manure. This finding is relevant, because in the absence of an emission model to predict CH4 emission specifically from digestate, emission models for manure based on VS mass have been used in LCA studies (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017; Ebner et al., 2015).

The storage tank emitted 2.87 and 4.98 g kWh-Superscript(1) in the start-up and operational years, respectively, which corresponded to 1 and 2% of the utilized CH4. These values were within the range, but close to its lower end, of residual emissions from the storage of digestate in 10 biogas plants: 0.22 to 11.2% Liebetrau et al. (2013).

The two AD tanks in sequence reduce the risk of incompletely digested material short circuiting the system and passing through in less than the 60 d HRT. The high CH4 emis- sion per VS might indicate that with the addition of IFW, a longer retention time is required to achieve the same digestion degree of VS observed in 2013. The increased CH4 emission potential in digestate compared to untreated manure suggests that IFW addition had a neg- ative effect in terms of reducing emissions from storage. However, to compare the overall impact of the AD, the alternative disposal of IFW (avoided emissions) has to be included
67: The depth of digestate (100% = 3.66 m) in the storage varied throughout 2014 from 17 to 99% of the total storage capacity and from 4 to 95% in 2015 (Figure 3.1c). In 2013, the digestate depth also varied throughout the year (from 27 to 96%). In the beginning of 2014, the tank was being loaded and the surface was almost fully ice covered, with a small opening close to the inlet loading pipe. An overall loading period with few emptying events happened until Apr 14 (Day of year, DOY 104), when the tank was almost completely full and all snow covering the surface was gone. During this period in 2014, the digestate

temperature at the bottom of the tank was above 8 °C, >4 °C higher than the same period in 2013 (Figure 3.1b), despite similar air temperatures (Figure 3.1a). The CH4 fluxes were also much higher than the same period in the previous year. There was a 20-day period until May 4, 2014 (DOY 124) when the tank volume stayed at its highest capacity, followed by a period of emptying until its lowest level was reached on Jun 3, 2014 (DOY 154). The remaining digestate in the tank was thick, a crust was formed at the surface and remained in the tank with some cracks and bubbling events observed until early Aug 8, 2014 (DOY 220). On Aug 1, 2014 (DOY 213) there was an unusual addition of beef processing sludge co-substrate that had an almost immediate impact on the biogas and energy production (Table 3.1) and, later, on the emissions from the digestate storage tank. The digestate released in mid-Sep (DOY 252) was foamy, probably due to the unusual large load of beef sludge in Aug, 2014. The beef sludge became part of the regular feed recipe along with a larger amount of chicken meat waste, keeping the high-energy production throughout the rest of the year and the next year, 2015. An extended black thick crust with foamy liquid spaced in the cracks was formed in Sep 23 (DOY 266) and present until Oct 4, 2014 (DOY 277). The thick crust dissipated with agitation, loading and emptying events and only some foam was present on the surface until the end of the year when the tank was emptied considerably.

Figure 3.2: Monthly averages of methane (CH4) fluxes scaled by volume (g CH4 m -3 d-¹ versus digestate temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank for manure (2010- 2011), digestate in 2013, 2014, 2015 and the combination of 2014/15 data, with respective linear fit.

The monthly VS concentrations in the digestate tank in 2014-2015 were similar to val- ues throughout 2013 (Figure 3.3), with the monthly averages not significantly different (Table 3.3). On the other hand, the CH4 fluxes were higher in 2014-2015 likely due to the much higher digestate temperature. However, the increased CH4 emissions in 2014-2015 were lower than emissions from the manure (2010-2011) in winter, likely due to its higher VS concentration (Figure 3.3). The spring 2014-2015 CH4 emissions were significantly higher than previously measured for that period at this farm for manure (2010-2011) or

digestate (2013). The rapid increase in CH4 emissions in May was due to a combination of frequent tank agitation and pumping from late April and the fast increase in the digestate temperature during the long tank loading period after almost complete emptying. Although the VS concentrations in the tank did not vary much during this period in 2014-2015, diges- tate temperature was higher than in spring 2010-2011 (manure year, Figure 3.3). From Aug on, manure in storage during 2010-2011 warmed up, even though it never reached temper- atures as high as in 2014-2015. Because of the much higher VS concentration, CH4 fluxes from manure were much higher (more than double) than from digestate in 2014-2015 until the rest of the year, with the highest monthly peak emission occurring in Sep 2010-2011 from manure.

Temperature was the main emission driver for digestate, but even with higher storage temperatures, digestate still emitted less CH4 than untreated manure in Summer and Fall.

Figure 3.3: Monthly total methane (CH4) emission per volume (gCH4 m-3 and monthly average volatile solids (VS) concentrations (kg m-3 and temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank in the manure year (2010-2011), 2013 (start up phase) and 2014- 2015(operational phase).

This environmental benefit, though, was offset by the much larger emissions from digestate in the Spring, as untreated manure took longer to warm up. Note that volume of manure or digestate in the tank was not a factor in this difference as the emissions were scaled by volume (Figure 3.3). Overall, the total annual CH4 emissions from digestate in 2014-2015, 5.6 kg , was significantly higher than in 2013 (1.0kgm-3y-1)and not significantly different than the annual total from manure (2010-2011), 6.6 kg m-3 y-1. . The conflicting results depending on season (spring VS. summer and fall) highlight the importance of year-
long measurements to evaluate the net effect of digestate storage.

The annual average CH4 emission per volume in the operational year (15.6 m-3 d-¹ was twice as much the summer average emission per volume found in pilot-scale studies (7.8 and 8.2 g m-3 d-1, in Rodhe et al. (2015) and Clemens et al. (2006), respectively), but similar to the another long-term study at a commercial biogas facility in Ottawa, ON (19 in Baldé et al. (2016b)). This finding stresses the importance of large-scale studies to proper quantify the emissions considering real-world situations, since agitation and emptying of the tank can increase CH4 emissions substantially.

In agreement with Baldé et al. (2016b), the lowest monthly average emission was 1 g m-3 d-¹, in Jan. The peak monthly average emission, though, was lower and occurred earlier in our study (35 g m d-¹, in Jun) when compared to the biogas facility in Ottawa (52 g m-3 d-1, in Jul), which had a 27-d HRT with 48% (vol) of off-farm materials. The seasonal emissions in our studies were at least 45% lower in all seasons, except in spring when the average CH4 emission (25 g m-3 d-¹ was 2x the spring average (13 g m-3 d-¹ reported in Baldé et al. (2016b). The monthly average digestate temperatures were similar in both sites, but while the volume of digestate stored was high throughout the spring at the Ottawa's biogas facility, there were peaks caused by tank agitation and emptying in our study site.

The total annual cumulative CH4 emissions were similar for digestate in the operational years and untreated manure, 8.9 and 9.6 Mg CH4 y-1, respectively, but the emissions OC- curred much earlier for digestate storage (Figure 3.4). By the middle of 2014-2015 (DOY
Figure 3.4: Cumulative methane (CH4) emission (Mg CH4) in the manure year (2010- 2011), 2013 (start up phase) and 2014-2015 (operational phase).
180), the cumulative CH4 emission had already reached the total annual in 2013 (1.8 Mg CH4) and increased more than double of this value in the second half of the year.

The average monthly CH4 emission scaled by the VS mass were 18.2+13.0 kg-1 VS month-1 in 2014-2015, much higher than from the digestate in 2013 (2.1+1.6 g kg-1 VS month-1 and even higher than the untreated manure in 2010-2011 (6.3+6.2 g kg-1 VS month-1. So, even though there was a significant VS reduction in the AD, the VS in the digestate from the operational years was more reactive in the storage tank, emitting more CH4 per mass unit. This observation is likely related to a more active microbial community in the operational digestate or to a presence of easily digestible VS. The increased digestate temperature also suggests that there might be some microbial differentiation in the opera-
tional year digestate. Analysis of the microbiota present in the digestate storage tank in the start up and operational phases would be desired to understand the observed phenomenon.

The CH4 emission scaled by the VS mass in the operational year (0.6 g kg-1 VS d-¹ was higher than the values found in previous studies (0.21 and 0.27 g kg-1 VS d-1, Rodhe et al. (2015) and Baldé et al. (2016b), respectively). Considering the poor correlation be- tween emissions from digestate and VS content, this parameter should not be used to pre- dict emissions from digestate storage, in contrast to emissions from manure. This finding is relevant, because in the absence of an emission model to predict CH4 emission specifically from digestate, emission models for manure based on VS mass have been used in LCA studies (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017; Ebner et al., 2015).

The storage tank emitted 2.87 and 4.98 g kWh-Superscript(1) in the start-up and operational years, respectively, which corresponded to 1 and 2% of the utilized CH4. These values were within the range, but close to its lower end, of residual emissions from the storage of digestate in 10 biogas plants: 0.22 to 11.2% Liebetrau et al. (2013).

The two AD tanks in sequence reduce the risk of incompletely digested material short circuiting the system and passing through in less than the 60 d HRT. The high CH4 emis- sion per VS might indicate that with the addition of IFW, a longer retention time is required to achieve the same digestion degree of VS observed in 2013. The increased CH4 emission potential in digestate compared to untreated manure suggests that IFW addition had a neg- ative effect in terms of reducing emissions from storage. However, to compare the overall impact of the AD, the alternative disposal of IFW (avoided emissions) has to be included
69: The depth of digestate (100% = 3.66 m) in the storage varied throughout 2014 from 17 to 99% of the total storage capacity and from 4 to 95% in 2015 (Figure 3.1c). In 2013, the digestate depth also varied throughout the year (from 27 to 96%). In the beginning of 2014, the tank was being loaded and the surface was almost fully ice covered, with a small opening close to the inlet loading pipe. An overall loading period with few emptying events happened until Apr 14 (Day of year, DOY 104), when the tank was almost completely full and all snow covering the surface was gone. During this period in 2014, the digestate

temperature at the bottom of the tank was above 8 °C, >4 °C higher than the same period in 2013 (Figure 3.1b), despite similar air temperatures (Figure 3.1a). The CH4 fluxes were also much higher than the same period in the previous year. There was a 20-day period until May 4, 2014 (DOY 124) when the tank volume stayed at its highest capacity, followed by a period of emptying until its lowest level was reached on Jun 3, 2014 (DOY 154). The remaining digestate in the tank was thick, a crust was formed at the surface and remained in the tank with some cracks and bubbling events observed until early Aug 8, 2014 (DOY 220). On Aug 1, 2014 (DOY 213) there was an unusual addition of beef processing sludge co-substrate that had an almost immediate impact on the biogas and energy production (Table 3.1) and, later, on the emissions from the digestate storage tank. The digestate released in mid-Sep (DOY 252) was foamy, probably due to the unusual large load of beef sludge in Aug, 2014. The beef sludge became part of the regular feed recipe along with a larger amount of chicken meat waste, keeping the high-energy production throughout the rest of the year and the next year, 2015. An extended black thick crust with foamy liquid spaced in the cracks was formed in Sep 23 (DOY 266) and present until Oct 4, 2014 (DOY 277). The thick crust dissipated with agitation, loading and emptying events and only some foam was present on the surface until the end of the year when the tank was emptied considerably.

In the beginning of 2015 the tank was also loaded, with one emptying event in mid-Feb (DOY 43) and another in mid-Mar (DOY 78). During the winter (Jan 1, DOY 1 to Mar 31, DOY 90), the digestate surface had continuous snow/ice cover. The CH4 flux measurements started in mid-Mar, 2015 (DOY 71), when the tank was full and being agitated before pumping. There was still some snow cover but most of the digestate was exposed with no crust at this time. Throughout the emptying period the digestate was constantly being agitated and some high fluxes for this period (Mar to May) were measured. During this period, there was always some floating material over the digestate surface with a foamy, viscous crust along the outer perimeter of the tank until a thick hard crust over the entire surface formed by the end of the period, when the digestate level was low. By Jun 1, 2015 (DOY152) the tank reached its lowest depth, with only 0.16 m (4% full, 119 m³) of digestate remaining, followed by a long period of loading an average rate of 25 m³ d-¹ until Sep. During the loading period, a soft crust started to form over most part of the surface with some bubbling events during the daytime. By Jul 27, 2015 (DOY 208), a black solid crust was covering the whole surface. The crust dissipated in mid-Aug (DOY 226), but by Aug 28 (DOY 240) a 100% black crust cover had formed again. After the agitation and pumping events, on Sep 9 (DOY 252) and Sep 25 (DOY 268) the crust had dissipated completely. After that, bubbling events and some crust formation were observed. From mid-Oct (DOY 289) to mid-Nov (DOY 319), there was another period of regular loading with crust formation followed by a significant emptying event.

The digestate temperature at the bottom of the tank was always much higher (average of 2 °C higher) in 2014 and 2015 as compared to 2013, except for Nov 26 (DOY 330), when temperature was lower in 2014 (Table 3.2). Despite similar monthly average air tem- peratures for all three years, the digestate temperature at 2 m depth and at 0.50 m from the bottom were significantly (p < 0.05) higher for 2014-2015 when compared to 2013 (Table 3.2). An increased storage temperature with AD was also found by Hansen et al. (2006). There was not enough information regarding to changes in the microbiota, digestate char- acteristics or system operation, though, to explain why this temperature increase happened. It may be related to microbiota activity in the tank or some insulation effect caused by the crust cover present most of 2014-2015 and not as frequent in 2013, since the AD and air temperatures were similar for all years. The temperature in the digesters was kept at 38 °C since the first year, but a change in the way or speed the digestate was transferred from digester to storage might have contributed to the increased storage temperature in the oper- ational years (e.g. skipping the solid-separation more often). Unfortunately, there were no reliable records to confirm this hypothesis.

There was not a significant correlation between CH4 fluxes per volume and VS concen- trations in the digestate tank, as also observed in 2013 (Chapter 2). Figure 3.3: Monthly total methane (CH4) emission per volume (gCH4 m-3 and monthly average volatile solids (VS) concentrations (kg m-3 and temperature (C) at 0.50 m from the bottom of the tank in the manure year (2010-2011), 2013 (start up phase) and 2014- 2015(operational phase).

This environmental benefit, though, was offset by the much larger emissions from digestate in the Spring, as untreated manure took longer to warm up. Note that volume of manure or digestate in the tank was not a factor in this difference as the emissions were scaled by volume (Figure 3.3). Overall, the total annual CH4 emissions from digestate in 2014-2015, 5.6 kg , was significantly higher than in 2013 (1.0kgm-3y-1)and not significantly different than the annual total from manure (2010-2011), 6.6 kg m-3 y-1. . The conflicting results depending on season (spring VS. summer and fall) highlight the importance of year-
long measurements to evaluate the net effect of digestate storage.

The annual average CH4 emission per volume in the operational year (15.6 m-3 d-¹ was twice as much the summer average emission per volume found in pilot-scale studies (7.8 and 8.2 g m-3 d-1, in Rodhe et al. (2015) and Clemens et al. (2006), respectively), but similar to the another long-term study at a commercial biogas facility in Ottawa, ON (19 in Baldé et al. (2016b)). This finding stresses the importance of large-scale studies to proper quantify the emissions considering real-world situations, since agitation and emptying of the tank can increase CH4 emissions substantially.

In agreement with Baldé et al. (2016b), the lowest monthly average emission was 1 g m-3 d-¹, in Jan. The peak monthly average emission, though, was lower and occurred earlier in our study (35 g m d-¹, in Jun) when compared to the biogas facility in Ottawa (52 g m-3 d-1, in Jul), which had a 27-d HRT with 48% (vol) of off-farm materials. The seasonal emissions in our studies were at least 45% lower in all seasons, except in spring when the average CH4 emission (25 g m-3 d-¹ was 2x the spring average (13 g m-3 d-¹ reported in Baldé et al. (2016b). The monthly average digestate temperatures were similar in both sites, but while the volume of digestate stored was high throughout the spring at the Ottawa's biogas facility, there were peaks caused by tank agitation and emptying in our study site.

The total annual cumulative CH4 emissions were similar for digestate in the operational years and untreated manure, 8.9 and 9.6 Mg CH4 y-1, respectively, but the emissions OC- curred much earlier for digestate storage (Figure 3.4). By the middle of 2014-2015 (DOY
Figure 3.4: Cumulative methane (CH4) emission (Mg CH4) in the manure year (2010- 2011), 2013 (start up phase) and 2014-2015 (operational phase).
180), the cumulative CH4 emission had already reached the total annual in 2013 (1.8 Mg CH4) and increased more than double of this value in the second half of the year.

The average monthly CH4 emission scaled by the VS mass were 18.2+13.0 kg-1 VS month-1 in 2014-2015, much higher than from the digestate in 2013 (2.1+1.6 g kg-1 VS month-1 and even higher than the untreated manure in 2010-2011 (6.3+6.2 g kg-1 VS month-1. So, even though there was a significant VS reduction in the AD, the VS in the digestate from the operational years was more reactive in the storage tank, emitting more CH4 per mass unit. This observation is likely related to a more active microbial community in the operational digestate or to a presence of easily digestible VS. The increased digestate temperature also suggests that there might be some microbial differentiation in the opera-
tional year digestate. Analysis of the microbiota present in the digestate storage tank in the start up and operational phases would be desired to understand the observed phenomenon.

The CH4 emission scaled by the VS mass in the operational year (0.6 g kg-1 VS d-¹ was higher than the values found in previous studies (0.21 and 0.27 g kg-1 VS d-1, Rodhe et al. (2015) and Baldé et al. (2016b), respectively). Considering the poor correlation be- tween emissions from digestate and VS content, this parameter should not be used to pre- dict emissions from digestate storage, in contrast to emissions from manure. This finding is relevant, because in the absence of an emission model to predict CH4 emission specifically from digestate, emission models for manure based on VS mass have been used in LCA studies (Aguirre-Villegas and Larson, 2017; Ebner et al., 2015).

The storage tank emitted 2.87 and 4.98 g kWh-Superscript(1) in the start-up and operational years, respectively, which corresponded to 1 and 2% of the utilized CH4. These values were within the range, but close to its lower end, of residual emissions from the storage of digestate in 10 biogas plants: 0.22 to 11.2% Liebetrau et al. (2013).

The two AD tanks in sequence reduce the risk of incompletely digested material short circuiting the system and passing through in less than the 60 d HRT. The high CH4 emis- sion per VS might indicate that with the addition of IFW, a longer retention time is required to achieve the same digestion degree of VS observed in 2013. The increased CH4 emission potential in digestate compared to untreated manure suggests that IFW addition had a neg- ative effect in terms of reducing emissions from storage. However, to compare the overall impact of the AD, the alternative disposal of IFW (avoided emissions) has to be included
71: A change in the digester recipe significantly affected the residual CH4 emissions from the digestate tank. In the first year of the on-farm biogas facility, feeding the AD with dairy manure and ~ 30% off-farm material composed basically of carbohydrates (onions, bananas and sugar), produced CH4 emissions that were significantly lower than untreated manure. The energy production was also lower than desired and, because of that, the recipe was adjusted by increasing the volume and composition of the off-farm materials. The operational recipe consisted of ~ 40% off-farm material, comprised of fats, proteins (chicken and beef meat waste) and a smaller proportion of carbohydrates. Keeping the 60-day hydraulic retention time, the energy production increased to a satisfactory level, but there was also a significant increase of the emissions from the digestate storage tank (CH4 loss).

The increased emissions could be explained by the higher temperature measured in the storage during the operational years as compared to the start-up phase, supported by evidence that temperature was the main emission driver of CH4 emissions. The exact cause of the temperature increase was not determined, but it might be related to insulation effect caused by storage crust, enhanced microbial activity or change in the manner digestate was transferred to the storage in the operational year as compare to the start-up period.

Although the results indicated that the operational AD was not able to reduce CH4 emissions from storage, to assess the overall environmental effect, a more comprehensive analysis should be performed. The increased volume and variety of off-farm material intro- duced complexity to the comparison between untreated and treated manure. The alternative fate of the off-farm as well as the increased in energy production should be accounted for. For a complex system like this, only a LCA approach using the data provided here, can determine the overall environmental impact of the anaerobic digestion of manure in com- parison to the conventional manure management practice.

The operational recipe consisted of ~ 40% off-farm material, comprised of fats, proteins (chicken and beef meat waste) and a smaller proportion of carbohydrates. 145: Conventional liquid manure systems in Canadian dairy farms usually consist of holding manure in an open storage tank until the weather conditions are favourable for land applica- tion. This practice has the disadvantage of CH4 emission, a potent greenhouse gas (GHG), from the organic matter decomposition that occurs in anaerobic storage. An alternative ma-

nure management technology that has been promoted to mitigate this environmental impact is the anaerobic digestion (AD) of manure. The AD systems add a manure treatment step before storage, where CH4 emissions are captured in a closed system - anaerobic digester - and used as fuel to generate energy (electricity and heat). Although the AD of manure is efficient to reduce GHG emissions from storage (Chapter 2), it is not financially attractive. Industrial food waste (IFW) is commonly added (in proportion usually < 50% by volume) as co-substrate into the reactors (Pehme and Veromann, 2015). After treatment, the open storage tank holds the treated manure (digestate) to be land applied when weather permits.

A higher proportion of energy-rich off-farm materials (fat, oil and grease - FOG) en- hance energy production but also increase the volume of waste to be treated and potentially the fugitive emissions, which can offset the environmental benefit from the system as com- pare to the conventional practice. A change in the AD recipe (more off-farm material in proportion to manure and off-farm materials richer in FOG) resulted in CH4 emissions from digestate on the same order of magnitude as that from untreated manure at the same farm (Chapter 3) and much higher emissions compared to an AD recipe with low off-farm material addition (Chapter 2).

In Chapter 3, it was shown that the Anaerobic Digestion (AD) of manure with IFW does not reduce the GHG emissions from the open storage tank when compared to the con- ventional manure management practice. The total annual methane (CH4) emission from the digestate storage tank was 5.6 kg m-3 y-1, not significantly different than the 6.6 kg m-3 y-1 measured for the untreated manure storage at the facility before the AD system

became operational. Also, ammonia (NH3) emissions from digestate storage has been re- ported to be 50% higher than emissions from untreated manure (Hou et al., 2017). The land application of digestate could result in increased nitrogen loss by nitrate (NO) leach- ing (Schwager et al., 2016) and higher nitrous oxide (N20) emissions (Cambareri et al., 2017). Emission data from experimental studies are very important, but, alone, they do not allow any conclusion about the overall impact of a system. When comparing envi- ronmental effects of different systems/products, pollution swapping often occurs where reducing an environmental impact of the product at one stage can cause another effect later (or earlier) in the product lifecycle. For that reason, to effectively compare the two manure management systems (conventional versus AD) in terms of environmental impact, a more comprehensive approach using these emission data is required.

The life cycle assessment (LCA) is an international standardized methodology to quan- tify the potential environmental impact of a product or system considering the natural re- sources consumed and the emissions released to the environment throughout its life cycle, since the acquisitions of raw materials to the final decommissioning and disposal of the product/system (ISO, 2006a,b). By considering the whole life cycle of a system and all its environmental interaction (resources and emissions), an LCA allows the identification of eventual impact trade-offs between different stages of a system chain. This methodology is a powerful tool to guide government and non-government decision makers comparing systems that provide similar outcomes (same function) and identifying within a system the stages of its life cycle that have the greatest influence on its potential environmental

There are many LCA studies on the potential environmental benefits of on-farm biogas systems around the world (Aguirre-villegas et al., 2014; Battini et al., 2014; Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; Ebner et al., 2015; Hishinuma et al., 2008; De Vries et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2013) and all of them have concluded that the digestate storage is an important GHG emission source in AD facilities. One problem is that the emission factors from the digestate storage are usually from batch and pilot-scale studies that measured digestate emissions only for a short period of time (few days to few months). Since storage emissions are an important source of the AD environmental impact, it is relevant to perform an LCA study using the best data available, preferable from long term field scale studies. Up to now, to my knowledge, there are only two year-long large scale measurements of CH4 emissions from digestate storage (Chapter 4, Baldé et al. (2016b)) and their results have not been applied in an LCA.

Furthermore, there is no universal solution to an environmental problem, since the over- all impact is very much affected by regional practices, energy sources and emission factors. For this reason, another common conclusion from LCA studies is the need to have region specific LCA research (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; Ebner et al., 2015; Poeschl et al., 2012a,b). Again, to my knowledge, an LCA study using Canadian practices, climate con- ditions and measured field emissions from digestate storage and land application has not been conducted up to now.
Most LCAs on AD focus on energy inputs and biogas production associated to specific

aspects of system design and operation, while comprehensive assessment of environmental impacts of AD as a manure management technology is lacking (Aguirre-villegas et al., 2014). Also, they were mostly conducted in Europe, where the feedstock, AD design, off-farm material disposal, transport distances and energy grid mix are different than the Canadian context. LCAs on AD in North America are still limited and necessary to support decisions of policy makers.
The objectives of this study were:

<list>1. Identify the main impact categories affected by conventional and anaerobic digestion manure treatments; 
2. Assess the potential GHG environmental impact of the anaerobic co-digestion of dairy manure with industrial food waste (IFW) as compared to the conventional ma- nure practice using data from a case study in Ontario, Canada; 
3. Identify the main GHG emissions sources in each system to guide target areas where mitigations strategies are needed.</list>

Most LCAs on AD focus on energy inputs and biogas production associated to specific

aspects of system design and operation, while comprehensive assessment of environmental impacts of AD as a manure management technology is lacking (Aguirre-villegas et al., 2014). Also, they were mostly conducted in Europe, where the feedstock, AD design, off-farm material disposal, transport distances and energy grid mix are different than the Canadian context. The objectives of this study were:

<list>1. Identify the main impact categories affected by conventional and anaerobic digestion manure treatments; 
2. Assess the potential GHG environmental impact of the anaerobic co-digestion of dairy manure with industrial food waste (IFW) as compared to the conventional ma- nure practice using data from a case study in Ontario, Canada; 
3. Identify the main GHG emissions sources in each system to guide target areas where mitigations strategies are needed.</list>
147: Conventional liquid manure systems in Canadian dairy farms usually consist of holding manure in an open storage tank until the weather conditions are favourable for land applica- tion. This practice has the disadvantage of CH4 emission, a potent greenhouse gas (GHG), from the organic matter decomposition that occurs in anaerobic storage. An alternative ma-

nure management technology that has been promoted to mitigate this environmental impact is the anaerobic digestion (AD) of manure. The AD systems add a manure treatment step before storage, where CH4 emissions are captured in a closed system - anaerobic digester - and used as fuel to generate energy (electricity and heat). Although the AD of manure is efficient to reduce GHG emissions from storage (Chapter 2), it is not financially attractive. Industrial food waste (IFW) is commonly added (in proportion usually < 50% by volume) as co-substrate into the reactors (Pehme and Veromann, 2015). After treatment, the open storage tank holds the treated manure (digestate) to be land applied when weather permits.

A higher proportion of energy-rich off-farm materials (fat, oil and grease - FOG) en- hance energy production but also increase the volume of waste to be treated and potentially the fugitive emissions, which can offset the environmental benefit from the system as com- pare to the conventional practice. A change in the AD recipe (more off-farm material in proportion to manure and off-farm materials richer in FOG) resulted in CH4 emissions from digestate on the same order of magnitude as that from untreated manure at the same farm (Chapter 3) and much higher emissions compared to an AD recipe with low off-farm material addition (Chapter 2).

In Chapter 3, it was shown that the Anaerobic Digestion (AD) of manure with IFW does not reduce the GHG emissions from the open storage tank when compared to the con- ventional manure management practice. The total annual methane (CH4) emission from the digestate storage tank was 5.6 kg m-3 y-1, not significantly different than the 6.6 kg m-3 y-1 measured for the untreated manure storage at the facility before the AD system

became operational. Also, ammonia (NH3) emissions from digestate storage has been re- ported to be 50% higher than emissions from untreated manure (Hou et al., 2017). The land application of digestate could result in increased nitrogen loss by nitrate (NO) leach- ing (Schwager et al., 2016) and higher nitrous oxide (N20) emissions (Cambareri et al., 2017). Emission data from experimental studies are very important, but, alone, they do not allow any conclusion about the overall impact of a system. When comparing envi- ronmental effects of different systems/products, pollution swapping often occurs where reducing an environmental impact of the product at one stage can cause another effect later (or earlier) in the product lifecycle. For that reason, to effectively compare the two manure management systems (conventional versus AD) in terms of environmental impact, a more comprehensive approach using these emission data is required.

The life cycle assessment (LCA) is an international standardized methodology to quan- tify the potential environmental impact of a product or system considering the natural re- sources consumed and the emissions released to the environment throughout its life cycle, since the acquisitions of raw materials to the final decommissioning and disposal of the product/system (ISO, 2006a,b). By considering the whole life cycle of a system and all its environmental interaction (resources and emissions), an LCA allows the identification of eventual impact trade-offs between different stages of a system chain. This methodology is a powerful tool to guide government and non-government decision makers comparing systems that provide similar outcomes (same function) and identifying within a system the stages of its life cycle that have the greatest influence on its potential environmental

There are many LCA studies on the potential environmental benefits of on-farm biogas systems around the world (Aguirre-villegas et al., 2014; Battini et al., 2014; Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; Ebner et al., 2015; Hishinuma et al., 2008; De Vries et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2013) and all of them have concluded that the digestate storage is an important GHG emission source in AD facilities. One problem is that the emission factors from the digestate storage are usually from batch and pilot-scale studies that measured digestate emissions only for a short period of time (few days to few months). Since storage emissions are an important source of the AD environmental impact, it is relevant to perform an LCA study using the best data available, preferable from long term field scale studies. Up to now, to my knowledge, there are only two year-long large scale measurements of CH4 emissions from digestate storage (Chapter 4, Baldé et al. (2016b)) and their results have not been applied in an LCA.

Furthermore, there is no universal solution to an environmental problem, since the over- all impact is very much affected by regional practices, energy sources and emission factors. For this reason, another common conclusion from LCA studies is the need to have region specific LCA research (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007; Ebner et al., 2015; Poeschl et al., 2012a,b). Again, to my knowledge, an LCA study using Canadian practices, climate con- ditions and measured field emissions from digestate storage and land application has not been conducted up to now.
Most LCAs on AD focus on energy inputs and biogas production associated to specific

aspects of system design and operation, while comprehensive assessment of environmental impacts of AD as a manure management technology is lacking (Aguirre-villegas et al., 2014). Also, they were mostly conducted in Europe, where the feedstock, AD design, off-farm material disposal, transport distances and energy grid mix are different than the Canadian context. LCAs on AD in North America are still limited and necessary to support decisions of policy makers.
The objectives of this study were:

<list>1. Identify the main impact categories affected by conventional and anaerobic digestion manure treatments;

3. Identify the main GHG emissions sources in each system to guide target areas where mitigations strategies are needed.</list>

152: Data were collected on a 160-cow dairy farm located in Alma, Ontario, Canada (43°45'N 80°40'W). The on-farm biogas facility used two reactors in series and operated at 38 °C

(mesophilic treatment) with a 60-day hydraulic retention time. The AD recipe was com- posed of liquid manure, solid manure and IFW as off-farm materials (49%, 12% and 39% of total input by volume, respectively). The off-farm material share was composed of chicken meat waste, onions and bananas and beef processing sludge representing 84%, 11% and 5%, by volume, respectively. The total AD loading rate was 34.5 m³ d-¹ (Table 4.1). The average biogas production was 60 m³ h-superscript(1). , with 57% CH4 content by volume. There was a 250 kW combined heat and power unit (CHP) producing on average 5220 kWh d-¹ with 43% electrical efficiency, and heat. The heat produced was used to keep the digesters at 38 °C and to heat farm buildings. A screw-press for solid-liquid separation had a removal efficiency of 11% of total solids as calculated using the total solids (TS) composition data before and after solid-separation (Table 4.2). The solids were used as barn bedding while the liquid digestate was loaded into an open storage tank.

The AD system became operational in Oct 2012. CH4 emissions from manure stor- age were measured from Jul 2010 to Nov 2011 and were used to represent the conventional system. Untreated manure, IFW and digestate before and after solid-liquid separation com- positions were analysed in terms of Total and Volatile Solids (TS and VS), Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN), Total Ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), Nitrate (NO) and pH (Table 4.2).
Table 4.1: Information from the on-farm biogas facility where the field study took place. The data was obtained from farm records and measurements.

153: Data were collected on a 160-cow dairy farm located in Alma, Ontario, Canada (43°45'N 80°40'W). The on-farm biogas facility used two reactors in series and operated at 38 °C

(mesophilic treatment) with a 60-day hydraulic retention time. The AD recipe was com- posed of liquid manure, solid manure and IFW as off-farm materials (49%, 12% and 39% of total input by volume, respectively). The off-farm material share was composed of chicken meat waste, onions and bananas and beef processing sludge representing 84%, 11% and 5%, by volume, respectively. The total AD loading rate was 34.5 m³ d-¹ (Table 4.1). The average biogas production was 60 m³ h-superscript(1). , with 57% CH4 content by volume. There was a 250 kW combined heat and power unit (CHP) producing on average 5220 kWh d-¹ with 43% electrical efficiency, and heat. The heat produced was used to keep the digesters at 38 °C and to heat farm buildings. A screw-press for solid-liquid separation had a removal efficiency of 11% of total solids as calculated using the total solids (TS) composition data before and after solid-separation (Table 4.2). The solids were used as barn bedding while the liquid digestate was loaded into an open storage tank.

The AD system became operational in Oct 2012. CH4 emissions from manure stor- age were measured from Jul 2010 to Nov 2011 and were used to represent the conventional system. Untreated manure, IFW and digestate before and after solid-liquid separation com- positions were analysed in terms of Total and Volatile Solids (TS and VS), Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN), Total Ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), Nitrate (NO) and pH (Table 4.2).
