157: The LCA relates the main substances consumed (resources depleted) and emitted (con- taminants) by the processes involved in a system life cycle (Life Cycle Inventory - LCI) to impact categories (midpoint impacts) that are associated to damage categories (human health, ecosystem diversity, resource availability). Each impact category has an impact in- dicator that provides an overall impact of the category. The impact indicator is calculated by multiplying the results of the LCI with a characterization factor. The impact categories commonly used are (with its respective impact indicator in brackets): global warming po- tential (kg CO2eq to air), ozone depleting potential (kg CFC-11eq to air), terrestrial acidi- fication potential (kg SO2eq to air), freshwater eutrophication potential (kg Peg to water), marine eutrophication potential (kg Neg to water), human toxicity potential (kg 14-DCBeq to air), photochemical oxidant formation potential (kg NMVOC eq - non CH4 volatile or- ganic carbon to air), particulate matter formation potential (kg PM10ec to air), terrestrial, aquatic and marine toxicity potential (kg 14-DCBeq to soil, freshwater and marine water, respectively), ionising radiation potential (kg U235), agricultural and urban land occupa- tion potential (m-2 agricultural and urban land, respectively), natural land transformation potential (m² natural land), water, mineral and fossil fuel depletion potential (m³ water, kg Fe, kg oil, respectively). The most important category of concern for manure management is the global warming potential, due to the emissions of GHG and NH3, that occur in the life cycle of manure treatments.
An LCA was done for two scenarios: the conventional system and the AD system, to

identify within each manure management practice the main environmental impact contrib- utors and compare the impact of both systems, identifying eventual environmental benefit trade-offs. In an LCA it is very important to guarantee equivalence between the systems being compared and all impact must be calculated in terms of the same functional unit (FU) (ISO, 2006a). The LCA was done using SimaPro software 8.3 version. Two impact meth- ods were used: Recipe Midpoint (H) V1.13, to identify the main impact categories among the ones stated above affected by the treatments, and IPCC 2013 GWP 100 y to focus on the GHG emission specifically.

In this study, the AD system is considered an alternative to the conventional liquid manure treatment, therefore, the functional unit selected was based on the mass of liquid manure to be treated (input) into the system (FU = 1 metric ton of liquid manure). The results were also expressed in terms of input waste for comparison purposes with other publications. The waste treated in the conventional system was liquid manure only, while the input waste in the AD system included liquid manure, solid manure and IFW in the proportion of the system recipe (Table 4.3). The AD treatment of the inputs replaces the conventional input destination of: storage (liquid manure), stockpile (solid manure), land- filling (70% of IFW) and composting (30% of IFW).

The output from the conventional practice is manure that replaces use of inorganic fer- tilizer, while the outputs (products) from the AD system are: liquid digestate (inorganic fer- tilizer replacement), solid digestate (barn bedding material replacement), electricity (grid electricity replacement) and heat (Table 4.4). The impact of the avoided processes was

Table 4.3: Functional unit (FU) composition for each treatment and density of the input materials. * density calculated from a weighted average of all IFW materials (chicken meat waste, onions and bananas and beef processing sludge representing 84%, 11% and 5%, by volume, respectively)
Input substrate (FU = 1 t of liquid manure)|Conventional System (t)|AD system (t)|Density (kg m -3 Liquid manure|1|1|1,000 Solid manure|0|0.2|800 Industrial food waste (IFW)|0|0.3|380*

calculated and accounted as a positive impact of the system. The thermal energy produced in the AD system was used to heat the farm and system building (including the digesters, kept always at 38 °C) and was excluded from the analysis as a conservative assumption.
The wheat straw production for barn bedding was not considered as an avoided product as it is a by product of the grain wheat production.
Table 4.4: Output amount from each system, calculated based on the functional unit (FU = 1 t liquid manure input) and the mass balance throughout the processes.

Output|Treatment|Mass (t)|kWh|System replaced Liquid manure|Conventional|0.996||Inorganic fertilizer Liquid digestate|AD|1.233||Inorganic fertilizer Solid digestate|AD|0.006||Whey straw production Grid electricity|AD||131|Ontario eletricity mix

The conventional system included the following processes: liquid manure storage, fuel consumption for transport and spreading of manure and soil emissions after manure land application. The avoided product considered for the conventional practice was the inor- ganic fertilizer production and transport to the field. Processes within the barn and crop
Figure 4.1: Reference system process flow diagram with system boundary and unit pro- cesses. Storage period was assumed to occur over 240 d before land application.

The AD system included the processes: transport of off-farm materials to the facility, anaerobic digesters, cogeneration unit, digestate storage tank, fuel consumption for trans- port and spreading of digestate and soil emissions after digestate land application (Figure 4.2). For the AD system, the avoided products were: grid electrical energy, IFW landfilling (70%) and composting (30%), solid manure stockpile and inorganic fertilizer production and transport to the field. Processes within the barn and crop yield were not included. GHG and NH3 missions from the solid-liquid separator were not included in the analysis due to lack of data. Emission associated with construction, maintenance and decommissioning of the AD plant was excluded as it represents < 1% of GHG emissions (Meyer-Aurich et al., 2012).

Figure 4.2: Anaerobic Digestion (AD) system process flow diagram with system boundary and unit processes. Industrial food waste (IFW), liquid and solid manure are shown as well as digestate mass flow and retention time in the digester and storage.

161: The LCA relates the main substances consumed (resources depleted) and emitted (con- taminants) by the processes involved in a system life cycle (Life Cycle Inventory - LCI) to impact categories (midpoint impacts) that are associated to damage categories (human health, ecosystem diversity, resource availability). Each impact category has an impact in- dicator that provides an overall impact of the category. The impact indicator is calculated by multiplying the results of the LCI with a characterization factor. The impact categories commonly used are (with its respective impact indicator in brackets): global warming po- tential (kg CO2eq to air), ozone depleting potential (kg CFC-11eq to air), terrestrial acidi- fication potential (kg SO2eq to air), freshwater eutrophication potential (kg Peg to water), marine eutrophication potential (kg Neg to water), human toxicity potential (kg 14-DCBeq to air), photochemical oxidant formation potential (kg NMVOC eq - non CH4 volatile or- ganic carbon to air), particulate matter formation potential (kg PM10ec to air), terrestrial, aquatic and marine toxicity potential (kg 14-DCBeq to soil, freshwater and marine water, respectively), ionising radiation potential (kg U235), agricultural and urban land occupa- tion potential (m-2 agricultural and urban land, respectively), natural land transformation potential (m² natural land), water, mineral and fossil fuel depletion potential (m³ water, kg Fe, kg oil, respectively). The most important category of concern for manure management is the global warming potential, due to the emissions of GHG and NH3, that occur in the life cycle of manure treatments.
An LCA was done for two scenarios: the conventional system and the AD system, to

identify within each manure management practice the main environmental impact contrib- utors and compare the impact of both systems, identifying eventual environmental benefit trade-offs. In an LCA it is very important to guarantee equivalence between the systems being compared and all impact must be calculated in terms of the same functional unit (FU) (ISO, 2006a). The LCA was done using SimaPro software 8.3 version. Two impact meth- ods were used: Recipe Midpoint (H) V1.13, to identify the main impact categories among the ones stated above affected by the treatments, and IPCC 2013 GWP 100 y to focus on the GHG emission specifically.

In this study, the AD system is considered an alternative to the conventional liquid manure treatment, therefore, the functional unit selected was based on the mass of liquid manure to be treated (input) into the system (FU = 1 metric ton of liquid manure). The results were also expressed in terms of input waste for comparison purposes with other publications. The waste treated in the conventional system was liquid manure only, while the input waste in the AD system included liquid manure, solid manure and IFW in the proportion of the system recipe (Table 4.3). The AD treatment of the inputs replaces the conventional input destination of: storage (liquid manure), stockpile (solid manure), land- filling (70% of IFW) and composting (30% of IFW).

The output from the conventional practice is manure that replaces use of inorganic fer- tilizer, while the outputs (products) from the AD system are: liquid digestate (inorganic fer- tilizer replacement), solid digestate (barn bedding material replacement), electricity (grid electricity replacement) and heat (Table 4.4). 164: <header>4.2.3 Life cycle inventory of on-site emissions </header>

The emission factors (EF) were mostly based on field studies in Ontario. This study was part of a large project that aimed to assess the environmental impact of GHG mitigation strategies based on farm-scale emissions studies (AGGP Project: Farm-scale Assessment of GHG Mitigation Strategies in Dairy Livestock-Cropping-systems). While this study focused on long term emissions from the operational AD facility in Ontario, Cambareri et al. (2017) and Schwager et al. (2016) measured nitrogen losses (leaching and emissions) from field application of untreated manure and digestate from the same facility. The goal was to have a specific, reliable and robust farm-scale dataset available for the LCA study. When local data were not available, EF from other studies or database were used. Emission
factors for the avoided products were based on current Canadian practices.

Total annual CH4 emissions per volume from manure and digestate storage were mea- sured year-round at the research site using the micrometeorological mass bass method (Chapter 3). Direct N2O emissions from storage was calculated based on the TKN content for manure and digestate multiplied by an emissions factor (Wood et al., 2014) (Table 4.5, Table 4.14, Table 4.15). The same emission factor was applied for both cases, since studies measuring N2O emission from manure and digestate storages concluded that there is no statistically significant difference between them (Amon et al., 2006; Rodhe et al., 2015). The ammonia emission factor from manure was taken from Wood et al. (2014). For diges- tate the EF was calculated by multiplying the manure EF value by 1.5 (Hou et al., 2017). The indirect N2O from manure and digestate storage was calculated using the IPCC 2006 emission factor (EF = 0.01 kg kg-1 (NH3-N+NO-N)) and considering the NH3 emission calculated for manure and digestate, respectively. Nitrogen monoxide and nitrogen dioxide (NOx) was calculated based on Pehme (2013)'s EF multiplied by untreated manure and digestate TAN (Table 4.5).
Table 4.5: Emission factors for the unit processes directly involved with the systems.

Unit process|Emission|Parameters|Emission factors|Emission factors|units|Source Unit process|Emission|Parameters|Conv|AD|units|Source Storage|CH4|volume|6.6|5.6||Chapter 3 Storage|N2Odirect|TKN|4.38|4.38|g kg-1 TKN y-1|Wood et al., 2014 Storage|N2OindirectbyNvolatilization|NH3-N+NO-N|0.01|0.01|kg kg-1 (NH3-N+NOx-N)|IPCC, 2006 Storage|NH3|TKN|21.5|32.3|g kg-1 y-1|Wood et al., 2014; Hou et al., 2017 Storage|NO|TAN|0.0001|0.0001|kg kg-1 TAN|Pehme, 2013 Transport and spreading|CO2eq|volume|3.48|3.48|kg m--3|Ecoinvent 3 database |N2Odirect|TKN|0.02|0.03|g kg- Napplied|Cambareri et al., 2017 |N2OindirectbyNvolatilization|NH3-N+NO-N|0.01|0.01|kg kg-1 (NH3-N+NO-N)|IPCC, 2006 "Soil emissions after land application"|N2OindirectbyNeaching|Nleaching|0.0075|0.0075|kg kg-1 Nleaching|IPCC, 2006 "Soil emissions after land application"|NH3|TAN|25.8|25.8|% of TAN|Evans, 2015 "Soil emissions after land application"|NO|N2Odirect|0.1|0.1|kg kg-1 N2Odirect|Pehme, 2013 "Soil emissions after land application"|NO3 leaching|TKN|1.1|5|% of total Napplied|Schwager et al., 2016 IFW transport|CO2eq|tkm||0.0934|kg tkm-1|USLCI database Anaerobic Digester fugitive emissions|CH4|CH4produced||3|% CH4produced|Ebner et al., 2015 Cogenerator unit|CH4|CH4utilized||2.5|% CH4utilized|Ebner et al., 2015 Cogenerator unit|N2O|CH4utilized||0.03|kg m -3 CH4utilized|Ebner et al., 2015

The storage period before land application was assumed equal to 240 d, the minimum storage capacity required in Ontario (OMAFRA, 2010). For AD, this period was subtracted from the 60-d hydraulic retention time (when input was stored in the reactors), resulting in 180 d of open storage of digestate.

The CO2eq emission from fuel consumption for land application of manure or digestate was taken from the Ecoinvent 3 database library (3.48 kg CO2eq m-3 and multiplied by the volume of manure and digestate at the end of the storage period, respectively. No fuel consumption was assigned to transportation between storage and on-farm field application due to the short distances (< 10 km).

Direct N2O emission factors for manure and digestate application were taken from Cambareri et al. (2017) who measured field N20 emissions of manure and digestate taken from the study site and applied on pilot-scale plots at a location ~30 km from the dairy farm (Table 4.5). Significant differences in emissions were found only for surface broadcasting and incorporation (SBI) method, with increased emissions from digestate application; the SBI was chosen, conservatively. The EFs were multiplied by TKN applied in each case to get the total direct N2O emission. The NH3 emissions were calculated using the manure EF measured in Ontario dairy farms (Evans, 2015) and multiplied by TAN in manure and di- gestate applied. Nitrogen loss by NO3 leaching was calculated by multiplying the EF from a field study in Ontario (Schwager et al., 2016), considering total N applied as manure and digestate. An EF of 0.75% of N leaching was used to calculate indirect N2O emissions by NO leaching. Indirect N20 emissions by volatilization were calculated as for storage

The emission factors (EF) were mostly based on field studies in Ontario. This study was part of a large project that aimed to assess the environmental impact of GHG mitigation strategies based on farm-scale emissions studies (AGGP Project: Farm-scale Assessment of GHG Mitigation Strategies in Dairy Livestock-Cropping-systems). While this study focused on long term emissions from the operational AD facility in Ontario, Cambareri et al. (2017) and Schwager et al. (2016) measured nitrogen losses (leaching and emissions) from field application of untreated manure and digestate from the same facility. The goal was to have a specific, reliable and robust farm-scale dataset available for the LCA study. When local data were not available, EF from other studies or database were used. Emission
factors for the avoided products were based on current Canadian practices.

Total annual CH4 emissions per volume from manure and digestate storage were mea- sured year-round at the research site using the micrometeorological mass bass method (Chapter 3). Direct N2O emissions from storage was calculated based on the TKN content for manure and digestate multiplied by an emissions factor (Wood et al., 2014) (Table 4.5, Table 4.14, Table 4.15). The same emission factor was applied for both cases, since studies measuring N2O emission from manure and digestate storages concluded that there is no statistically significant difference between them (Amon et al., 2006; Rodhe et al., 2015). The ammonia emission factor from manure was taken from Wood et al. (2014). For diges- tate the EF was calculated by multiplying the manure EF value by 1.5 (Hou et al., 2017). The indirect N2O from manure and digestate storage was calculated using the IPCC 2006 emission factor (EF = 0.01 kg kg-1 (NH3-N+NO-N)) and considering the NH3 emission calculated for manure and digestate, respectively. Nitrogen monoxide and nitrogen dioxide (NOx) was calculated based on Pehme (2013)'s EF multiplied by untreated manure and digestate TAN (Table 4.5).
Table 4.5: Emission factors for the unit processes directly involved with the systems.

Unit process|Emission|Parameters|Emission factors|Emission factors|units|Source Unit process|Emission|Parameters|Conv|AD|units|Source Storage|CH4|volume|6.6|5.6||Chapter 3 Storage|N2Odirect|TKN|4.38|4.38|g kg-1 TKN y-1|Wood et al., 2014 Storage|N2OindirectbyNvolatilization|NH3-N+NO-N|0.01|0.01|kg kg-1 (NH3-N+NOx-N)|IPCC, 2006 Storage|NH3|TKN|21.5|32.3|g kg-1 y-1|Wood et al., 2014; Hou et al., 2017 Storage|NO|TAN|0.0001|0.0001|kg kg-1 TAN|Pehme, 2013 Transport and spreading|CO2eq|volume|3.48|3.48|kg m--3|Ecoinvent 3 database |N2Odirect|TKN|0.02|0.03|g kg- Napplied|Cambareri et al., 2017 |N2OindirectbyNvolatilization|NH3-N+NO-N|0.01|0.01|kg kg-1 (NH3-N+NO-N)|IPCC, 2006 "Soil emissions after land application"|N2OindirectbyNeaching|Nleaching|0.0075|0.0075|kg kg-1 Nleaching|IPCC, 2006 "Soil emissions after land application"|NH3|TAN|25.8|25.8|% of TAN|Evans, 2015 "Soil emissions after land application"|NO|N2Odirect|0.1|0.1|kg kg-1 N2Odirect|Pehme, 2013 "Soil emissions after land application"|NO3 leaching|TKN|1.1|5|% of total Napplied|Schwager et al., 2016 IFW transport|CO2eq|tkm||0.0934|kg tkm-1|USLCI database Anaerobic Digester fugitive emissions|CH4|CH4produced||3|% CH4produced|Ebner et al., 2015 Cogenerator unit|CH4|CH4utilized||2.5|% CH4utilized|Ebner et al., 2015 Cogenerator unit|N2O|CH4utilized||0.03|kg m -3 CH4utilized|Ebner et al., 2015

The storage period before land application was assumed equal to 240 d, the minimum storage capacity required in Ontario (OMAFRA, 2010). For AD, this period was subtracted from the 60-d hydraulic retention time (when input was stored in the reactors), resulting in 180 d of open storage of digestate.

The CO2eq emission from fuel consumption for land application of manure or digestate was taken from the Ecoinvent 3 database library (3.48 kg CO2eq m-3 and multiplied by the volume of manure and digestate at the end of the storage period, respectively. No fuel consumption was assigned to transportation between storage and on-farm field application due to the short distances (< 10 km).

Direct N2O emission factors for manure and digestate application were taken from Cambareri et al. (2017) who measured field N20 emissions of manure and digestate taken from the study site and applied on pilot-scale plots at a location ~30 km from the dairy farm (Table 4.5). Significant differences in emissions were found only for surface broadcasting and incorporation (SBI) method, with increased emissions from digestate application; the SBI was chosen, conservatively. The EFs were multiplied by TKN applied in each case to get the total direct N2O emission. The NH3 emissions were calculated using the manure EF measured in Ontario dairy farms (Evans, 2015) and multiplied by TAN in manure and di- gestate applied. Nitrogen loss by NO3 leaching was calculated by multiplying the EF from a field study in Ontario (Schwager et al., 2016), considering total N applied as manure and digestate. An EF of 0.75% of N leaching was used to calculate indirect N2O emissions by NO leaching. Indirect N20 emissions by volatilization were calculated as for storage

170: For the AD treatment, the IFW transport impact was included. The EF of 0.0934 kg CO2eq tkm-1 from the USLCI database was used considering an average distance of 100 km round trip and the total mass of IFW delivered (Table 4.3). A sensitivity analysis was done considering half (50 km) and double (200 km) the arbitrary distance.

Fugitive emissions from the anaerobic digesters should be nearly negligible when the system works properly, since it is a gas-tight system from the biogas capture to the co- generator. However, CH4 leaks are expected during maintenance openings or during pres- sure release events through the pressure relief valves located in the digester covers. The EF from the digester is expressed in terms of percentage of CH4 produced and can be highly variable depending of the system design and operation, varying from negligible to 26% (Debruyn, 2017). The IPCC default value for leakage from a digester is 10%, but most studies found much lower values from 0.6 to 2.8% (Liebetrau et al., 2013). In this study, 3% was assumed based on North America studies (Ebner et al., 2015) and scenarios with 1 and 10% of fugitive emissions were evaluated in the sensitivity analysis. The CO2 emissions from the biogas are considered to be a replacement of the CO2 uptake during crop produc- tion of animal feed, therefore, were not included. Ammonia and N2O emissions from the digester were considered negligible based on Liebetrau et al. (2013) who found low NH3 emissions and negligible N2O emission from digesters in 10 biogas facilities within the agricultural sector.
Emissions from gas slips or incomplete combustion in the cogeneration unit were con-

sidered the second most important GHG source in biogas plants after digestate storage (Liebetrau et al., 2013). Ebner et al. (2015) measured an EF of 2.5% of the CH4 utilized in 7 biogas plants in the USA, within the range reported by Liebetrau et al. (2013) (0.4 - 3.3%). In this study, CH4 fugitive emission from the co-generator was assumed to be 2.5% of the CH4 utilized. The CH4 utilized is the CH4 produced minus the fugitive emission from the digesters. The N2O emission factor for the co-generator was assumed to be 0.03 kg N2O m-3 CH4 utilized (Ebner et al., 2015).

A sensitivity analysis was done considering half (50 km) and double (200 km) the arbitrary distance.

173: <header>4.2.4 Life cycle inventory of off-site emissions (avoided emissions) </header>

The avoided processes included in the analysis were: grid electricity provision, IFW conventional treatments (landfilling and composting) and inorganic fertilizer (production and transport to the field, assuming a 50 km distance). A sensitivity analysis for 25 and 100 km was done.

The electricity fuel mix in Ontario is mostly based on nuclear (< 60%), hydroelectric (< 20%) energy sources, with < 10% derived from fossil fuels (Table 4.6, Table 4.16). The carbon intensity impact per kWh (CIPK) was 83.7 kg CO2eq kWh-1, calculated considering the average reported CIPK for years 2010-2014 - (Canadian Energy Issues, 2017), when the field data were collected (Table 4.7). There is a decreasing trend of the CIPK in Ontario due to increased wind and solar energy use replacing oil and gas. To account for the worst-case scenario, a sensitivity analysis was done using the lowest impact (38.2 kg CO2eq kWh-1,
Table 4.6: Ontario electricity grid data mix in 2014.
Fuel|Electricity delivered (%) Nuclear|61.8 Hydro|23.7 Wind|4.4 Natural gas|9.1 Oil & Gas|0.1 Biofuel|0 Solar|0.8 Coal|0.1
Table 4.7: Carbon intensity impact per kWh (CIPK) in Ontario.

The current IFW treatment displaced was assumed to be mainly landfilling (70%) with the remaining amount of IFW sent to composting (30%). A sensitivity analysis evaluated different scenarios of IFW treatments. The EF from IFW landfilling and composting were 0.555 (assuming 70% landfill CH4 was captured) and 0.07 kg CO2eq kg-lorganic waste, respectively (Table 4.8). The solid manure stockpile and land application was also replaced by the AD treatment and their impacts included in the analysis (Table 4.8). The emission factor for the stockpile was calculated averaging the summer emission data from Pattey et al. (2005) and the winter emission data from Ahn et al. (2011).

The last stage of the life cycle of the manure treatment systems is land application of manure/digestate as organic fertilizer. This use replaces the production and transport to the field of inorganic fertilizers. The inorganic fertilizer avoided was assumed to be urea transported 50 km, with EF of 3.29 kg CO2eq tkm-1 from the USLCI database (Table 4.8). A sensitivity analysis was done to evaluate, separately, the effect of the transport distance (25 and 100 km).

177: Sensitivity analyses were done by changing one parameter at time and evaluating the impact of the change in the overall result. Unit process|Parameter|LCA|Sensitivity analysis|unit Grid electricity|EF (CIPK)|0.0837|0.0382|kg kWh-Superscript(1) "IFW landfilling:composting"|ratio|70:30:00|50:50|ratio "IFW landfilling:composting"|ratio|70:30:00|30:70|ratio "IFW landfilling:composting"|ratio|70:30:00|10:90|ratio IFW transport|distance|100|50|km IFW transport|distance|100|200|km Inorganic fertilizer|distance|50|25|km Inorganic fertilizer|distance|50|100|km "Anaerobic Digester fugitive emissions"|CH4 produced|3|1|%CH4 produced "Anaerobic Digester fugitive emissions"|CH4 produced|3|10|%CH4 produced Storage|EF|5.6|1.1| Storage|EF|5.6|6.9| Storage|EF|5.6|11.2|
The two main emission sources in the AD system, the fugitive emission from the anaer- obic digesters and the open storage, were also considered for the sensitivity analysis. The

potential benefit impact from the AD system was calculated by changing the EF of CH4 from the digesters, from 3% to 1% (Miranda et al., 2015) and 10% (IPCC, 2006) of CH4 produced. The storage emissions was modeled using the EF obtained at the same facility in the first year of the system operation (Chapter 2), the EF from the other Canadian on-farm study (Baldé et al., 2016b) and an EF twice the value used in the model (Table 4.9).

179: The impact categories affected by the manure management systems were climate change (CO2eq), terrestrial acidification (SO2eq), marine eutrophication (Neq), photochemical oxi- dant formation (NMVOC) and particulate matter formation (PM10eq). The target impact category of this study was climate change, but these other categories are affected by the CH4, NH3, NO and NO as well (Table 4.10). The total impact of each category was not presented because the emission inventory focused on the GHG only. The main objective here was to identify other impact categories affected and the substances that contribute for it.

180: For the conventional system, the net GWP impact was 158 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure, as a result of 170 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure emitted and -11 kg CO2eq t-11i manure avoided due to displaced inorganic fertilizer. The main source of emissions was manure storage (81%, 137 kg CO2eq t-11q manure), followed by soil emissions after manure application (17%, 29 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure) and fuel consumption in transport and spreading of manure (2%, 3 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure) (Figure 4.3). The main GHG contributor for the overall impact was CH4 (132 kg CO2eq; 80%) followed by N20 (34 kg CO2eq; 20%) (Table 4.11).

182: For the biodigester system, the net GHG impact was 126 kg CO2eq t-11q manure, from the difference between the GHG emitted (354 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure) and GHG emissions avoided due to processes replaced by the AD (-228 kg CO2eq t-11q manure). The emission contributors were 31% (109 kg CO2eq t-11i manure) from digestate storage, 28% (97 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure) from soil after digestate land application, 22% (77 kg CO2eq

t-11iq manure) as fugitive emission from the biodigester and 18% (63 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure) from CHP unit. Fuel consumption in digestate transport and land application and IFW transport accounted for ~ 1% of the GHG emissions (Figure 4.4). The environmental benefit (emissions from avoided processes) came from: avoidance of IFW landfilling (- 51%, - -117 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure), followed by avoidance of solid manure stockpiling and land application (37%, -85 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure), displaced grid electricity (5%, - 11 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure), displaced inorganic fertilizer (4%, -9 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure) and IFW composting avoided (3%,-6 kg CO2eq 1 t-11iq manure).
Figure 4.4: Anaerobic digestion system impact network.

The AD treatment reduced GHG emissions from the open storage by 20% (Table 4.12) despite the larger volume. However, there are additional important emission sources of GHG in the farm with the AD system implementation (reactors and CHP unit) impacting the site negatively (Figure 4.5). Furthermore, AD significantly increased GHG emissions from soil after land application (>3x), slightly increased emissions from transport and spreading due to the larger volume and decreased environmental benefit of displaced in- organic fertilizer. The total emission from the common processes increased 27% and the on-site overall GHG emission was twice as much for AD treatment (Figure 4.6).
Table 4.12: GHG emission per process (in kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure) in processes common in both systems.

Process (kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure)|Conventional system|AD system|AD impact effect on GWP|AD impact effect on GWP Storage|137|109|-20%|decrease Transport and spreading|3|4|23%|increase Soil emissions after land application|29|97||increase Displaced inorganic fertilizer production and transport|-11|-9|-20%|decrease Total|158|201|27%|increase

Considering the benefits of the AD system (the avoided processes off-site), the system reduced by 20% the GHG emissions from the conventional manure treatment. Aguirre- Villegas and Larson (2017) modeling a typical dairy farm in Wisconsin, WI, USA, found a 48% GHG emission reduction by AD compared to the conventional practice, mostly
Displaced solid manure stockpile and land application
Displaced inorganic fertilizer production and transport to store

Figure 4.5: GHG emissions per process in both systems (kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure). from lower CH4 and N2O emissions during storage and land application and avoided fossil fuel CO2 emission due to production of electricity from biogas. Ebner et al. (2015) found the AD system reduced GHG emissions by 71% compared to the conventional system for an on-farm biogas facility in New York, NY, USA, mainly due to displaced of grid electricity, followed by the avoidance of IFW disposal and reduced emission from storage. In Ontario, the GHG emissions from the provision of grid electricity is one of the lowest in the world, due to the predominant use of nuclear (< 60%) and hydroeletric (< 20%) power and renewable energy sources (wind and solar). The impact for electricity provision ranges widely around the world (7 to 1130 g CO2eq kWh-1, Moller et al. (2009) and the larger the impact of the electricity provision in a region, the larger the benefit of AD, like
Figure 4.6: GHG emissions occurrence (on and off-site) in both systems (kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure).
in Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) and Ebner et al. (2015).

The emission reduction in terms of CO2eq t-1input waste, 22 kg CO2eq t-1 input waste, was comparable to the reduction found in Ebner et al. (2015) and Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) (37.5 and 48.6 kg CO2eq t-1input waste, respectively). Considering the volume of waste treated annually, the AD resulted in 305 t CO2eq y-1 emission reduction in the Canadian biogas facility and 4,512t CO2eq y-1 in the American biogas facility studied in Ebner et al. (2015).
The main GHG was CH4, which was 86% higher in the AD system, the N2O emission also increased in the AD system (53%), but the CO2 avoided dramatically compensate for

these emission increases (Table 4.11). In Ebner et al. (2015), CH4 was also the larger source of GHG emissions, followed by N20. However, CH4 contributed to 164 and 88 kg CO2e 2eq t-1input waste in our case study for AD and conventional systems, respectively, but only 48 and 63 kg CO2eq t-1input waste in Ebner et al.'s (2015). Similarly, N2O emissions direct and indirect were 34 and 22 kg CO2eq t-1input waste in our study for AD and conventional systems, respectively, but 21 and 18 kg CO2eq t-1input waste in Ebner et al.'s (2015). Here it is important to highlight the reliability of our emission factors based on large-scale long- term studies as opposed to the emission factors predicted in Ebner et al. (2015), which were calculated using IPCC methodology.

Annually, the amount of waste treated on the facility is 9,235 ton, on average (Table 4.1). Considering this amount, the replacement of the conventional practice by the AD technology can potentially reduce 2.0 t CO2eq cow-1 y-1 from the impact of the conven- tional system (9.1 t CO2eq cow-1 y-1). A manure treatment system is usually built to have a large lifespan; assuming 20 years, the environmental benefit would be 6.1 Mt CO2eq that would not be emitted.

Annually, the amount of waste treated on the facility is 9,235 ton, on average (Table 4.1). Considering this amount, the replacement of the conventional practice by the AD technology can potentially reduce 2.0 t CO2eq cow-1 y-1 from the impact of the conven- tional system (9.1 t CO2eq cow-1 y-1). A manure treatment system is usually built to have a large lifespan; assuming 20 years, the environmental benefit would be 6.1 Mt CO2eq that would not be emitted.
184: For the biodigester system, the net GHG impact was 126 kg CO2eq t-11q manure, from the difference between the GHG emitted (354 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure) and GHG emissions avoided due to processes replaced by the AD (-228 kg CO2eq t-11q manure). The emission contributors were 31% (109 kg CO2eq t-11i manure) from digestate storage, 28% (97 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure) from soil after digestate land application, 22% (77 kg CO2eq

t-11iq manure) as fugitive emission from the biodigester and 18% (63 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure) from CHP unit. Fuel consumption in digestate transport and land application and IFW transport accounted for ~ 1% of the GHG emissions (Figure 4.4). The environmental benefit (emissions from avoided processes) came from: avoidance of IFW landfilling (- 51%, - -117 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure), followed by avoidance of solid manure stockpiling and land application (37%, -85 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure), displaced grid electricity (5%, - 11 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure), displaced inorganic fertilizer (4%, -9 kg CO2eq t-11iq manure) and IFW composting avoided (3%,-6 kg CO2eq 1 t-11iq manure).
Figure 4.4: Anaerobic digestion system impact network.

The AD treatment reduced GHG emissions from the open storage by 20% (Table 4.12) despite the larger volume. However, there are additional important emission sources of GHG in the farm with the AD system implementation (reactors and CHP unit) impacting the site negatively (Figure 4.5). Furthermore, AD significantly increased GHG emissions from soil after land application (>3x), slightly increased emissions from transport and spreading due to the larger volume and decreased environmental benefit of displaced in- organic fertilizer. The total emission from the common processes increased 27% and the on-site overall GHG emission was twice as much for AD treatment (Figure 4.6).
Table 4.12: GHG emission per process (in kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure) in processes common in both systems.

Process (kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure)|Conventional system|AD system|AD impact effect on GWP|AD impact effect on GWP Storage|137|109|-20%|decrease Transport and spreading|3|4|23%|increase Soil emissions after land application|29|97||increase Displaced inorganic fertilizer production and transport|-11|-9|-20%|decrease Total|158|201|27%|increase

Considering the benefits of the AD system (the avoided processes off-site), the system reduced by 20% the GHG emissions from the conventional manure treatment. Aguirre- Villegas and Larson (2017) modeling a typical dairy farm in Wisconsin, WI, USA, found a 48% GHG emission reduction by AD compared to the conventional practice, mostly
Displaced solid manure stockpile and land application
Displaced inorganic fertilizer production and transport to store

Figure 4.5: GHG emissions per process in both systems (kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure). from lower CH4 and N2O emissions during storage and land application and avoided fossil fuel CO2 emission due to production of electricity from biogas. Ebner et al. (2015) found the AD system reduced GHG emissions by 71% compared to the conventional system for an on-farm biogas facility in New York, NY, USA, mainly due to displaced of grid electricity, followed by the avoidance of IFW disposal and reduced emission from storage. In Ontario, the GHG emissions from the provision of grid electricity is one of the lowest in the world, due to the predominant use of nuclear (< 60%) and hydroeletric (< 20%) power and renewable energy sources (wind and solar). The impact for electricity provision ranges widely around the world (7 to 1130 g CO2eq kWh-1, Moller et al. (2009) and the larger the impact of the electricity provision in a region, the larger the benefit of AD, like
Figure 4.6: GHG emissions occurrence (on and off-site) in both systems (kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure).
in Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) and Ebner et al. (2015).

The emission reduction in terms of CO2eq t-1input waste, 22 kg CO2eq t-1 input waste, was comparable to the reduction found in Ebner et al. (2015) and Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) (37.5 and 48.6 kg CO2eq t-1input waste, respectively). Considering the volume of waste treated annually, the AD resulted in 305 t CO2eq y-1 emission reduction in the Canadian biogas facility and 4,512t CO2eq y-1 in the American biogas facility studied in Ebner et al. (2015).
The main GHG was CH4, which was 86% higher in the AD system, the N2O emission also increased in the AD system (53%), but the CO2 avoided dramatically compensate for

these emission increases (Table 4.11). In Ebner et al. (2015), CH4 was also the larger source of GHG emissions, followed by N20. However, CH4 contributed to 164 and 88 kg CO2e 2eq t-1input waste in our case study for AD and conventional systems, respectively, but only 48 and 63 kg CO2eq t-1input waste in Ebner et al.'s (2015). Similarly, N2O emissions direct and indirect were 34 and 22 kg CO2eq t-1input waste in our study for AD and conventional systems, respectively, but 21 and 18 kg CO2eq t-1input waste in Ebner et al.'s (2015). Here it is important to highlight the reliability of our emission factors based on large-scale long- term studies as opposed to the emission factors predicted in Ebner et al. (2015), which were calculated using IPCC methodology.

However, there are additional important emission sources of GHG in the farm with the AD system implementation (reactors and CHP unit) impacting the site negatively (Figure 4.5). Furthermore, AD significantly increased GHG emissions from soil after land application (>3x), slightly increased emissions from transport and spreading due to the larger volume and decreased environmental benefit of displaced in- organic fertilizer. The total emission from the common processes increased 27% and the on-site overall GHG emission was twice as much for AD treatment (Figure 4.6).
Table 4.12: GHG emission per process (in kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure) in processes common in both systems.

Process (kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure)|Conventional system|AD system|AD impact effect on GWP|AD impact effect on GWP Storage|137|109|-20%|decrease Transport and spreading|3|4|23%|increase Soil emissions after land application|29|97||increase Displaced inorganic fertilizer production and transport|-11|-9|-20%|decrease Total|158|201|27%|increase

Considering the benefits of the AD system (the avoided processes off-site), the system reduced by 20% the GHG emissions from the conventional manure treatment. Aguirre- Villegas and Larson (2017) modeling a typical dairy farm in Wisconsin, WI, USA, found a 48% GHG emission reduction by AD compared to the conventional practice, mostly
Displaced solid manure stockpile and land application
Displaced inorganic fertilizer production and transport to store

Figure 4.5: GHG emissions per process in both systems (kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure). from lower CH4 and N2O emissions during storage and land application and avoided fossil fuel CO2 emission due to production of electricity from biogas. Ebner et al. (2015) found the AD system reduced GHG emissions by 71% compared to the conventional system for an on-farm biogas facility in New York, NY, USA, mainly due to displaced of grid electricity, followed by the avoidance of IFW disposal and reduced emission from storage. In Ontario, the GHG emissions from the provision of grid electricity is one of the lowest in the world, due to the predominant use of nuclear (< 60%) and hydroeletric (< 20%) power and renewable energy sources (wind and solar). The impact for electricity provision ranges widely around the world (7 to 1130 g CO2eq kWh-1, Moller et al. (2009) and the larger the impact of the electricity provision in a region, the larger the benefit of AD, like
Figure 4.6: GHG emissions occurrence (on and off-site) in both systems (kg CO2eq t-1 liq manure).
in Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) and Ebner et al. (2015).

The emission reduction in terms of CO2eq t-1input waste, 22 kg CO2eq t-1 input waste, was comparable to the reduction found in Ebner et al. (2015) and Aguirre-Villegas and Larson (2017) (37.5 and 48.6 kg CO2eq t-1input waste, respectively). Considering the volume of waste treated annually, the AD resulted in 305 t CO2eq y-1 emission reduction in the Canadian biogas facility and 4,512t CO2eq y-1 in the American biogas facility studied in Ebner et al. (2015).
The main GHG was CH4, which was 86% higher in the AD system, the N2O emission also increased in the AD system (53%), but the CO2 avoided dramatically compensate for

these emission increases (Table 4.11). In Ebner et al. (2015), CH4 was also the larger source of GHG emissions, followed by N20. However, CH4 contributed to 164 and 88 kg CO2e 2eq t-1input waste in our case study for AD and conventional systems, respectively, but only 48 and 63 kg CO2eq t-1input waste in Ebner et al.'s (2015). Similarly, N2O emissions direct and indirect were 34 and 22 kg CO2eq t-1input waste in our study for AD and conventional systems, respectively, but 21 and 18 kg CO2eq t-1input waste in Ebner et al.'s (2015). Here it is important to highlight the reliability of our emission factors based on large-scale long- term studies as opposed to the emission factors predicted in Ebner et al. (2015), which were calculated using IPCC methodology.

Annually, the amount of waste treated on the facility is 9,235 ton, on average (Table 4.1). Considering this amount, the replacement of the conventional practice by the AD technology can potentially reduce 2.0 t CO2eq cow-1 y-1 from the impact of the conven- tional system (9.1 t CO2eq cow-1 y-1). A manure treatment system is usually built to have a large lifespan; assuming 20 years, the environmental benefit would be 6.1 Mt CO2eq that would not be emitted.
190: <header>4.3.3 Sensitivity analysis and opportunities to increase AD efficiency </header>

The overall GHG emissions from the AD system increased only 5% when modelling an electricity country mix scenario with the lowest CIPK (2014), remaining as an alter- native to reduce GHG from manure treatment. The transportation distances also had low impact on results (< 3%). The parameters that had the greatest influence on the potential
GHG environmental reduction achieved by replacing conventional manure systems with AD systems were: digestate storage, fugitive emissions from the anaerobic digesters and IFW treatment (Table 4.13).
Table 4.13: Sensitivity analysis results. Treatment effect is the overall GHG emission effect comparing the results with the impact of conventional manure treatment.

Unit process|Parameter|LCA|unit|Sensitivity analysis|delta paremeter|delta effect AD|treatment effect Grid electricity|EF (CIPK)|0.0837|kg kWh-¹|0.0382|-54%|5%|-17% "IFW landfilling:composting"|ratio|70:30:00|ratio|50:50||23%|-2% "IFW landfilling:composting"|ratio|70:30:00|ratio|30:70||47%|17% "IFW landfilling:composting"|ratio|70:30:00|ratio|10:90||70%|35% IFW transport|distance|100|km|50|-50%|-1%|-21% IFW transport|distance|100|km|200|100%|3%|-19% Inorganic fertilizer|distance|50|km|25|-50%|0%|-20% Inorganic fertilizer|distance|50|km|100|100%|0%|-20% "Anaerobic Digester fugitive emissions"|CH4 produced|3|%CH4 produced|1|-67%|-41%|-53% "Anaerobic Digester fugitive emissions"|CH4 produced|3|%CH4 produced|10|233%|143%|93% Storage|EF|5.6|kg m-3 y-1|1.1|-80%|-67%|-74% Storage|EF|5.6|kg m-3 y-1|6.9|23%|19%|-5% Storage|EF|5.6|kg m-3 y-1|11.2|100%|85%|47%
* Increased GHG emissions for AD systems replacing conventional systems

For the IFW scenarios, the higher the percentage of IFW landfilling, the larger the ben- efit of AD (Table 4.2). A 50% landfilling of IFW offset the AD GHG emission reduction, and above that value, the AD started to emit more GHG than the conventional system. If 90% of IFW is being composted and only 10% landfilled, the AD system increased the overall GHG emissions in 35%.

Considering the digestate emission factor from the first year of the AD system oper- ation (Chapter 2), the potential GHG emission reduction when comparing to the conven- tional system was > 70%. Assuming the EF from Baldé et al. (2016b), which was ~ 20% higher than the operational year in our study site and similar to the emission from untreated manure in our site before the AD installation, there was only 5% of GHG reduction from the conventional manure treatment. When the EF for digestate storage was doubled, the overall GHG impact was 50% higher for the AD scenario (Table 4.12). Varying + 10% the EF from storage represented a + 8% variation of the AD system potential environmental impact.

The fugitive emissions from the anaerobic digesters were the second parameter with major influence on the overall impact. A + 10% variation in the fugitive emissions EF re- sulted in + 6% variation of the AD system potential environmental impact. Assuming the IPCC (2006) default value of 10% leakage from the digesters, the overall impact was ac- tually ~ 30% higher than the impact calculated for the conventional manure system (Table 4.12).
This study showed that AD systems can potentially reduce GHG emissions from the

conventional manure treatment and, for that reason, should be promoted as a beneficial environmental alternative for farmers. However, care should be taken when operating a on- farm biogas facility, because the fugitive emissions form the biodigesters, the co-generation unit and digestate storage can easily offset the potential environmental benefit. Keeping a routine of system maintenance and monitoring could decrease the losses from the digesters and co-generation unit and help to increase the efficiency of the system. Regular solid- separation of digestate, longer HRT and digestate storage cover and capturing of residual emissions could reduce the losses from the storage tank. The CH4 emissions from the digestate storage tank indicated that there was a significant loss occurring and capturing these emissions might be profitable for the farmer to increase electricity grid income, and also beneficial to the environment.

195: The AD of manure is an effective mitigation strategy to reduce potential environmen- tal impact from manure management systems, since it reduced the emission by 20% per mass of liquid manure in the commercial biogas facility studied. There was a significant increase (2x) in the on-site GHG emissions in an AD facility compared to a farm with con- ventional manure management practice. The overall GHG environmental impact, however, was smaller for the AD treatment, considering the processes avoided by the system (IFW disposal, grid electricity, solid manure stockpile and land application).

There are opportunities to increase AD efficiency and the environmental benefit of the technology. The main emission sources were the digestate storage, the anaerobic digesters and slips from the co-generator unit. Storage covers to recover CH4 losses have been stud- ied to mitigate emissions from storage and to increase AD facility revenue. Solid-separation of digestate not only mitigates storage emissions, but provides barn bedding material as a by-product. Regular maintenance and proper AD design would mitigate emissions from the reactors and co-generation unit.

If the conventional liquid manure treatment avoided is added as an input to the LCA analysis, the overall impact of the AD is positive, which means the AD system alone reduce GHG emission. Undoubtedly, the co-digestion of manure and IFW is a practice that should be encouraged and financially supported by the governmental agencies to mitigate GHG emissions and climate change effect. It is important, however, to keep a routine of checking on the biogas facilities operation focusing on detecting indicators of fugitive emission.

0: <title>Conclusions and recommendations </title>

This study had an overall goal to assess the potential environmental benefit of anaerobic digestion as an alternative to mitigate GHG emissions compared to conventional manage- ment practice of manure storage and to identify the key parameters driving CH4 emissions from digestate storage.

In Chapter 2, a year-round field study on CH4 emissions from digestate storage showed that the anaerobic digestion significantly reduced emissions as compared to untreated ma- nure in the first year of AD operation. The total annual CH4 emission from the storage (1.0 kg CH4 m-3 y-1) was significantly lower than before the biodigestion treatment (6.6 kg CH4 m-3 y-1): 85% lower by volume, and 65% lower by mass of VS, suggesting that the VS in the digestate was less digestible than the VS form in the untreated manure. The main factor driving emissions was digestate temperature, especially at 2 m depth (r = 0.98, p < 0.001). Differently from manure, digestate emissions were not correlated to VS concentra-

In Chapter 3, a change in the AD recipe with the addition of increased proportion of IFW rich in fat, oil and grease (chicken meat waste, onions and bananas and beef processing sludge) lead to an increase in CH4 emission per volume (5.6 kg to levels similar to observed for untreated manure. The VS concentration in the digestate in the operational years (28 g L-1 was similar to the first year (25 g L-1), but the digestate temperature was much higher, causing the higher emissions. The highest emissions per VS were observed in the operational years (18.2+13.0 g kg-1 VS month-1), even higher than for untreated manure (6.3+6.2 g kg-1 VS month-1), suggesting the presence of easily digestible form of VS with the IFW addition. This observation also suggested that the 60-d HRT was not enough to completely digest the new input. Although there was no CH4 emission reduction from the storage in the operational year scenario, to assess the overall environmental impact of the AD, a broad approach was necessary.

In Chapter 4, a LCA comparing the AD with the conventional manure system showed that the anaerobic co-digestion of manure and IFW reduced the GHG emission by 20% per mass of liquid manure, representing a GHG reduction of 2.0 t CO2eq cow- y-1. De- spite the whole farm emission increase, the benefit came from the displacement of current waste treatment, especially IFW landfilling, solid manure stockpiling and land application and grid electricity provision. A sensitivity analysis indicated that waste scenarios dis- placed where landfilling is the major disposal option, AD was always a beneficial practice to mitigate GHG emissions. The LCA also showed that there is potential to increase AD ef-
ficiency and environmental benefits by adopting means to reduce CH4 losses from digestate and fugitive emissions from the digesters and co-generation unit.
In conclusion, the main findings of this work are:

<list>Temperature, especially at 2 m depth in the storage tank is the key CH4 emissions driver for digestate storage; 
Differently than manure, VS content is not a reliable parameter to predict CH4 emis- sions from digestate; 
AD could reduce CH4 emissions from manure storage per volume by > 80% and overall GHG from conventional manure treatment by > 70% (LCA); 
AD increased storage temperature as compared to conventional practice, the addition of IFW without changing operational parameters, such as HRT, increased CH4 losses from the digestate storage; 
CH4 emissions from digestate in the same order of magnitude than untreated manure, AD emission did not significantly reduce overall GHG emissions; 
There are still opportunities to increase AD environmental benefit (and revenue), by reducing CH4 losses from the digesters, co-generation unit and storage tank.</list>

Therefore, the AD of manure is an effective mitigation strategy to reduce potential en- vironmental impact from manure management systems. There should be governmental incentives to replacement of the current manure management systems to AD systems to

mitigate Canada's GHG emissions and its climate change effect. To assure the environ- mental benefit, the government should also implement a routine of checking on the bio- gas facilities in operation focusing on detecting indicators of fugitive emission that might cause the environmental benefit offset. Two main indicators would be: digester overpres- sure valves frequently open indicating that excess of biogas is being produced (fix: flaring the excess gas or even changing the AD recipe), and digestate storage with surface crust and bubbles (fix: solid-separation, storage cover and capture of gas).

Future research should focus on developing models to estimate CH4 emissions from digestate, since it was shown here that the VS content, usually used to predict emissions from untreated manure, does not play an important role in driving CH4 emissions from digestate. Also, it is important to develop strategies to reduce CH4 loss from digestate storage, such as storage covers and capture of residual CH4, and storage dimension and logistics to minimize stored volume in the warm months, especially in summer and fall. Microbial community characterization throughout the digestion process could also provide relevant information that might contribute to development of mitigation strategies.
